parts.content: (c) Using a demand-and-supply-of-currency diagram, explain how growing export revenues (paragraph 7) might affect the exchange rate of the Uzbek som.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/9e498371-6bee-4d0b-b44b-a387212b3d5a)

(c) Demand-and-supply-of-currency explanation  
• Growing export revenues raise the demand for Uzbek som (as foreign buyers convert their currency to purchase Uzbek exports).  
• The demand for som shifts right from D1 to D2, increasing its exchange rate from ER1 to ER2.  
• The supply of som may not change immediately, so the currency appreciates.  
• A stronger som reflects increased export receipts and can stabilize inflation by reducing import prices.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: (d) Using a production possibilities curve (PPC) diagram, explain how investments in infrastructure (paragraph 2) may impact Uzbekistan’s long-run potential output.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/7115e7ff-a196-456f-ac9b-1ba2c8e3df12)

(d) PPC explanation  
• Investment in infrastructure extends the economy’s productive capacity, shifting the PPC outward from PPC1 to PPC2.  
• With better roads, railways, and energy grids, efficiency improves, raising maximum potential output.  
• Over time, this leads to sustainable economic growth, as more goods and services can be produced.  
• This long-run growth can reduce unemployment and raise income levels.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: (e) Using a Lorenz curve diagram, explain the significance of the change in Uzbekistan’s Gini coefficient (Table 2).
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/970c2050-cf7e-4e05-b595-2fc9dab6eefd)

(e) Lorenz curve explanation  
• A falling Gini coefficient (from 0.32 to 0.30) indicates a more equal distribution of income.  
• On a Lorenz curve, the curve moves closer to the line of perfect equality, suggesting a reduction in income inequality.  
• An improvement from 0.32 to 0.30 is moderate but still points to progress in achieving more equitable income distribution.  
• This shift could partly be attributed to remittances and targeted government policies.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: (f) Using a poverty cycle diagram, explain how limited access to education and finance (paragraph 3) could trap rural households in persistent poverty.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/68f58d1d-ce43-474d-8ae9-8af7af5ffad5)

(f) Poverty cycle diagram explanation  
• Limited access to education and finance leads to low human capital and inadequate investment, resulting in low productivity and low incomes.  
• Low incomes reduce savings, hindering the funds available for investment in education, further trapping households in poverty.  
• This cycle continues as low productivity contributes to persistent underdevelopment in rural areas.  
• Government interventions in vocational training and microfinance may help break this cycle by boosting skills, income, and reinvestment.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: (g) Using information from the text/data and your knowledge of economics, evaluate the impact of Uzbekistan’s poverty-targeted social programs and agricultural reforms on long-term economic growth and development.
parts.markscheme:
(g) Evaluation of the impact of Uzbekistan’s poverty-targeted social programs and agricultural reforms on long-term economic growth and development

Definition  
• Economic growth: An increase in real GDP or real output over time.  
• Economic development: Sustained improvements in living standards, reduction in poverty, and enhanced access to basic services.

Arguments on social programs  
• Social programs aimed at reducing the poverty rate (from 11% to possibly 7.8% by 2025) can increase the productive capacity of the workforce through better health and education.  
• More inclusive growth reduces income inequality, supported by the Gini coefficient decline from 0.32 to 0.30.  

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/a1b225bf-b677-4da7-bec6-aa02d1144065)

• Greater social spending might strain government budgets, potentially increasing reliance on debt or tax revenues.  
• In rural areas, policies promoting vocational training and microfinance can improve employment prospects but may face logistical constraints (e.g., poor infrastructure, limited bank access).

Arguments on agricultural reforms  
• Reduced state procurement and movement toward market-based pricing can foster efficiency and innovation in agriculture.  
• Subsidies for fertilizer and irrigation technology raise yields and incomes, helping reduce rural poverty.  
• Environmental concerns over water scarcity and excessive cotton cultivation could undermine long-term sustainability.  
• Agricultural diversification might strengthen resilience, though reliance on the primary sector may still expose the economy to volatile commodity prices.

Linking to growth and development  
• Support for SMEs and farmers creates employment opportunities, expands tax revenue, and broadens the base for economic activity.  
• Improving infrastructure and market-based policies encourage FDI, fueling capital formation and technology transfers that enhance long-run growth and development.  
• Inflation remains a concern if increased spending is not matched by productivity gains; maintaining macroeconomic stability is crucial.  
• Remittances help boost household incomes, but dependence on external labor markets can limit domestic development. Over time, social initiatives and reforms could encourage more labor-intensive sectors to grow locally.

Weighing up  
• Social programs and agricultural reforms can stimulate both short-term and long-term growth by improving human capital and productivity.  
• Environmental challenges, funding constraints, and reliance on remittances remain key limitations.  
• Striking a balance between promoting equity and preserving fiscal stability is fundamental to sustaining economic progress.  
• If reforms continue alongside responsible macroeconomic management, Uzbekistan could achieve diversification, higher incomes, and improved human development indicators.

questions.specification:
## Economic Challenges and Policies in Israel

Israel is a high-income country in the Middle East, well-known for its advanced technology sector and entrepreneurial culture. Over the past decade, real GDP growth rates have averaged 3.5% per year, although 2022 saw an unusually high rate of 5.0% driven by surging exports of high-tech services. The Bank of Israel has generally maintained a relatively low interest rate, aiming to support domestic economic activity while keeping inflation near its 1–3% target range. However, inflation recently climbed to 3.6%, prompting discussions about tightening monetary policy.

Unemployment in 2022 stood at 4.2%, close to pre-pandemic levels. Nevertheless, structural unemployment persists among specific groups, including some low-skilled workers and marginalized communities. To address labor market disparities, the government funds vocational training programs to improve job prospects for these groups.

Israel faces significant social and regional inequalities. Despite its high GDP per capita, an estimated 16.5% of the population lives under the national poverty line. Moreover, certain regions in the south and north exhibit underdeveloped infrastructure and limited access to quality education. Critics argue that while the technology sector flourishes in major cities, too few resources are being channelled into bridging the urban-rural divide. Supporters of current policies highlight that government spending on social welfare programs has increased steadily, particularly in subsidized childcare and housing allowances.

International trade plays a pivotal role in Israel’s economy. The country is known for exporting pharmaceuticals, high-tech equipment, and software services. However, agricultural exports remain small, partly due to limited arable land. Israel’s main trading partners are the United States and the European Union, with exports driven by strong demand for medical devices and cybersecurity services. A major trade policy debate centers on removing tariffs on imported food products. Advocates argue that this would reduce living costs and ease inflationary pressures, whereas local farmers and food processors fear that cheaper imports could threaten their livelihoods.

Foreign direct investment (FDI) flows into Israel are concentrated in high-tech industries. While FDI accelerates technological advancement and job creation, some people worry about reliance on capital from large multinational firms, especially since profits often flow out of the country. In response, the government provides targeted tax incentives for local startups to encourage domestic innovation and economic diversification.

To tackle poverty and advance economic development, officials have introduced community-based initiatives designed to strengthen human capital. This includes the expansion of adult education programs and healthcare subsidies in low-income areas. Additionally, the government has signalled that it may raise the minimum wage to help those at the bottom of the income distribution. Opponents caution that these moves, if not carefully managed, could lead to price increases and reduced competitiveness in labour-intensive industries.

In the global sphere, Israel’s currency (the New Israeli Shekel, ILS) floats freely, although the Bank of Israel occasionally intervenes to curb excess volatility. Some economists argue that the shekel’s recent appreciation, fueled by robust export performance and FDI inflows, could undermine price competitiveness for traditional manufacturing and tourism services. Meanwhile, fiscal policy has focused on balancing progressive taxation and social spending. The national debt-to-GDP ratio, at 68.1%, remains manageable but higher than in previous years due to pandemic-related expenditures.

Looking ahead, policymakers face the delicate task of fostering economic growth, reducing poverty, and maintaining macroeconomic stability. Achieving these goals will require strategic infrastructure investments, ongoing skill development initiatives for underserved populations, and prudent macro policy coordination between the government and the Bank of Israel.

Below are two tables with data relevant to Israel’s economic situation.

**Table 1: Israel’s Selected Macroeconomic Indicators (2019–2022)**

| Indicator                      | 2019  | 2020  | 2021  | 2022  |
|--------------------------------|-------|-------|-------|-------|
| Real GDP Growth Rate (%)       | 3.4   | -2.6  | 4.3   | 5.0   |
| Inflation Rate (%)             | 0.8   | 0.6   | 1.2   | 3.6   |
| Unemployment Rate (%)          | 3.6   | 5.8   | 5.1   | 4.2   |
| Government Spending (% of GDP) | 28.0  | 31.2  | 30.5  | 29.7  |

**Table 2: Poverty and Development Indicators for Israel**

| Indicator                                | 2019  | 2020  | 2021  | 2022  |
|------------------------------------------|-------|-------|-------|-------|
| Population Living Below Poverty Line (%) | 17.0  | 18.2  | 17.3  | 16.5  |
| Gini Coefficient                         | 0.36  | 0.37  | 0.37  | 0.38  |
| FDI Inflows (US$ billion)               | 18.5  | 13.0  | 15.2  | 16.1  |
| Minimum Wage (ILS/month)                | 5300  | 5300  | 5400  | 5500  |

- Increase vocational training programs targeted at workers whose skills are outdated.  
- Provide subsidies or tax incentives for firms that offer retraining and upskilling.  
- Improve geographical mobility through better transport infrastructure and housing support.  
- Encourage new industries (especially outside major cities) to diversify job opportunities.

parts.content: Using information from the text/data and your knowledge of economics, evaluate the impact of Israel’s poverty-reduction policies on its economic growth and development.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include 

**Definition:**

- Economic Growth: An increase in a country’s real output (real GDP) over time.  
- Economic Development: Improvement in standards of living, reductions in poverty, and greater provision of social services and infrastructure.  
- Poverty Reduction Policies: Government strategies (e.g., raising minimum wage, social welfare spending, vocational training) aimed at reducing poverty and improving equality.

**Range of Arguments and Evaluation**

- Argument 1: Increased Minimum Wage and Social Welfare  
  - Economic Theory: A higher minimum wage can stimulate consumer spending, potentially increasing Aggregate Demand, causing a rightward shift of the curve from AD1 to AD2, and promoting short-run growth. Greater welfare spending can reduce inequality by raising the disposable income of the poorest households.  
  - Data Reference: The minimum wage rose from ILS 5300 to ILS 5500 between 2019 and 2022, alongside an increase in social spending from 28.0% of GDP to 29.7% by 2022.  
   Evaluation: While these policies may help lift people out of poverty (reducing the poverty rate from 17.0% in 2019 to 16.5% in 2022), critics caution that higher labor costs may reduce business profitability and competitiveness. In labor-intensive industries, employers might cut jobs or turn to automation, mitigating gains in employment among low-skilled workers.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/e7f82962-319d-49c9-8aff-85c4321c9a6a)

- Argument 2: Vocational Training and Human Capital Formation  
  - Economic Theory: Improving educational opportunities and workforce skills raises labor productivity, shifting the PPC outward. In the long run, better skills help individuals find employment in higher-paying sectors, further driving economic growth and development.  
  - Data Reference: Vocational and adult education programs have targeted minority groups, helping to address structural unemployment (4.2% in 2022).  
  - Evaluation: These measures may be very effective in reducing structural unemployment and boosting the technology sector’s competitiveness. However, implementation requires consistent funding, and progress can be slow. In the short run, fiscal constraints might limit the scale of these programs.

- Argument 3: Healthcare Subsidies  
  - Economic Theory: Improved health outcomes increase labor productivity by reducing absenteeism and raising worker efficiency, contributing to higher potential output and development.  
  - Data Connection: The government expanded healthcare subsidies in low-income areas, helping reduce poverty but adding to government expenditures.  
  - Evaluation: Better health can enhance economic development by increasing labor supply quality. However, higher public spending can raise the debt-to-GDP ratio (68.1% in 2022). If debt financing becomes unsustainable, there is a risk of crowding out other essential investments.

- Argument 4: Regional Inequality Considerations  
  - Economic Theory: Persistent poverty and underinvestment in rural or marginalized regions may hinder long-term development, preventing an economy from reaching its full productive potential. Income inequality, indicated by a Gini coefficient of 0.38 in 2022, can slow growth by limiting the capacity of a segment of the population to contribute to output.  
  - Data Reference: Paragraphs 3 and 4 highlight underdeveloped infrastructure in the north and south, suggesting that social spending might not be sufficient to address rural underinvestment.  
  - Evaluation: Targeted infrastructure and educational investments can bring down poverty rates further; however, bridging the urban-rural divide requires substantial public and private partnerships. Failure to address these disparities could perpetuate pockets of poverty despite overall growth.

-  Argument 5: Balancing Fiscal Responsibility and Development Goals  
  - Economic Theory: Expansionary fiscal measures that boost welfare and human capital can raise both AD and LRAS. However, if financed via debt, rising costs might put upward pressure on interest rates, incentivizing cuts in other services.  
  - Data Reference: The national debt-to-GDP ratio has increased to 68.1%.  
  - Evaluation: A moderate debt level can be managed if growth outpaces interest obligations. Over-reliance on external financing, however, could undermine the long-term sustainability of anti-poverty programs, especially if economic growth slows.

**Conclusion:**  

- Israel’s poverty-reduction policies—ranging from raising the minimum wage to investing in training and healthcare—have the potential to stimulate both economic growth and development outcomes if effectively targeted and sustained.  
- Short-term gains include higher aggregate demand and marginal reductions in poverty. In the long run, improved human capital and infrastructure can push out the country’s productive capacity.  
- However, policymakers must address risks such as rising public debt, structural unemployment among marginalized groups, and possible losses in competitiveness for labor-intensive industries. Carefully balancing these factors could ensure that growth becomes more inclusive, creating a virtuous cycle of development and sustained economic expansion.

questions.specification:
## Japan’s Economic Transition and Challenges

Japan, located in East Asia, is the world’s third-largest economy by nominal GDP. Despite its long-standing reputation for technological innovation and efficient manufacturing processes, Japan faces persistent challenges tied to demographic shifts, high public debt, and intensified global competition. According to government estimates, Japan’s population declined from about 127 million in 2012 to around 125 million in 2022, placing upward pressure on social security spending. As of 2022, public debt stood at approximately 266% of GDP, reflecting decades of expansionary fiscal measures aimed at stimulating domestic demand.

The Bank of Japan (BOJ) has historically maintained an accommodative monetary policy to address deflationary pressures. Between 2019 and 2021, inflation hovered close to zero. However, in 2022, partly due to rising global energy prices, consumer price inflation rose to 2.5%. Although this figure remains modest by international standards, any uptick in inflation poses questions about the sustainability of ultra-low interest rates, especially given Japan’s massive debt burden. Meanwhile, real GDP growth, which contracted sharply in 2020 amidst the COVID-19 pandemic (–4.5%), recovered to 2.0% by 2022, helped by a revival in exports and strengthening private consumption.

Microeconomic concerns have emerged over labor market rigidity and low productivity growth, particularly in the service sector. Labor shortages in sectors such as health-care and agriculture have become more pronounced as the population ages. While the government has implemented programs to encourage automation and foreign labor inflows, some industries remain challenged by outdated regulations. Additionally, small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) struggle with limited access to capital, restricting their ability to invest in new technologies that enhance productivity. Analysts also note that structural unemployment has risen in specific regions.

International trade remains crucial to Japan’s economic health, with exports of automobiles, electronics, and machine tools playing a central role. In 2022, exports reached US 700 billion (up from US 640 billion in 2020), reflecting partial recovery from pandemic-related disruptions. Imports also surged to US 710 billion, driven by higher energy costs and a weaker yen. While Japan continues to record a modest current account surplus, it has narrowed in recent years to 2.7% of GDP in 2022, down from 3.4% in 2019. Amid ongoing global uncertainty, the government promotes green investment to modernize industries and reduce dependence on fossil fuels, allocating around US$70 billion to renewable energy projects and clean technologies in 2022.

Environmental concerns have prompted policymakers to re-evaluate the nation’s energy mix. Fossil fuels still make up a large portion of electricity generation, although CO2 emissions declined slightly to 8.4 metric tons per capita in 2022 from 9.0 in 2019. Public-private partnerships in green technology such as hydrogen fuel cells and electric vehicle infrastructure aim to drive innovation, reduce external costs, and create export opportunities in emerging international markets. Critics argue, however, that high government spending on green initiatives further exacerbates Japan’s debt issues and raises questions about the efficiency of subsidies.

Future prospects hinge on policy coordination among various stakeholders-government agencies, the BOJ, and private enterprises. Proposals for market-based supply-side policies include labor market liberalization, corporate tax incentives for research and development, and structural reforms to foster competition in traditionally protected sectors. Yet implementation is complex, as political pressures often favor preserving entrenched interests, especially in areas like agriculture. The success of these reforms may determine whether Japan can transition to a more dynamic, inclusive economy without undermining fiscal sustainability.

**TABLE 1: Japan’s Selected Macroeconomic Indicators (2019–2022)**

| Indicator                | 2019  | 2020   | 2021  | 2022  |
|--------------------------|-------|--------|-------|-------|
| Nominal GDP (US$ bn)     | 5050  | 4950   | 5000  | 5100  |
| Real GDP Growth Rate (%) | 0.7   | –4.5   | 1.6   | 2.0   |
| Inflation Rate (%)       | 0.5   | –0.2   | 0.5   | 2.5   |
| Unemployment Rate (%)    | 2.4   | 2.8    | 2.9   | 2.6   |
| Public Debt (% of GDP)   | 236   | 248    | 257   | 266   |

**TABLE 2: Japan’s Trade and Environmental Indicators**

| Indicator                              | 2019 | 2020 | 2021 | 2022 |
|----------------------------------------|------|------|------|------|
| Exports (US$ bn)                       | 720  | 640  | 680  | 700  |
| Imports (US$ bn)                       | 670  | 610  | 650  | 710  |
| Current Account Balance (% of GDP)     | 3.4  | 3.0  | 3.1  | 2.7  |
| CO2 Emissions (metric tons per capita) | 9.0  | 8.7  | 8.6  | 8.4  |
| Green Investment (US$ bn)              | 30   | 42   | 55   | 70   |

parts.content: Define the term structural unemployment mentioned in the text (Paragraph 3).
parts.markscheme: Structural unemployment happens when there is a mismatch between workers’ skills and available jobs.

parts.content: Define the term labor market rigidity mentioned in the text (Paragraph 3).
parts.markscheme: Labor market rigidity refers to the factors that prevent wages from adjusting or hinder workers from moving freely between jobs, causing inflexibility in employment contracts, wage setting, or workforce mobility.

parts.content: Using a labour market diagram, explain how the aging population and labor market rigidities described in the text could lead to structural unemployment.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/25f8c066-95a8-4144-9dc9-a8399ead373e)

- Using a labour market diagram, explain how the aging population and labor market rigidities described in the text could lead to structural unemployment.  
- Labor market rigidities (such as strict contracts or limited mobility) can reduce the ability of firms to adjust wages or attract new workers from different regions or sectors. 
- An aging population decreases the pool of workers in rapidly expanding industries like health-care, but it also raises the mismatch between existing worker skills and emerging job requirements. 
- When wages cannot adjust downwards or when older workers do not have the required skills for new positions, structural unemployment increases. 
- On a labour market diagram, the equilibrium wage may remain above the market-clearing level, creating a surplus of labor for some jobs and unfilled vacancies in others.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using a business cycle diagram, explain how Japan’s real GDP growth path from 2019 to 2022 (see Table 1) illustrates the recovery phase after an economic downturn.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/75545d80-908f-4282-a9ca-4b8fe6cf21c6)

- Using a business cycle diagram, explain how Japan’s real GDP growth path from 2019 to 2022 (see Table 1) illustrates the recovery phase after an economic downturn.  
- Japan’s real GDP growth dipped sharply from 0.7% in 2019 to –4.5% in 2020, indicating a recession. From 2021 onward, growth rates of 1.6% and 2.0% show that the economy moved into a recovery phase on the business cycle diagram, gradually closing the recessionary gap.
-As aggregate demand picked up (partly due to revived exports and private consumption), there was a move back toward the long-term trend level of output, signaling improvement following the downturn.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using an externalities diagram (marginal private cost and marginal social cost), explain the rationale for the Japanese government’s green investment to reduce CO2 emissions.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/15a985d6-8dc3-4aa4-aaf7-143ab035d7a1)

- Using an externalities diagram (marginal private cost and marginal social cost), explain the rationale for the Japanese government’s green investment to reduce CO2 emissions.  
CO2 emissions generate negative externalities because marginal social cost (MSC) is higher than marginal private cost (MPC). (MSC>MPC)
- Without intervention, the market overallocates resources to polluting energy production (Qm > Qs), where Qs represents the socially efficient output). 
- Government green investment attempts to shift energy production toward cleaner technologies, internalizing the externality by lowering MSC and reducing the gap between MPC and MSC. 
- This decreases overall CO2 emissions and moves production closer to the socially optimal level.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using an exchange rate diagram, explain how rising imports (refer to Table 2) could affect the exchange rate of the Japanese yen.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/6461220d-9359-41b1-8fed-e640eb5fc51e)

- Higher imports (from US 610 bn in 2020 to US 710 bn in 2022) increase demand for foreign currency, meaning more yen is supplied on foreign exchange markets to buy foreign goods.
- In an exchange rate diagram, this is shown by a rightward shift (D1 to D2) in the supply of yen, possibly leading to a depreciation of the yen if other factors remain the same. 
- A weaker yen can, in turn, heighten import costs further, affecting the current account balance.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using information from the text/data (particularly Table 2) and your knowledge of economics, evaluate the potential impact of Japan’s increasing green investment on its economic growth and external balance.
parts.markscheme:
**Definition:**

- Green Investment: Government or private sector spending on technologies and projects that reduce negative externalities and promote environmental sustainability.  
- External Balance: The equilibrium in a country’s balance of payments, including current account performance (exports, imports, and net investment flows).

**Range of Arguments:**

1. Positive Growth Impacts  
- Increased spending of US$70 bn in 2022 on renewable energy and clean technologies may stimulate aggregate demand (AD) through higher investment.  
- New green technologies can improve productivity (especially if SMEs gain greater access to capital), thereby shifting long-run aggregate supply (LRAS1 to LRAS2) to the right.  
- Export opportunities in emerging markets for hydrogen fuel cells or electric vehicle infrastructure can increase net exports, supporting real GDP growth (which can impact also AD (AD1 to AD2)).

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/8e31d248-94d4-4478-8db8-a1b69e9152f7)

2. Higher Public Debt and Opportunity Cost  
- Rising debt (266% of GDP in 2022) might worsen if additional funds are borrowed for green projects.  
- The government faces opportunity costs resources allocated to green investment might otherwise address pension liabilities in an aging society or other pressing needs.  
- High debt servicing costs can limit future spending on productive infrastructure if interest rates rise.

3. Potential Improvement in External Balance  
- Exports of advanced green technologies can widen Japan’s technological edge, contributing positively to export revenues.  
- If reliance on imported fossil fuels diminishes over time, the growth of imports could slow, aiding the current account balance.  
- A shift to domestic renewable resources could reduce the import of costly energy inputs, helping to stabilize the current account.

4. Uncertainties and Short-Term Challenges  
- There might be time lags before green technologies become profitable export products, temporarily pressuring the current account if imports of equipment or raw materials for green projects increase.  
- Critics argue that if subsidies are inefficient or if industries resist structural reforms, the intended gains in productivity and export competitiveness may not materialize fully.

**Conclusion:**

- Japan’s increasing green investment can potentially yield long-term gains in economic growth through higher productivity, expanded export opportunities, and reduced imports of fossil fuels. However, short-term pressures on the public debt and the possibility of inefficient subsidy allocation must be considered. 
- The impact on external balance is likely to be positive in the long run if green technologies boost exports and reduce energy imports, though immediate results depend on policy effectiveness and international demand for green innovations.
- The overall outcome rests on whether Japan can manage its growing debt and implement structural reforms to optimize the green transition, thereby balancing the benefits of environmental sustainability with fiscal responsibility.

questions.specification:
## Afghanistan’s Struggles with Inclusive Development

Afghanistan is a landlocked country in South Asia, characterized by rugged terrain and a predominantly agricultural economy. Persistent challenges including conflict, limited infrastructure, and low private-sector development have kept real GDP relatively stagnant over the past few years. In 2021, real GDP growth was estimated at –2.5%, rebounding slightly to 1.5% in 2022. During the same period, inflation accelerated from 8% to 12%, driven primarily by higher food and fuel prices. Many Afghan households rely on subsistence farming, and with limited access to capital and technology, they often find themselves in what economists describe as a “poverty trap,” where low incomes lead to low savings, underinvestment in human capital, and perpetuation of poverty.

The government has struggled to collect sufficient tax revenue, resulting in a budget deficit of 6% of GDP in 2022. International aid remains a critical source of funds, but donor contributions have fluctuated, creating uncertainty about long-term public investment. Agriculture accounts for over 40% of total employment, though exports remain narrow in scope primarily dried fruits and raw materials reflecting minimal specialization in value-added goods. 

Foreign aid inflows have historically financed public services such as education, health-care, and basic infrastructure. However, a considerable portion of the population, an estimated 47%, still lives below the national poverty line. Rural households in particular face obstacles in accessing quality education and health-care, hindering improvements in labor productivity. Women’s labor force participation remains very low, limiting the economy’s productive capacity and reducing the potential for a more diversified export base. 

Recognizing the need to modernize agriculture and stimulate industrial growth, the government has partnered with international organizations to construct roads and irrigation systems. Supporters of these efforts argue that better infrastructure can reduce costs and attract foreign direct investment (FDI). Critics worry about the sustainability of foreign-funded projects and the extent to which they promote inclusive development, especially for smallholder farmers. 

To address persistent unemployment, which reached 23% in 2022, some policymakers advocate for targeted public spending to stimulate demand, coupled with initiatives to enhance value-added agricultural processing. Meanwhile, the Afghan central bank has attempted to stabilize the exchange rate of the Afghani by managing its foreign currency reserves, amid pressure from fluctuating exports and reduced foreign aid. 

Despite myriad challenges, there are signs of resilience. Pocket industries, such as saffron cultivation and artisanal crafts, have gained international recognition, hinting at meaningful possibilities for greater specialization. Nonetheless, structural barriers remain: low human capital, weak governance, and limited access to finance impede the capacity of domestic firms to expand. 

Over the long run, many economists agree that a combination of improved security, stable institutions, and carefully sequenced reforms can help break the cycle of poverty. Enhanced education, particularly for women, could raise productivity and incomes, creating a virtuous cycle that lifts living standards. Ultimately, the trajectory of Afghanistan’s economy will depend on the success of these reforms, the magnitude and stability of international support, and the country’s ability to create inclusive growth that benefits all segments of society.

**Table 1: Selected Macroeconomic Indicators for Afghanistan** 

| Indicator                          | 2021  | 2022  |
|------------------------------------|-------|-------|
| Real GDP (US$ billions)            | 19.5  | 19.8  |
| Nominal GDP (US$ billions)         | 20.3  | 21.5  |
| Real GDP Growth Rate (%)           | -2.5  | 1.5   |
| Inflation Rate (%)                 | 8.0   | 12.0  |
| Unemployment Rate (%)              | 22.5  | 23.0  |
| Budget Deficit (% of GDP)          | 5.0   | 6.0   |

**Table 2: Development and Poverty Indicators for Afghanistan ** 

| Indicator                                 | 2021      | 2022      |
|-------------------------------------------|-----------|-----------|
| Poverty Rate (% below national line)      | 46.5      | 47.0      |
| Literacy Rate (%)                         | 38.0      | 38.2      |
| Foreign Aid Inflows (US$ billions)        | 3.0       | 2.4       |
| Life Expectancy (years)                   | 64        | 64        |
| Female Labour Force Participation Rate (%)| 18.0      | 17.5      |
| Gini Coefficient                          | 0.38      | 0.39      |

parts.content: Define the term “poverty trap” as indicated in the text (paragraph 1).
parts.markscheme: A poverty trap is a self-reinforcing mechanism where low incomes lead to insufficient savings and investment in human or physical capital, trapping households or nations in persistent poverty.

parts.content: Define the term “specialization” mentioned in the text (paragraph 3).
parts.markscheme: Specialization is the concentration of a firm, region, or country on producing a limited range of goods or services in order to achieve greater efficiency and productivity.

parts.content: Using information from Table 1, calculate the difference (in US$ billions) between Afghanistan’s nominal GDP in 2021 and 2022.
parts.markscheme:
Difference in nominal GDP (2021 vs 2022)  
- From Table 1: Nominal GDP in 2021 is US 20.3 billion; in 2022, it is US 21.5 billion.  
- Difference: US 21.5 billion – US 20.3 billion = US 1.2 billion.

parts.content: Using a poverty cycle diagram, explain how limited access to education in Afghanistan may perpetuate poverty.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/0f5489f2-57fd-4250-969a-1501ec6db1f5)

-  Low levels of education reduce human capital, leading to low productivity and reduced incomes. 
-  Low incomes limit households’ ability to invest in education (school fees, opportunity costs of not working).  
- This perpetuates a cycle where the next generation also remains unskilled and earns low incomes.  
- The lack of quality education, especially in rural areas and for women, reinforces a cycle of low savings, underinvestment, and continued poverty.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using an AD/AS diagram, explain how increased government spending on infrastructure might affect Afghanistan’s unemployment rate in the short run.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/150e6505-66bf-4188-a4d0-fa9ad4475af4)

- Government infrastructure spending raises aggregate demand (AD) through higher public investment, shifting AD to the right (AD1 to AD2). 
- As AD increases, firms produce more to meet demand, requiring more labor and reducing unemployment in the short run.  
- The resulting increase in real output (from Y1 to Y2) corresponds to a fall in cyclical unemployment.  
- However, the eventual impact on unemployment depends on the responsiveness of aggregate supply, the availability of skills, and potential crowding out.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using a demand-and-supply-of-currency diagram, explain how changes in exports could influence the value of the Afghani (Afghanistan’s currency).
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/c989a57d-524a-484d-a3cc-15a347834b7e)

- An increase in exports raises foreign buyers’ demand for the Afghani, shifting the demand curve for the currency to the right (D1 to D2).  
- This puts upward pressure on the exchange rate, strengthening the Afghani if supply remains stable.  
- In contrast, falling exports would shift the demand curve leftward and depreciate the Afghani.  
- Changes in export volumes thus affect currency value through shifts in demand for the currency.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using a Lorenz curve diagram, explain how the slight increase in the Gini coefficient from 0.38 to 0.39 (Table 2) reflects changes in income distribution.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/820fb807-cabd-4712-b0d4-4d7efe95fca6)

- A Lorenz curve illustrates the degree of income inequality by showing the cumulative share of income earned by cumulative share of the population.  
- A shift from a Gini coefficient of 0.38 to 0.39 suggests a slightly more unequal distribution, moving the Lorenz curve farther from the line of perfect equality.  
- This indicates that a larger proportion of total income is held by higher-income groups.  
- Inequality may have increased due to factors such as unequal access to resources, limited savings/investment, or job market disparities.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using information from the text/data and your knowledge of economics, evaluate the impact of foreign aid inflows on Afghanistan’s long-term development prospects.
parts.markscheme:
**Definition:**

- Foreign aid: Transfer of funds or resources from one country/organization to another to promote economic development or welfare.  
- Economic development: A broad measure of well-being involving increases in income, improvements in standards of living, reductions in poverty, and various social indicators.

**Range of Arguments:**

- Arguments for positive impacts of foreign aid  
	- Stimulus for critical infrastructure and public services  
	- Foreign aid helps finance schools, hospitals, and roads.  
	-  Better infrastructure reduces production costs, making exports more competitive.  
	-  References the government’s partnership with international organizations for roads and irrigation (case study, paragraph 5).  

- Possible multiplier effects on real GDP and employment  
  -  Injection of funds can increase aggregate demand and create jobs.  
  -  Table 1 shows high unemployment (23% in 2022); additional aid could lower this if invested effectively.  

- Support for human capital formation  
  - Aid directed toward education can raise literacy (38.2% in 2022) and labor productivity.  
  - Higher skills and productivity reduce the persistence of the poverty trap (case study, paragraph 1).  

- Assistance in boosting specialized industries  
  - Emerging pocket industries (e.g., saffron, artisanal crafts) benefit from external support.  
  - Foreign aid can develop more value-added exports to diversify the economy.  

- Arguments for limitations of foreign aid  
	- Aid dependency and volatility of donor contributions  
  - Fluctuating aid inflows (US 3.0 to US 2.4 billion from Table 2) create reliance and uncertainty.  
  - Unpredictable external funds may disrupt long-term planning and sustainable growth.  

- Possible misallocation or corruption risks  
  - Weak governance can limit effective use of aid, reducing development impact.  
  - Budget deficits (6% of GDP) combined with potentially poor revenue collection can compound inefficiencies.  

- Limited absorptive capacity and sustainability concerns  
  - Large inflows may overwhelm domestic institutions, reducing effectiveness of aid-funded projects.  
  - Maintaining infrastructure, hospitals, and schools requires local capacity that may not be fully developed.  

- Crowding out of local initiatives  
  - Overdependence on foreign services may suppress private-sector growth.  
  - Domestic entrepreneurs may be discouraged if aid programs dominate certain sectors.

**Conclusion:**

- Foreign aid has the potential to kickstart infrastructure, enhance education, and improve healthcare: fundamental prerequisites to break the poverty cycle.  
- Long-term development also depends on policy reforms (e.g., stable institutions, improvements in governance, gender inclusion).  
- Aid effectiveness is highest when coordinated with local needs and accompanied by capacity-building to reduce reliance on external funds.  
- The case study suggests that combining foreign aid with better security, more inclusive governance, and targeted human capital investment can yield sustainable development gains.  
- Overall, foreign aid can be a catalyst, but it must address structural barriers, avoid dependency, and align with domestic reforms for lasting improvements in living standards.

questions.specification:
## Nigeria-Morocco Trade Negotiations

Text A — Background and Economic Overview  

Nigeria, located in West Africa, is one of the continent’s largest economies with an estimated GDP of US$450 billion in 2022. It has a population of over 210 million people, and between 2018 and 2022, the average inflation rate rose from 9.3% to 16.9%. The government has responded by tightening monetary policy and increasing spending on infrastructure to stimulate employment and reduce poverty. However, concerns remain about the country’s over-reliance on crude oil exports, which account for more than 85% of export revenue.  

Morocco, situated in North Africa, has a more diversified economy, driven by agriculture, phosphates, and tourism. In 2022, Morocco’s GDP stood at approximately US$130 billion, with an annual inflation rate increasing from 1.9% in 2018 to 4.1% in 2022. The country has invested in renewable energy projects to reduce carbon emissions and has also sought to expand its industrial base, particularly in automobile manufacturing.  

Text B — The Trade Negotiations  

Discussions between Nigeria and Morocco gained momentum in 2021, focusing on developing a new free trade agreement (FTA). Both governments aim to remove or significantly lower tariffs on key exports, foster foreign direct investment (FDI), and align the agreement with Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). Nigeria, holding an absolute advantage, seeks to reduce tariffs on its crude oil, natural gas, and agricultural goods, while Morocco looks to expand exports of phosphate fertilizers, textiles, and automotive components.  

Negotiators also emphasize cooperation in energy, including the possibility of jointly developing a pipeline to carry Nigerian natural gas to Morocco’s Atlantic coast. Leaders from both countries hope this infrastructure project will attract global investors, boost regional trade, and enhance energy security in North and West Africa. Additionally, they intend to facilitate technology transfers and investment in Nigeria’s downstream refining sector, mitigating the region’s vulnerability to volatile oil prices.  

Text C — Impact on Local Industries and SDGs  

Several domestic industries in both countries have expressed concerns about increased foreign competition. In Nigeria, local manufacturing firms fear that cheaper imports from Morocco could diminish their market share. Meanwhile, Moroccan phosphate producers worry about potential drop-offs in demand if the global market shifts toward renewables and reduces fertilizer consumption. Stakeholders argue that, to remain competitive, companies must innovate and improve production efficiencies.  

Both Nigeria and Morocco see the FTA as an opportunity to address issues aligned with the SDGs. Officials highlight SDG 7 (Affordable and Clean Energy), SDG 8 (Decent Work and Economic Growth), and SDG 9 (Industry, Innovation and Infrastructure) as key areas of focus. In Morocco, solar and wind power already account for over 36% of energy generation, and Nigeria aspires to expand its renewable capacity from the current 2% of the energy mix to 10% by 2030. Policymakers believe that increased trade and shared technological expertise could hasten the transition to cleaner energy.  

Trade liberalization could contribute to higher economic growth, but it also raises questions about equity. Nigeria’s rural populations depend heavily on agriculture, and the removal of tariffs might expose local farmers to intense foreign competition. In Morocco, labor unions have expressed concerns that cheaper agricultural imports from Nigeria could increase unemployment in the country’s farming regions. Both governments plan to continue negotiating social safeguards, vocational training programs, and targeted subsidies to protect vulnerable groups.

**Table 1: Selected Macroeconomic Indicators, 2022**
| Indicator                           | Nigeria | Morocco |
| ----------------------------------- | ------- | ------- |
| Nominal GDP (US\$ billions)         | 450     | 130     |
| Inflation Rate (%)                  | 16.9    | 4.1     |
| Unemployment Rate (%)               | 14.2    | 10.5    |
| Currency Exchange Rate (local/US\$) | 415.0   | 10.0    |


**Table 2: Proposed Tariff Reductions and FDI Projections**  

| Sector                | Proposed Tariff Reduction (%) | Projected FDI Inflows (US\$ millions) |
| --------------------- | ----------------------------- | ------------------------------------- |
| Crude Oil (Nigeria)   | 10 → 0                        | 450 → 600                             |
| Phosphate Fertilizers | 12 → 3                        | 270 → 350                             |
| Textiles              | 15 → 5                        | 190 → 250                             |
| Automotive Components | 20 → 8                        | 300 → 400                             |

parts.content: Using information from Table 1, calculate the approximate difference in nominal GDP per capita between Nigeria and Morocco.
parts.markscheme:
- Nigeria’s nominal GDP = US$450 billion; 
- Morocco’s nominal GDP = US$130 billion.  
- Nigeria’s population = ~210 million; Morocco’s population = ~37 million (assuming ~37 million to reflect typical approximate population data for Morocco).  
- Nigeria’s GDP per capita ≈ 450 billion ÷ 210 million = US$2,143.  
- Morocco’s GDP per capita ≈ 130 billion ÷ 37 million = US$3,514.  
- Approximate difference ≈ US\$3,514- US\$2,143 = US\$1,371

parts.content: Using an AD/AS diagram, explain how Morocco’s investment in renewable energy (Text C, paragraph 6) could impact its long-run economic growth.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/6e9501f8-0038-42c6-b8b1-4fec6515997c)

- The investment in renewable energy increases Morocco’s productive capacity and shifts its long-run aggregate supply (LRAS) curve to the right as capital and technology improve.  
- In the short run, an increase in investment spending shifts aggregate demand (AD) to the right, boosting real output. In the long run, improved infrastructure and technology raise productive efficiency.  
- The result is a higher potential output (rightward shift of LRAS), leading to long-run economic growth.  
- This can be shown on an AD/AS diagram where AD shifts right (from AD1 to AD2) and LRAS shifts right (from LRAS1 to LRAS2), increasing real GDP in the long term.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 
<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using an exchange rate diagram, explain the likely effect on Nigeria’s currency if foreign demand for Nigerian crude oil significantly increases (Text C, paragraph 5).

- An increase in foreign demand for Nigerian crude oil raises export revenue and leads to higher demand for Nigeria’s currency in order to purchase Nigerian oil.  
- On a currency market diagram for the naira (Nigerian currency), the demand curve shifts right, causing an appreciation of the currency.  
- An appreciated currency makes imports cheaper for Nigerian consumers and might reduce the competitiveness of other exports (due to a higher exchange rate).  
- However, it may also help lower inflationary pressures by making imported goods less expensive.

parts.content: Using an externalities diagram, explain how increased adoption of renewable energy could address negative externalities associated with fossil fuels in Nigeria (Text C, paragraph 6).
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/c027463c-2a8c-482f-b365-ff885c3a3015)

- Fossil fuels generate negative externalities, meaning the social cost (MSC) exceeds the private cost (MPC).  
- By increasing renewable energy, the quantity of fossil fuel use may fall, reducing the divergence between private and social costs and decreasing welfare loss.  
- The externalities diagram would show a shift in demand away from fossil fuels or a shift in supply (if considering a reduced external cost), moving closer to the socially optimal output where MSC = MSB.  
- This helps address environmental concerns (fewer carbon emissions) and promotes more sustainable energy consumption.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 
<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using information from the text/data (including Table 2) and your knowledge of economics, discuss the potential impact of the proposed Nigeria–Morocco trade agreement on both countries meeting two sustainable development goals.
parts.markscheme:
**Definition:**

- Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs): International objectives aimed at achieving sustainable economic growth, social inclusion, and environmental conservation.  
- Trade agreement: The removal or reduction of trade barriers to facilitate increased exchange of goods, services, and investment flows.

**Range of Arguments:**

- Impact on SDG 7 (Affordable and Clean Energy)  
  - The agreement includes potential joint development of infrastructure projects such as the natural gas pipeline (Text B, paragraph 4). This may improve energy access in both regions, making energy more affordable.  
  - Shared technological expertise in renewables (Text C, paragraph 6) accelerates the transition to cleaner energy, reducing emissions and diversifying the energy mix.  
  - Nigeria’s target to increase renewables from 2% to 10% by 2030 might be more achievable if technology and investment flows from Morocco are realized.  
  - Potential limitation: High implementation costs and dependence on global energy markets might restrict short-term gains in affordable energy.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/610a5c41-f44c-433e-bdd1-450c4d498bcd)

- Impact on SDG 8 (Decent Work and Economic Growth)  
  - Removal of tariffs and trade barriers (Table 2) encourages greater FDI, potentially creating jobs in both nations. For instance, automotive components FDI in Morocco and new refining investments in Nigeria (Text C, paragraph 5, Table 2) could expand employment.  
  - Trade liberalization may increase GDP growth through specialisation; Nigeria exporting oil and agricultural products, Morocco exporting phosphates and automotive parts.  
  - Limitations arise from possible displacement of workers in vulnerable industries. Nigerian manufacturing or Moroccan agriculture may face pressure if they cannot compete. Social safeguards and vocational training (Text C, paragraph 7) become crucial to prevent unemployment.

- Impact on SDG 9 (Industry, Innovation and Infrastructure)  
  - The pipeline project (Text B, paragraph 4) enhances regional infrastructure, incentivizes industrial expansion.  
  - Investment in downstream refining in Nigeria fosters technology transfer and diversifies industrial base, reducing over-reliance on crude oil.  
  - Constraints emerge from large initial investment costs and potential policy uncertainties that may slow innovation.

- Positive Effects  
  - Table 2 shows projected FDI inflows (e.g., crude oil US 600 million, phosphate US 350 million), supporting industrialization and potential job creation.  
  - Environmental aspects are partly addressed by technology sharing in renewable energy, fulfilling green infrastructure investments.  
  - Larger market size through reduced tariffs might stimulate private sector growth, raising incomes and contributing to poverty reduction.

- Negative Effects  
  - Inequality might rise if rural farmers in Nigeria and small-scale producers in Morocco cannot match foreign efficiencies. This could jeopardize inclusive development goals.  
  - There may be a risk of reduced tariff revenues, potentially constraining government funds for public services that support SDGs.  
  - Volatile oil prices threaten stability, and dependence on single export commodities can hamper sustainable growth.

**Conclusion:**

- The trade agreement can promote SDG 7 and SDG 8 (and possibly SDG 9) by fostering cleaner energy, expanding employment, and enhancing industrial infrastructure.  
- However, challenges include uneven beneficiary distribution (potentially harming some local industries and rural populations), uncertain commodity prices, and high transitional costs.  
- Policymakers would need to address these issues through targeted subsidies, training programs, and long-term diversification measures to ensure the agreement significantly contributes to sustainable development in both countries.

questions.specification:
## Economic and Social Developments in Turkey

Turkey, uniquely positioned at the crossroads of Europe and Asia, has long been an emerging economic power in its region. Over the past decade, its real Gross Domestic Product (GDP) growth rate has fluctuated between 2% and 7% annually, supported primarily by strong exports in textiles, agricultural products, and tourism. However, recent years have seen growing macroeconomic challenges, including double-digit inflation averaging around 15% in 2021—as well as currency volatility stemming from shifts in monetary policy and changes in investor confidence.

The Turkish lira has experienced several periods of sharp depreciation. In 2022 alone, the lira depreciated by nearly 20% against the US dollar, making imports more expensive and contributing to higher consumer prices. The government responded with various measures, such as lowering certain interest rates to stimulate domestic demand and introducing temporary subsidies on electricity and staple foods. Nonetheless, authorities face criticism that these policies might fuel further inflation if not managed carefully.

Beyond inflation and currency issues, Turkey has been grappling with income inequality and persistent poverty in some regions, especially in the eastern and southeastern provinces. Roughly 9% of the population lives below the national poverty line, exacerbated by limited access to high-quality education, underdeveloped infrastructure, and high youth unemployment rates. The government has launched programs aimed at providing microloans to low-income families, increasing access to education, and improving rural health-care facilities. Meanwhile, some development economists warn that without sustained investment in human capital and targeted social spending, vulnerable households can become trapped in a cycle of poverty that is difficult to escape.

Foreign direct investment (FDI) inflows have been inconsistent, reflecting both global economic uncertainties and domestic policy shifts. While some international firms remain attracted by Turkey’s strategic location and large consumer market, others are deterred by regulatory barriers and concerns over macroeconomic instability. Turkey has also sought to expand its export markets through regional trade agreements, especially with the European Union, which remains a key partner. Exports of machinery and automobile parts have increased, while agricultural producers benefit from vibrant demand in neighboring regions.

In parallel, Turkey continues to pursue major infrastructure projects, such as new highways and energy pipelines, to enhance connectivity and attract more investment. Critics, however, point to rising external debt, which now stands at around 54% of GDP, as a potential risk. Nonetheless, proponents argue that modernized transport corridors, airports, and port facilities can stimulate economic growth by reducing logistical costs and encouraging tourism.

Addressing social and economic development remains a central focus. The government’s Vision 2025 agenda includes goals to reduce poverty rates to below 5% and increase female labor force participation. Plans are underway to reform the education system, particularly in rural areas, to develop skilled labor that can compete in global markets. While social protection budgets have increased, concerns persist over the efficiency of these programs. Some analysts advocate for targeted subsidies rather than broad-based energy subsidies, noting that wealthier households often benefit disproportionately from general subsidies on electricity or fuel.

With one of the largest youth populations in the region, Turkey faces an opportunity for a demographic dividend if education and labor market reforms are effectively implemented. At the same time, the government must manage inflation, maintain currency stability, and encourage long-term foreign investment. The future trajectory of Turkey’s economy will depend on balancing short-term stimulus measures with structural reforms, fostering integration into global trade networks, and alleviating persistent poverty through well-directed social policies.

Below are two sets of data that illuminate Turkey’s economic conditions and development indicators.

**Table 1: Selected Macroeconomic Indicators for Turkey (2019–2022**)

| Indicator                       | 2019  | 2020  | 2021  | 2022 (est.) |
|---------------------------------|-------|-------|-------|-------------|
| Nominal GDP (US$ billion)       | 760   | 720   | 815   | 830         |
| Real GDP Growth Rate (%)        | 2.8   | 1.8   | 7.1   | 3.0         |
| Inflation Rate (%)              | 12.0  | 14.0  | 15.0  | 18.0        |
| Unemployment Rate (%)           | 13.7  | 13.2  | 11.8  | 11.5        |
| External Debt (% of GDP)        | 48.0  | 51.0  | 53.0  | 54.0        |
| Exchange Rate (TRY per US$)     | 5.8   | 6.5   | 8.0   | 9.6         |

**Table 2: Poverty and Development Indicators for Turkey**

| Indicator                                     | 2019 | 2020 | 2021 | 2022 (est.) |
|-----------------------------------------------|------|------|------|-------------|
| Population below National Poverty Line (%)    | 9.5  | 9.3  | 9.0  | 8.7         |
| Female Labor Force Participation Rate (%)     | 34.5 | 35.8 | 36.0 | 36.5        |
| Government Expenditure on Education (% of GDP)| 3.8  | 3.7  | 3.9  | 4.0         |
| FDI Inflows (US$ billion)                     | 10.0 | 7.5  | 9.0  | 8.5         |
| Rural Electrification Rate (%)                | 98   | 99   | 99   | 99          |

parts.content: Using information from the text/data and your knowledge of economics, discuss the extent to which Turkey can balance short-term stimulus measures (such as subsidies and low interest rates) with its long-term objectives of reducing poverty and encouraging foreign investment.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include 

**Definition:**

- Short-term stimulus measures: Government policies, such as low interest rates and subsidies, aimed at boosting immediate economic activity.  
- Long-term objectives: Sustained economic growth, reduced poverty, and an environment conducive to foreign investment.  

**Range of Arguments:**

- Argument 1: Role of Low Interest Rates in Stimulating Demand  
  - Low interest rates can lead to increased consumer spending (as seen in Table 1 with real GDP growth peaking at 7.1% in 2021), ultimately increasing Aggregate Demand, causing a rightward shift from AD1 to AD2. helping to reduce unemployment in the short run.  

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/75026ade-0543-4c86-a664-8b10f3c337cf)

  - However, the accompanying higher inflation (rising from 14% in 2020 to 18% in 2022) can undermine investor confidence if price instability persists.  
  - Persistent inflation could discourage long-term FDI inflows (Table 2 shows FDI variations that may be linked to macroeconomic stability), thus conflicting with the objective of encouraging foreign investment.

- Argument 2: Government Subsidies and Poverty Alleviation  
  - Subsidies on staple foods and electricity lower costs for low-income households, supporting short-term poverty reduction. The text indicates concern that poverty rates, while slowly decreasing to 8.7%, remain a challenge.  
  - General subsidies can be costly and may benefit wealthier households as well, increasing fiscal burden. This poses a risk to the government’s goal of achieving more targeted poverty reduction (Vision 2025 agenda). Targeted subsidies could potentially be more efficient, ensuring that resources are directed to those most in need while preserving budget space for development projects.

- Argument 3: Macroeconomic Stability and Foreign Investor Confidence  
  - Stable inflation and exchange rates are generally crucial for attracting FDI. Table 2 suggests FDI inflows fluctuate (from US$10.0 billion in 2019 down to US$7.5 billion in 2020, then up again to US$9.0 billion in 2021).  
  - Excessive short-term stimulus (like low interest rates) can prompt further currency depreciation, deterring foreign investment if investors fear exchange rate losses.  
  - Balancing a supportive monetary policy with credible anti-inflation measures could help maintain investor trust and allow Turkey to leverage its strategic location for increased FDI in the long term.

- Argument 4: Infrastructure Projects for Long-Run Growth  
  - Building highways, pipelines, or logistics corridors could boost productivity and help reduce regional disparities, thus tackling some root causes of poverty.  
  - In the long run, improved infrastructure can invite foreign firms seeking efficient supply chains, encouraging capital inflows.  
  - However, rising external debt, now 54% of GDP (Table 1), indicates potential risk if infrastructure spending is not managed prudently. Debt servicing costs may crowd out social spending on health and education.

- Argument 5: Human Capital Investment and Poverty Reduction  
  - The text highlights the risk of persistent poverty without adequate spending on education and training (supported by government expenditure on education rising to 4.0% of GDP).  
  - Directing funds to education cultivates a highly skilled workforce, potentially attracting long-term FDI into higher-value industries.  
  - If short-term subsidies limit the government’s ability to expand educational programs, poverty and inequality might remain entrenched, especially in the eastern and southeastern regions.

**Conclusion:**

- Balancing short-term stimulus measures with long-term objectives requires careful coordination between fiscal and monetary policies. 
- Moderate stimulus can support immediate growth, but maintaining price stability is essential for sustaining investor confidence. 
- Targeted social spending and infrastructure projects can address structural issues of poverty and boost productivity, though careful debt management is needed to avoid long-term risks. 
- Turkey’s strategic location and large youth population offer potential for robust growth, but success hinges on calibrated policies that do not overemphasize short-term demand at the expense of macroeconomic stability, human capital development, and investor trust.

questions.specification:
## Liberia’s Growth and Sustainability

Liberia is a low-income country in West Africa, historically relying on primary commodities such as rubber, iron ore, and timber for export earnings. Despite a significant decline in real GDP growth during the global economic downturn from 2020 to 2021, Liberia has gradually rebounded, registering an estimated 3.5% real GDP growth in 2022. Yet, inflation remains high at about 11% in the same year, pushing up the cost of living and exposing vulnerabilities in Liberia’s reliance on imported goods.

To boost economic activity, the Liberian government provides subsidies on fertilizers and basic food items. These subsidies aim to reduce production costs for smallholders and stabilize household spending. However, the fiscal burden of these expenditures has raised concerns about the sustainability of government finances, especially as external debt reached 54% of GDP in 2021. Moreover, subsidies have sometimes been criticized for benefiting larger commercial farms more than subsistence farmers.

In an effort to expand local manufacturing, Liberia maintains moderate tariffs on selected imported consumer goods, especially processed foods. Officials argue that these tariffs protect emerging local industries from external competition. However, critics claim they also raise prices for consumers. Meanwhile, trade relations within the Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS) have led to discussions on gradually lowering tariffs to promote regional trade.

Foreign direct investment (FDI) has been a significant driver of Liberia’s economic recovery. In mining and forestry, multinational firms have partnered with local businesses to upgrade infrastructure and technology. Despite a growing presence of investors in rubber processing, some local entrepreneurs struggle to gain access to financing. Government policymakers have also begun to explore new sectors, such as light manufacturing and tourism, to diversify the economy.

Environmental challenges, especially deforestation and soil erosion, have intensified. Liberia’s vast forests are at risk due to logging and surge in farmland expansion. Many small-scale gold mining operators also create negative externalities through water pollution. International organizations have urged stronger environmental regulations, but enforcement remains weak. Policymakers debate whether stricter rules might discourage inflows of FDI, a key pillar of the national development strategy.

Amid these challenges, the government launched the National Agriculture Development Initiative (NADI) in 2019. The program includes farm modernization projects, education in sustainable farming practices, and credit facilities for small and medium-sized enterprises. By 2021, agricultural productivity grew by 6%, according to official estimates. The authorities aim to increase Liberia’s self-sufficiency in staple crops, lessening dependency on volatile global commodity prices.

Liberia’s social indicators reflect both progress and persistent inequality. Official data suggest unemployment reached 14% in 2020, disproportionately affecting young people in urban centers. Although per capita income remains low, the Gini coefficient dropped slightly from 0.37 in 2018 to 0.35 in 2021, indicating mild improvements in income distribution. Critics note that many residents still work in informal sectors, lacking social protections.

Looking forward, the government plans to strengthen institutions, reduce tariff barriers within ECOWAS, and encourage private investment in infrastructure. Supporters of these initiatives see potential for enhanced export competitiveness and job creation. On the other hand, some analysts worry that overreliance on foreign borrowing and a narrow export base could expose the country to external shocks. As Liberia transitions from post-conflict recovery to a more stable growth path, balancing fiscal sustainability, social welfare, and environmental integrity remains a central policy challenge.

**Table 1: Liberia’s Selected Macroeconomic Indicators (2018–2021)**

| Indicator                   | 2018   | 2019   | 2020   | 2021   |
|-----------------------------|--------|--------|--------|--------|
| Nominal GDP (US$ billion)   | 2.1    | 2.3    | 2.2    | 2.4    |
| Real GDP Growth Rate (%)    | 2.0    | 1.4    | -3.3   | 2.0    |
| Inflation Rate (%)          | 23.4   | 20.5   | 13.8   | 11.5   |
| Unemployment Rate (%)       | 12.0   | 12.8   | 14.0   | 13.5   |
| Gov’t Debt (% of GDP)       | 49.0   | 52.5   | 53.0   | 54.0   |
| Exchange Rate (LRD per US$) | 155    | 185    | 200    | 190    |

**Table 2: Data on the National Agriculture Development Initiative (NADI)**

| Indicator                                    | 2018  | 2019  | 2020  | 2021  |
|----------------------------------------------|-------|-------|-------|-------|
| Agricultural Productivity Growth (%)         | 1.5   | 2.0   | 3.0   | 6.0   |
| Gov’t Spending on Agriculture (US$ m)        | 80    | 90    | 95    | 110   |

parts.content: Using a production possibilities curve (PPC) diagram, explain how increases in foreign direct investment (FDI) in mining (paragraph 4) might affect Liberia’s capacity to produce goods and services in the long run.

- This capital deepening can shift the PPC outward, reflecting an increase in the economy’s overall capacity to produce both capital and consumer goods.  
- Increased investment may improve productivity, leading to higher potential output in the long run if resources are efficiently allocated.  
- As the PPC shifts out (PPC1 to PPC2), Liberia can produce more across multiple sectors beyond mining, contributing to long-term growth and diversification.

parts.content: Using a demand-and-supply-of-currency diagram, explain how lowering tariff barriers within ECOWAS (paragraph 8) could impact the exchange rate of the Liberian dollar.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/a9cc2254-e657-470c-bb25-bd8ee9b876bf)

- Lower tariff barriers could increase Liberia’s exports within ECOWAS if regional trade expands, raising foreign demand for Liberian dollars and shifting the demand curve for the currency to the right (Dd1 to Dd).  
- Lower tariffs also allow for more imports, which might increase the supply of Liberian dollars in the foreign exchange market when importers exchange LRD for foreign currencies.  
- If the demand increase exceeds the supply increase, the Liberian dollar may appreciate; if supply increases more than demand, the currency may depreciate.  
- The net effect depends on the relative strengths of increased export competitiveness versus increased import expenditures in the region.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using a negative externality diagram, explain how small-scale gold mining (paragraph 5) may lead to market failure if environmental regulations remain weak.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/7136117e-f0e5-443c-8691-8be7f6dd9464)

- Small-scale gold mining can generate costs (e.g., water pollution) borne by society but not fully accounted for in private mining decisions, causing a divergence between marginal private cost (MPC) and marginal social cost (MSC).  
- With weak environmental regulations, miners do not internalize these costs, leading to overproduction of gold (and overuse of environmental resources).  
- The market equilibrium output exceeds the socially optimum level (Qm and Qopt, resulting in welfare loss.  
- Effective regulation, or a mechanism to internalize external costs, is required to move production closer to the socially efficient output.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

- Lockdowns or reduced global demand might have also curtailed exports (rubber, iron ore), contributing to job losses.
