parts.content: Using a microeconomics demand-and-supply diagram, explain how lowering tariff rates on machinery for agribusiness modernization (paragraph 7) could affect market outcomes for domestic producers and consumers.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/a19559b1-2916-4df2-b6f5-ed8210a2b8b1)

- Cutting tariff rates reduces the price of imported machinery. In a standard microeconomics demand-and-supply diagram for machinery, domestic consumers (farmers) benefit from lower prices and increase their quantity demanded.
-  Domestic producers of machinery may face more competition, leading to potential decreases in domestic output if they cannot match price or quality. 
-  Overall, consumer surplus (for farmers purchasing machinery) increases because they pay a lower price, while domestic producer surplus might shrink.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using a poverty cycle diagram, explain how limited access to affordable credit for small farms (paragraph 2) might perpetuate Uzbekistan’s rural poverty rate.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/5481ab11-6145-4c05-9682-eb55a1ad3765)

- Small farms that struggle to secure loans at reasonable interest rates cannot invest sufficiently in modern equipment, irrigation, or higher-quality inputs. This lowers productivity and output, hindering income growth and perpetuating low saving capacity. 
- A poverty cycle diagram would show how low income leads to low savings, which leads to limited investment in human or physical capital, reinforcing low incomes and poverty levels in rural areas.

parts.content: Using a monetary policy diagram (showing money supply and interest rates), explain how the central bank’s relatively high policy rate could affect inflation and economic growth.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/8131d10a-0f01-4b00-b1a9-555aa58ada10)

- Keeping a high policy rate discourages excessive borrowing and helps reduce inflationary pressure by dampening aggregate demand. 
- However, it can also slow economic growth by discouraging investment and consumption financed by credit. 
- In a monetary policy diagram (with interest rate on the vertical axis and quantity of money on the horizontal axis), a relatively high interest rate corresponds to a stricter monetary policy stance, controlling inflation but potentially reducing real GDP growth.

parts.content: Using information from the text/data (including Table 2) and your knowledge of economics, evaluate the effectiveness of Uzbekistan’s reform policies in achieving both economic growth and poverty reduction.
parts.markscheme:
**Definition:**

- Economic growth: An increase in real output of goods and services measured by real GDP over time.  
- Poverty: A situation in which individuals or groups lack the resources needed to meet basic needs, often measured by poverty rates or income thresholds.  
- Structural reforms: Policies aimed at changing fundamental economic structures, such as reducing barriers to investment or liberalizing trade.

**Range of Arguments:**

- Argument 1: Diversification Beyond Primary Commodities  
  - Economic Theory: Reducing reliance on primary commodities can stabilize export revenues and enable more consistent growth, as export earnings become less vulnerable to external price shocks.  
  - Application to Case: Uzbekistan’s shift from cotton, gold, and gas exports to textile manufacturing, IT, and tourism could promote sustainability and job creation (text references the government’s focus on diversification).  
  - Evaluation: While diversification can reduce risks, it requires time, significant investment in infrastructure, and improvements in human capital. In the short run, global commodity prices might still dominate exports, maintaining vulnerability to fluctuations.

- Argument 2: Attraction of FDI Through Bureaucratic Reforms  
  - Economic Theory: Lowering bureaucratic barriers and ensuring a more business-friendly environment can attract greater FDI inflows, which can boost capital formation, technology transfers, and employment.  
  - Application to Case: Paragraph 2 points to easing administrative processes for foreign investors, with foreign inflows of US 1.3 billion in 2021 (Table 2).  
  - Evaluation: FDI may concentrate in certain sectors (e.g., textiles, resource extraction), leaving rural areas and agriculture less affected. If corruption and regulatory unpredictability persist, FDI potential could be under-realized.

- Argument 3: Exchange Rate Reforms and Inflation  
  - Economic Theory: A flexible exchange rate can promote export competitiveness, but a devaluation often leads to cost-push inflation through increased import prices.  
  - Application to Case: Devaluation in 2017 improved exports (e.g., cotton, natural gas) and encouraged remittance inflows but contributed to inflation rising to 12.4% in 2021 (Table 1).  
  - Evaluation: While growth might benefit from boosted exports, rising inflation could hurt low-income households and worsen poverty if wages do not keep pace. Monetary policy remains crucial to keep inflation manageable.

- Argument 4: Tariff Reduction and Trade Liberalization  

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/c52e1155-5041-4be2-840b-62d0bb621bc2)

  - Economic Theory: Lower tariffs generally increase efficiency by allowing imports at cheaper prices, benefitting consumer surplus and encouraging competition.  
  - Application to Case: Uzbekistan reduced average tariff rates to 9% (Table 2), particularly on machinery to spur modernization. This potentially raises agricultural productivity.  
  - Evaluation: Such reforms can stimulate rural production and growth, yet domestic producers unprepared for foreign competition may suffer. Some industries might need strategic support (e.g., subsidies for upgrading technology).

- Argument 5: Social Policies to Address Poverty  
  - Economic Theory: Targeted cash transfers can boost incomes of the poor, possibly creating a multiplier effect in rural areas if it stimulates consumption of local goods and services.  
  - Application to Case: Uzbekistan’s official poverty rate is 15% (Table 2), with some underemployment unaccounted for. Government measures (vocational training, cash transfers) can break the poverty cycle by improving human capital.  
  - Evaluation: Success depends on the scale and continuity of these programs. Without stronger rural credit markets and infrastructure, small farms and businesses might not escape persistent poverty.

- Argument 6: High Policy Rate and Macro Stability  
  - Economic Theory: A high interest rate can curb inflation but may limit aggregate demand and slow growth.  
  - Application to Case: Policymakers are trying to ensure stability while pursuing reforms. Real GDP growth jumped to 7.4% in 2021 (Table 1), suggesting some success, though inflation also remained high at 12.4%.  
  - Evaluation: Striking a balance is challenging. Tight monetary policy can improve macroeconomic credibility but might dampen domestic investment, slowing long-term growth and limiting job creation.

**Conclusion and Evaluation:**

- Although reforms have stimulated notable real GDP growth (averaging around 5.8%), poverty remains a significant concern, especially in rural areas.  
- Short-term gains from devaluation and FDI inflows may be partly offset by inflation and uneven sectoral development.  
- The government’s range of policies—tariff reductions, exchange rate liberalization, and social programs—shows promise, but the effectiveness ultimately relies on ongoing improvements in governance, financial accessibility, and human capital.  
- Sustained reform consistency, transparency, and infrastructure development will likely be crucial to ensuring that economic growth filters down to vulnerable communities, thus reducing poverty in the longer term.

questions.specification:
Dominica is a small island economy in the Caribbean with a population of approximately 72 000 people. The country is heavily reliant on agriculture and tourism. Hurricanes and tropical storms frequently disrupt economic activity, causing significant damage to infrastructure, agriculture, and tourism facilities. Despite these challenges, Dominica’s government has introduced various strategies to improve economic growth and reduce poverty.

In recent years, Dominica has sought to diversify away from agriculture (particularly bananas), moving toward eco-tourism and financial services. However, agriculture still plays a vital role in the economy, especially in rural areas where employment opportunities outside farming are limited.

Table 1 below shows selected macroeconomic indicators for Dominica for 2021 and 2022:

| Indicator                        | 2021   | 2022   |
|----------------------------------|--------|--------|
| Nominal GDP (EC$ millions)*      | 1 720  | 1 817  |
| Real GDP growth (%)              | 3.0    | 5.2    |
| Unemployment rate (%)            | 11.9   | 10.5   |
| Inflation rate (%)               | 1.5    | 3.8    |
| Government spending (EC$ millions) | 470    | 490    |
| Average monthly banana exports (tonnes) | 1 800  | 1 950  |

*EC$ = Eastern Caribbean dollars

Table 2 shows data on Dominica’s banana exports to a regional trading partner and estimated price elasticity of demand (PED) for bananas in that market from 2021 to 2022:

| Year | Quantity of bananas exported (tonnes) | Price per tonne (EC$) | Estimated PED |
|------|---------------------------------------|-----------------------|---------------|
| 2021 | 1 800                                 | 800                   | –0.6          |
| 2022 | 1 950                                 | 840                   | –0.6          |

Dominica’s tax system consists of a value-added tax (VAT), corporate income tax, and personal income tax. Some taxes are progressive, helping to reduce income inequality. Table 3 displays key features of Dominica’s tax rates (approximate) and Gini coefficient values before and after implementation of new social programs in 2022:

| Measure                                       | Rate / Value                  |
|-----------------------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Standard VAT rate                              | 15 %                          |
| Reduced VAT rate (applied to essential goods)  | 10 %                          |
| Corporate income tax                           | 25 %                          |
| Personal income tax (top marginal rate)        | 35 %                          |
| Gini coefficient (2021)                        | 0.42                          |
| Gini coefficient (2022, after social programs) | 0.40                          |

Figure 1 (hypothetical) shows a simplified Keynesian multiplier effect in Dominica following an increase in government spending in 2022:

• Initial injection into the economy: EC$20 million  
• Estimated marginal propensity to consume (MPC): 0.8  

Government officials are debating policies to promote economic diversification, attract foreign direct investment, and reduce inequality.

parts.content: Using the information provided in Table 1, calculate the approximate percentage increase in Dominica’s nominal GDP from 2021 to 2022.
parts.markscheme:
• Subtract 2021 GDP (EC$1720 million) from 2022 GDP (EC$1817 million) to find the increase: 1817 – 1720 = 97.  
• Divide 97 by 1720 and multiply by 100: (97 ÷ 1720) × 100 ≈ 5.64%.  
• Final answer: approximately 5.6%.

parts.content: Using the data provided on banana exports in Table 2 and assuming the PED remains constant at –0.6, calculate the percentage change in quantity demanded if the price per tonne were to increase by 5 % from its 2022 level.
parts.markscheme:
• The formula for percentage change in quantity demanded: (%ΔQ) = PED × (%ΔP).  
• PED = –0.6, %ΔP = +5%.  
• (%ΔQ) = –0.6 × 5% = –3%.  
• Final answer: quantity demanded would decrease by approximately 3%.

parts.content: Using Figure 1’s information on the Keynesian multiplier effect (initial injection of EC$20 million with an MPC of 0.8), calculate the total change in real GDP in Dominica resulting from this injection.
parts.markscheme:
• The multiplier = 1 ÷ (1 – MPC) = 1 ÷ (1 – 0.8) = 5.  
• Total change in real GDP = 5 × EC$20 million = EC$100 million.

parts.content: Assume that the government decides to increase government spending further by EC$15 million. Using the same MPC of 0.8, calculate the additional change in real GDP in Dominica generated by this new spending.
parts.markscheme:
• Multiplier = 5.  
• Additional change in real GDP = 5 × EC$15 million = EC$75 million.

parts.content: Define the term “progressive tax.”
parts.markscheme: • A progressive tax is one in which the tax rate increases as an individual’s income rises, meaning those with higher incomes pay a proportionally higher rate of tax.

parts.content: Using an AD/AS diagram, explain how a sudden decrease in tourist arrivals due to a severe tropical storm might affect short-run economic output and the price level in Dominica.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/1b669548-0445-4a86-9761-3111800151a1)
• A decline in tourist arrivals reduces export revenues from tourism, leading to a decrease in aggregate demand.  
• The AD curve would shift left in the short run, resulting in lower real output and a decrease in the average price level.  
• Loss of income for tourism-related businesses and workers further dampens consumption, reinforcing the leftward shift of AD.  
• The new equilibrium would show reduced GDP and a lower price level compared to before the storm.  

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks>

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using information from Table 2, calculate the percentage change in total revenue from banana exports between 2021 and 2022. Show your working.
parts.markscheme:
- Revenue in 2021: 1,800 × 800 = EC$1,440,000
- Revenue in 2022: 1,950 × 840 = EC$1,638,000
- Change in revenue: 1,638,000 - 1,440,000 = EC$198,000
- Percentage change: (198,000 ÷ 1,440,000) × 100 = 13.75%
- Percentage change in total revenue: 13.8% (or 13.75%)

parts.content: Using information from the text above, explain two ways in which dependence on agriculture might leave Dominica vulnerable to external shocks.
parts.markscheme:
• Frequent hurricanes damage crops and infrastructure, reducing agricultural output and export revenues, which undermines farmers’ earnings and the country’s GDP.  
• Fluctuations in global or regional demand and prices for important crops like bananas can sharply affect rural incomes, leading to instability in employment and foreign exchange earnings.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and your knowledge of economics, recommend a policy that the government of Dominica could implement to reduce income inequality while maintaining economic growth.
parts.markscheme:
• Income inequality is the unequal distribution of income among individuals or households within an economy, while economic growth is an increase in real output over time.  
• A policy that targets enhanced skills training and education programs in rural and urban areas can reduce inequality by improving employability and productivity; this can expand the long-run aggregate supply while increasing the earning potential of lower-income groups.  
• Dominica’s reliance on agriculture and the data showing a reduction in the Gini coefficient from 0.42 to 0.40 suggest that social programs have already helped, so additional targeted spending in vocational education or infrastructure upgrades could amplify this effect.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/1ca4d93a-6f31-4fe2-ae69-ff53772f7fb5)
• Strengthening infrastructure (roads, communication networks) in rural regions supports agricultural efficiency and fosters eco-tourism growth, expanding economic activity and job opportunities.  
• Such government spending can be financed through slightly higher progressive taxation or reallocation of expenditure, ensuring that high-income earners contribute more while avoiding excessive distortions to investment.  
• The multiplier effect in Dominica is relatively large (as indicated by the information on MPC = 0.8); continued injections into skills development and infrastructure can stimulate aggregate demand and support higher output.  
• Greater human capital formation improves the productivity of the non-farm sector, diversifies the economy beyond agriculture, and reduces vulnerability to storms and price shocks.  
• The approach balances equity (by improving wages for the lower-income population and limiting regressive effects) and efficiency (by growing productive capacity through better-trained labor).  
• For long-term impact, policies that encourage foreign direct investment in sectors like eco-tourism and financial services widen employment opportunities, solidify growth prospects, and reduce inequality if linked to skill-development initiatives.  
• Sustained reduction in the Gini coefficient and stable real GDP growth are likely outcomes if the government invests in human capital, expands progressive measures, and promotes economic diversification.

questions.specification:
**Economic Recovery and Policy in Arcadia**

The Republic of Arcadia is emerging from a period of stagnation. While consumer confidence is rising, the economy remains below its full employment level of output. The government and the Central Bank are debating the most effective way to stimulate further growth without triggering excessive inflation. 

Recent data suggests that while domestic consumption has increased, investment remains cautious due to previous volatility. Furthermore, global trade conditions have impacted Arcadia's trade balance. The following tables provide data on Arcadia’s macroeconomic performance.

Table 1: Components of Aggregate Demand (Billion ARD)

| Component | 2022 | 2023 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Consumption ($C$) | 450 | 480 |
| Investment ($I$) | 120 | 110 |
| Government Spending ($G$) | 200 | 215 |
| Exports ($X$) | 120 | 130 |
| Imports ($M$) | 100 | 125 |

Table 2: Marginal Propensities and Potential Output

| Indicator | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Marginal Propensity to Consume (MPC) | 0.8 |
| Marginal Propensity to Tax (MPT) | 0.1 |
| Marginal Propensity to Import (MPM) | 0.1 |
| Full Employment Output ($Y_f$) | 850 Billion ARD |
| Current Real Output ($Y_1$) | 810 Billion ARD |

Arcadia’s Central Bank is considering a decrease in interest rates to close the output gap, but some economists argue that supply-side constraints, such as rising nominal wages in the manufacturing sector, could complicate this strategy.

parts.content: Define aggregate demand.
parts.markscheme:
- The total spending on all goods and services produced in an economy <marks>1 mark</marks>
- at a given price level and over a given period of time. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Define the Consumer Price Index (CPI) and explain one way it is used to measure inflation.
parts.markscheme:
- CPI is a measure that examines the weighted average of prices of a basket of consumer goods and services. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explanation: It measures inflation by tracking the percentage change in the cost of this basket over time relative to a base year, reflecting the change in the cost of living for a typical household. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using Table 1, calculate the percentage change in Arcadia’s trade balance (net exports) between 2022 and 2023.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate Net Exports ($X - M$) for each year:
  - 2022: $120 - 100 = 20$ Billion ARD
  - 2023: $130 - 125 = 5$ Billion ARD

- Apply percentage change formula: $\frac{5 - 20}{20} \times 100$

- Correct substitution and calculation: $-75\%$ or a $75\%$ decrease. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Correct final answer with sign/direction: $-75\%$. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using an AD/AS diagram, explain how an increase in interest rates would likely affect Arcadia’s price level and real output.
parts.markscheme:
- Correctly Drawn AD/AS Diagram <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Axes: Price Level (y) and Real GDP (x).
  - Curves: AD, SRAS.
  - Leftward shift of AD ($AD_1$ to $AD_2$).

- Explanation of impact on AD <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Higher interest rates increase the cost of borrowing for households (reducing $C$) and firms (reducing $I$), leading to a fall in Aggregate Demand.

- Explanation of impact on Price Level and Output <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - The leftward shift in AD leads to a lower equilibrium price level and a decrease in real GDP/output.

parts.content: Explain, using the provided diagram and referring to Table 2, what is meant by a deflationary (recessionary) gap in Arcadia.
parts.markscheme:
- Definition: A deflationary gap exists when the equilibrium level of real output ($Y_1$) is less than the full employment level of output ($Y_f$). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reference to Table 2: $Y_f = 850$ Billion while current $Y_1 = 810$ Billion, meaning there is an output gap of $40$ Billion ARD. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Diagram reference: The gap is the horizontal distance between $Y_1$ (where AD intersects AS) and the vertical part of the Keynesian AS curve (or the LRAS curve) at $Y_f$. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Significance: This indicates that the economy is underutilizing its resources, leading to cyclical unemployment and downward pressure on the price level. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<marks>2 marks for correct diagram reference OR explanation</marks>
<marks>4 marks for both diagram reference AND explanation with data</marks>

parts.content: Using an AD/AS diagram, explain how an increase in nominal wages in the manufacturing sector might lead to cost-push inflation.
parts.markscheme:
- Correctly Drawn AD/AS Diagram <marks>2 marks</marks>
  - Axes: Price Level (y) and Real GDP (x).
  - Leftward/Upward shift of the SRAS curve ($SRAS_1$ to $SRAS_2$).
  - New equilibrium showing higher Price Level ($P_1$ to $P_2$) and lower Output ($Y_1$ to $Y_2$).

- Explanation <marks>2 marks</marks>
  - Higher nominal wages increase the costs of production for firms.
  - To maintain profit margins, firms reduce the quantity supplied at every price level, shifting SRAS to the left.
  - This results in a higher price level (inflation) alongside stagnant or falling output (stagflation).

parts.content: Explain the concept of the multiplier and how it might influence the final impact of a change in government spending on Arcadia’s national income.
parts.markscheme:
- The multiplier effect occurs when an initial injection into the circular flow (like $G$) leads to a larger final increase in national income. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This happens because one person's spending becomes another person's income, which is then partially re-spent. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The size of the multiplier ($k$) is determined by leakages: $k = \frac{1}{MPS + MPT + MPM}$ or $k = \frac{1}{1 - MPC}$. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- In Arcadia, an increase in $G$ will lead to multiple rounds of spending, but the final impact is limited by the propensity to save, tax, and import. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain the difference between the Keynesian and New Classical (Monetarist) views of the Long-Run Aggregate Supply (LRAS) curve.
parts.markscheme:
- New Classical View: The LRAS is vertical at the full employment level of output ($Y_{fe}$), suggesting that in the long run, the economy is self-correcting and changes in AD only affect the price level, not output. <marks>2 marks</marks>
- Keynesian View: The AS curve has three sections: horizontal (spare capacity), upward-sloping (bottlenecks), and vertical (full employment). It suggests that an economy can remain in an equilibrium below full employment (a deflationary gap) for a long time without government intervention. <marks>2 marks</marks>

parts.content: Using information from the text/data and your knowledge of economics, evaluate the effectiveness of the Arcadia Central Bank using expansionary monetary policy (lowering interest rates) to close the deflationary gap.
parts.markscheme:
### Arguments for the effectiveness of expansionary monetary policy
- Lower interest rates reduce the cost of borrowing for firms, encouraging Investment ($I$), which has been noted as "cautious" in the text <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduced interest rates lower the incentive to save and decrease mortgage/loan repayments, increasing disposable income and Consumption ($C$) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Expansionary policy can lead to a depreciation of the currency (ARD), making exports more competitive and imports more expensive, potentially improving the declining trade balance shown in Table 1 <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The multiplier effect will amplify the initial increase in $C$ and $I$, helping to close the 40 Billion ARD gap more effectively <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Compared to fiscal policy, monetary policy can be adjusted quickly by the Central Bank without political delays <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Limitations and risks
- "Cautious" business confidence may mean that even with lower interest rates, firms do not increase Investment if they are pessimistic about future demand <marks>1 mark</marks>
- There is a time lag (often 12-18 months) before the full effects of interest rate changes are felt in the real economy <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Lowering interest rates may be ineffective if the economy is in a liquidity trap or if commercial banks do not pass on the rate cuts to consumers <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Potential for inflationary pressure: if AD shifts too far or if supply-side constraints (rising wages mentioned in text) exist, it may cause demand-pull inflation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Impact on the trade balance: while a weaker ARD helps $X$, if the MPM is high (0.1 in Table 2), the increase in income will lead to significantly higher imports ($M$), worsening the trade deficit <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Evaluation
- The policy's success depends on the level of consumer and business confidence; without confidence, $C$ and $I$ remain interest-inelastic <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The magnitude of the multiplier (Table 2 data shows leakages of $MPT+MPM+MPS$) determines how much AD actually shifts <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Monetary policy might be more effective if used in conjunction with expansionary fiscal policy or supply-side policies to address the wage-push constraints mentioned <marks>1 mark</marks>
- In the Keynesian view, if the economy is on the horizontal section of the AS curve, expansionary policy is highly effective at increasing output without inflation, but this changes as the economy nears $Y_f$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award up to 4 marks for application to the Arcadia context (e.g., using the gap of 40bn or trade data). Award up to 4 marks for development of relevant economic concepts (e.g., AD components, multiplier). Award up to 7 marks for evaluative comments.</note>

questions.specification:
Greenland is an autonomous territory within the Kingdom of Denmark, with a population of approximately 56,000. Its economy depends heavily on fishing, particularly for shrimp and halibut, which account for about 90% of its total export revenues. In recent years, Greenland has also experienced steady growth in the tourism sector, with more visitors attracted by the region’s unique Arctic landscapes. 

Due to harsh weather conditions and limited arable land, Greenland’s domestic production of agricultural goods is minimal, and most food is imported. Real GDP has shown modest growth in the last several years, supported by fishing exports and increasing public expenditure aimed at improving infrastructure. However, concerns have been raised about inequalities in income distribution, high dependence on a single export (fish), and the sustainability of public finances.

Below are selected data and tables on Greenland’s economy.

**Table 1: Key macroeconomic indicators for Greenland (2018–2021**)

| Year | Real GDP (million DKK) | Real GDP growth (%) | Population | Unemployment Rate (%) |
| ---  | -----------------------| --------------------|------------|----------------------- |
| 2018 | 18,700                 | 2.0                | 56,000     | 5.8                   |
| 2019 | 19,100                 | 2.1                | 56,200     | 5.5                   |
| 2020 | 19,200                 | 0.5                | 56,300     | 6.2                   |
| 2021 | 19,600                 | 2.1                | 56,400     | 5.9                   |

**Table 2: Market data for fish in Greenland (annual domestic demand)**

(Prices in DKK per kilogram)

| Price per kg | Quantity Demanded (tons) |
| ------------ | ------------------------ |
| 40           | 12,000                  |
| 45           | 11,600                  |
| 50           | 11,100                  |
| 55           | 10,300                  |

**Table 3: Distribution of income in Greenland and selected countries**

| Country    | Gini Coefficient | % of Population below national poverty line |
|------------|------------------|---------------------------------------------|
| Greenland  | 0.33            | 9%                                          |
| Denmark    | 0.28            | 5%                                          |
| Iceland    | 0.25            | 6%                                          |

**Table 4: Tax rates in Greenland (2021)**

| Type of tax          | Rate of tax              |
|----------------------|--------------------------|
| Corporate income tax | 25%                     |
| Personal income tax  | Progressive up to 42%*  |
| Indirect tax (VAT)   | 15%                     |

*Higher marginal tax rates may apply above certain income thresholds.

Government spending on infrastructure has been steadily increasing in Greenland, especially in the construction and improvement of new ports to accommodate more cruise ships and cargo vessels. It is estimated that every 100 million DKK spent on infrastructure increases real GDP by 120 million DKK, suggesting a potential Keynesian multiplier effect of 1.2 in the local economy.

parts.content: Using the information in Table 1, calculate Greenland’s average annual rate of real GDP growth for the entire period 2018–2021.
parts.markscheme:
- Add the annual growth rates: 2.0% + 2.1% + 0.5% + 2.1% = 6.7%.  
- Divide by 4 to find the average: 6.7% ÷ 4 = 1.675%.  
- The average annual rate of real GDP growth is approximately 1.7%.

parts.content: The government of Greenland plans to invest an additional 200 million DKK in port infrastructure. Assuming the Keynesian multiplier for Greenland is 1.2, calculate the total increase in real GDP resulting from this additional investment.
parts.markscheme:
- Multiply the injection by the multiplier: 200 million DKK × 1.2 = 240 million DKK.  
- The total increase in real GDP is 240 million DKK.

parts.content: Using the information in Table 2, calculate the price elasticity of demand (PED) for fish in Greenland when the price increases from 45 DKK/kg to 50 DKK/kg.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate percentage change in quantity demanded: ((11,100 − 11,600) ÷ 11,600) × 100% ≈ −4.31%.  
- Calculate percentage change in price: ((50 − 45) ÷ 45) × 100% ≈ 11.11%.  
- PED ≈ −4.31% ÷ 11.11% ≈ −0.39 (absolute value ≈ 0.39).

parts.content: Assume that, with a price increase from 45 DKK/kg to 50 DKK/kg, total spending on fish by domestic consumers changes from 522,000 DKK to 555,000 DKK. Using this information, calculate the change in total spending on fish.
parts.markscheme:
- Subtract the initial spending from the new spending: 555,000 DKK − 522,000 DKK = 33,000 DKK.  
- The change in total spending is 33,000 DKK.

parts.content: Define the term “Gini coefficient.”
parts.markscheme: The Gini coefficient is a numerical measure of income inequality, indicating how far a population’s income distribution deviates from perfect equality.

parts.content: Using a demand–supply diagram, explain how a rise in tourism could affect the market for locally produced fish in Greenland (assuming some tourists also consume local fish).
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/5ea8fc5f-8663-4f72-8e4b-1c768ec015fa)

- Additional tourists increase demand for local fish, shifting the demand curve to the right.  
- As demand rises, the new equilibrium is likely to show a higher price and higher equilibrium quantity.  
- A rightward shift in demand reflects growing consumption driven by tourist arrivals.  
- Producers supplying local fish may respond by increasing quantity to meet increased demand.
- Incrase in Demand shift Demand curve to the right (D1 -> D2), increasing equilibrium price (Pe -> P1) and equilibrium quantity (Q1 -> Q2)

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks>

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using the data in Table 3, explain how income inequality in Greenland compares to that of the other countries shown and discuss one possible economic implication of Greenland’s level of inequality.
parts.markscheme:
- Greenland’s Gini coefficient (0.33) is higher than Denmark’s (0.28) and Iceland’s (0.25), suggesting greater income inequality.  
- The percentage of the population below the national poverty line (9%) is also higher compared to the other two countries.  
- Higher income inequality could lead to lower overall consumption if a large segment of the population has reduced purchasing power.  
- This may result in lower potential economic growth or increased government spending on social welfare to address inequality.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and knowledge of economics, recommend one policy which the government of Greenland could implement to reduce reliance on fishing exports. In the answer, refer to how this policy might affect macroeconomic goals such as growth, employment, and income distribution.
parts.markscheme:
- Diversification of the economy can be pursued by incentivizing the development of alternative industries, such as expanding tourism or promoting small-scale manufacturing.  
- Economic diversification reduces dependence on fishing exports by creating new sources of revenue, aligning with the existing growth in tourism and taking advantage of Greenland’s Arctic landscapes.  
- Government support can include targeted subsidies or tax incentives for private firms to invest in tourism infrastructure beyond port facilities, stimulating the emergence of related service industries and attracting foreign direct investment.  
- Increased output in the tourism or alternative sectors contributes to higher real GDP over time, especially if the multiplier effect spreads through local communities.  
- Employment might rise in hospitality, transport services, and marketing fields, potentially reducing unemployment from current levels shown in Table 1.  
- Income distribution may improve if new job opportunities in alternative sectors draw workers from various income brackets, reducing the concentration of income in fishing alone.  
- Income inequality could be addressed by ensuring training programs, so the labor force is equipped to work in new industries, which helps lower-income groups.  
- Government subsidies and tax incentives boost investment and consumption, causing AD to shift right (AD1 -> AD2). This leads to a higher real GDP (Y1 -> Y2) and higher price level (pl1 -> pl2), showing short-run economic growth. The diagram captures how demand-side policies can stimulate output without necessarily causing inflation if there's spare capacity.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ced8f236-b30e-40b3-9894-cb27c98718e0)

- Careful monitoring of government finances is necessary to manage the cost of subsidies or infrastructure projects, minimizing long-term debt risks.  
- Pursuing alternative industries would mitigate external vulnerabilities (such as fluctuations in global fish prices) and underpin long-term sustainable development of Greenland’s economy.

questions.specification:
Dominica is a small island nation in the Eastern Caribbean, with a population of approximately 72 000 people. Known as the “Nature Isle of the Caribbean,” its economy depends heavily on ecotourism, banana exports, and foreign investment. Having experienced significant hurricane damage in recent years (notably Hurricane Maria in 2017), Dominica has been focusing on rebuilding infrastructure, encouraging sustainable agriculture, and diversifying tourism activities to boost economic resilience.

In 2021, tourism made up around 25 % of GDP while bananas continued to be a key export crop. The government has been working to modernize the banana industry by providing subsidies for more climate-resilient farming methods, hoping to maintain Dominica’s main export market in the region. However, fluctuations in global banana prices have affected farmers’ incomes.

Dominica’s tax system includes corporate tax at 25 %, a progressive personal income tax with top rates close to 35 %, and a value-added tax (VAT) of 15 % on most goods and services (some basic food items are zero-rated). Small firms often struggle with compliance, contributing to lower-than-expected government tax revenues.

Dominica’s income distribution shows moderate inequality, which the government aims to address by expanding social safety nets and creating job-training programs. Nonetheless, the authorities have considered further policy measures to make growth more inclusive and resilient.

Below are three tables containing recent data useful for understanding economic performance and key markets in Dominica.

Table 1: Macroeconomic Indicators for Dominica (2018–2021)

| Year | Real GDP (EC$ millions) | Population | Unemployment Rate (%) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 2018 | 480 | 71 000 | 21.0 |
| 2019 | 495 | 71 200 | 20.5 |
| 2020 | 500 | 71 300 | 22.0 |
| 2021 | 520 | 71 500 | 20.0 |

Table 2: Banana Market Data (2021)

| Price per kg (EC$) | Quantity Demanded (tonnes) | Quantity Supplied (tonnes) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1.00 | 14 000 | 10 000 |
| 1.50 | 12 000 | 12 000 |
| 2.00 | 10 000 | 15 000 |

Table 3: Income Distribution, Taxation, and Other Indicators (2021)

| Gini Coefficient | Progressive Tax Rates (personal) | Corporate Tax Rate | VAT Rate | Average MPC (estimated) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 0.42 | 15 %–35 % | 25 % | 15 % | 0.80 |

parts.content: Using the information in Table 1, calculate the approximate percentage change in real GDP for Dominica from 2020 to 2021.
parts.markscheme:
• Subtract 500 (real GDP in 2020) from 520 (real GDP in 2021) to get an increase of 20.  
• Divide 20 by 500 and multiply by 100% to get approximately 4%.

parts.content: Assume that the marginal propensity to consume (MPC) in Dominica is as shown in Table 3. Using the formula for the Keynesian multiplier, calculate the total change in real GDP if the government injects an additional EC$10 million into public infrastructure spending.
parts.markscheme:
• The Keynesian multiplier is 1 ÷ (1 – MPC) = 1 ÷ (1 – 0.80) = 1 ÷ 0.20 = 5.  
• Multiply 5 by the EC$10 million injection to get a total change in real GDP of EC$50 million.

parts.content: Referring to the data in Table 2, calculate the price elasticity of demand (PED) for bananas in Dominica when the price increases from EC$1.00 per kg to EC$1.50 per kg.
parts.markscheme:
• The percentage change in quantity is (12 000 – 14 000) ÷ 14 000 × 100% = –14.3%.  
• The percentage change in price is (1.50 – 1.00) ÷ 1.00 × 100% = 50%.  
• PED ≈ (–14.3%) ÷ 50% ≈ –0.29 (inelastic demand).

parts.content: Again using Table 2, calculate the surplus or shortage in the banana market at a price of EC$2.00 per kg.
parts.markscheme:
• Quantity demanded is 10 000 tonnes; quantity supplied is 15 000 tonnes.  
• There is a surplus of 5 000 tonnes.

parts.content: Using an AD/AS diagram, explain how a rise in ecotourism arrivals might affect real GDP and the price level in Dominica in the short run.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/fda47c43-6b1f-4056-bf46-71b0276a437f)
• The increase in ecotourism arrivals raises aggregate demand (AD) as tourists spend on accommodation, transport, and other services.  
• In the short run, the AD curve shifts to the right, leading to a higher level of real output (real GDP).  
• The outward shift of AD typically causes an increase in the average price level, as resources become more fully employed.  
• In an AD/AS diagram, the new equilibrium shows both higher real GDP and a higher price level compared to the initial equilibrium.

parts.content: Using information from Table 1, calculate Dominica's real GDP per capita in 2021. Show your working.
parts.markscheme:
- Real GDP in 2021: EC$520 million
- Population in 2021: 71,500
- GDP per capita = 520,000,000 ÷ 71,500 = EC$7,272.73
- Real GDP per capita: EC$7,273 (or EC$7,272.73)

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and your knowledge of economics, recommend a policy that the government of Dominica could implement to reduce unemployment while maintaining growth and resilience in the economy. Support your recommendation with appropriate economic theory.
parts.markscheme:
• A policy that targets structural unemployment and encourages diversification is the introduction of a government-financed vocational training and skill-development program for workers in ecotourism and the banana sector.  
• Unemployment is defined as the percentage of the labor force not employed and actively seeking work; reducing it supports higher output and economic growth.  
• Economic growth refers to an increase in real GDP, which can be supported by skilled labor producing more goods and services, particularly in the expanding eco-friendly tourism market.  
• In the Keynesian framework, government spending on training programs acts as an injection into the economy, generating a multiplier effect that increases aggregate demand and real output.  
• Improving skills in ecotourism helps match labor supply with the growing demand for specialized services, shifting the long-run aggregate supply (LRAS) curve rightward, supporting resilience by diversifying the economic base.  
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/8d499fd7-327b-4f3b-a785-19132669965d)
• Enhanced skills in climate-resilient banana farming can increase agricultural productivity, sustain exports, and stabilize farming incomes, reducing vulnerability to global price fluctuations.  
• A better-trained workforce can raise international competitiveness in both tourism and agriculture, supporting stable export revenue and increasing real GDP in line with data showing banana exports and tourism as core sectors.  
• Income gains from new jobs and higher productivity can improve tax revenue without raising tax rates, allowing further investment in infrastructure and social safety nets to address moderate inequality.  
• Long-run benefits involve reduced structural unemployment, higher output from more skilled labor, and decreased dependence on narrow industries vulnerable to natural disasters.  
• Opportunity costs exist if government funds are diverted from other projects, but the resulting increase in productivity and resilience can offset these costs, enhancing Dominica’s ability to recover from external shocks and maintain stable growth.

questions.specification:
## Economic and Social Developments in Malta

Malta is a small island nation located in the central Mediterranean Sea, often admired for its strategic position and historical heritage. Over the last decade, the country’s economy has expanded steadily, with average annual real GDP growth of approximately 4%. Tourism remains one of the main pillars of the Maltese economy, contributing around 15% of GDP in 2022. Related service industries such as accommodation, restaurants, and cultural events have seen consistent investment. However, this reliance on tourism has also made Malta vulnerable to external shocks, most notably global downturns in travel.

Malta adopted the euro in 2008. Because the country does not control its own monetary policy, it relies significantly on fiscal measures to manage economic fluctuations. Moderately low unemployment, averaging 4.0% in 2021, contrasts with pockets of underemployment in regions that have not fully embraced growing sectors like information technology and financial services. Meanwhile, youth unemployment in these areas marginally exceeds 10%. Inflation has ticked upward to 2.5%, fueled by rising housing costs and energy prices. The government has introduced energy subsidies aimed at mitigating the impact of higher utility bills, though debate continues on whether this policy disproportionately benefits higher-income groups.

Poverty rates in Malta stand at about 14% of the population (using a relative poverty line), with persistent challenges linked to insufficient social housing and limited access to higher education for low-income families. Government data indicate that an estimated 8% of the population faces food insecurity at least once per year, prompting charitable organizations and public agencies to call for more robust social programs. The government has responded with targeted budgeting for health-care subsidies and vocational training. However, several policy analysts argue that a stronger focus on reducing the intergenerational poverty cycle is needed to ensure long-term improvements in living standards.

Internationally, Malta has positioned itself as a hub for financial services and online gaming, benefiting from relatively low corporate taxation policies within the EU framework. Exports are dominated by electronics components and pharmaceuticals, with the European Union as its largest trading partner. Simultaneously, Malta imports most raw materials and energy. This dependence on imports occasionally exposes the economy to supply chain bottlenecks, especially visible during global crises. Foreign direct investment (FDI) inflows have been robust over the last several years, reflecting investor confidence in Malta’s stable political environment and skilled workforce. However, certain smaller local businesses believe they are not seeing the benefits of FDI due to increased property prices and competition in the labor market.

One key dilemma involves balancing environmental sustainability with continued economic growth: as tourism booms, Malta’s coastline and historical sites experience increased congestion and pollution. The government has proposed stricter environmental regulations for hotel expansions and introduced pilot programs for renewable energy to reduce reliance on imported fuel. Critics are concerned that these regulations may slow the approval process for foreign investors in real estate, while supporters argue that preserving Malta’s unique environment is critical for the long-term health of the tourism sector.

In terms of social development, progress has been made in improving health-care quality and technology access in schools. Policymakers hope that narrowing Malta’s digital divide will help the next generation engage in higher-value-added industries, thus reducing vulnerability to persistent poverty cycles. Despite ongoing reforms, Malta’s policymakers must juggle multiple objectives: fostering inclusive economic growth, safeguarding the natural and cultural environment, and ensuring social equity, all while functioning within the constraints of the Eurozone.

**Table 1: Key Macroeconomic Indicators for Malta (2019–2022)**

| Indicator                       | 2019    | 2020    | 2021    | 2022    |
|--------------------------------|---------|---------|---------|---------|
| Nominal GDP (EUR billion)      | 14.0    | 13.2    | 13.8    | 15.0    |
| Real GDP Growth Rate (%)       | 4.3     | -8.0    | 5.9     | 4.4     |
| Unemployment Rate (%)          | 3.7     | 4.4     | 4.1     | 4.0     |
| Inflation Rate (%)             | 1.2     | 0.5     | 1.0     | 2.5     |
| Fiscal Balance (% of GDP)      | -1.3    | -9.7    | -5.6    | -3.4    |
| Tourism Receipts (EUR billion) | 2.1     | 0.8     | 1.3     | 2.0     |

**Table 2: Social and Development Indicators for Malta**

| Indicator                                 | 2019 | 2020 | 2021 | 2022 |
|-------------------------------------------|------|------|------|------|
| Population (thousand)                     | 495  | 501  | 516  | 521  |
| Poverty Rate (% of population)            | 14.5 | 14.3 | 14.2 | 14.0 |
| Gini Coefficient                          | 0.29 | 0.29 | 0.28 | 0.28 |
| FDI Inflows (EUR million)                 | 182  | 90   | 210  | 250  |

parts.content: Using a demand and supply diagram, explain how Malta’s proposed stricter environmental regulations could affect hotel expansion in the tourism market.

- On the demand and supply diagram for hotel development, the supply curve shifts leftwards (S1 to S2) because higher costs reduce the quantity of hotel expansions supplied at each price level.  
- With supply shifting left, the new equilibrium in the hotel market moves to a higher price (or cost) and a lower quantity of expansions.  
- This may slow the growth of the tourism infrastructure, but it could also preserve the environmental assets that sustain future tourism demand.

parts.content: Using information from the text/data in Table 2 and your knowledge of economics, evaluate the impact of Malta’s reliance on tourism on both economic growth and development.
parts.markscheme:
**Definition:**

- Economic growth: An increase in a country’s real output (real GDP) over time.  
- Economic development: Broad-based improvements in standards of living, including health, education, and income distribution.  
- Tourism: A key export-earning service sector that can stimulate aggregate demand, employment, and foreign exchange.

**Range of Arguments:**

1. Tourism as a Driver of Economic Growth  
- Tourism contributed 15% of GDP in 2022, indicating its significance for Malta’s overall output.  
- Rising tourism receipts from EUR 0.8 billion in 2020 to EUR 2.0 billion in 2022 illustrate how the sector can boost growth and employment, as seen by Malta’s low unemployment rate of 4% in 2022.  
- Higher aggregate demand from tourism can stimulate other service industries (restaurants, hotels), promoting positive multiplier effects on real GDP.  
- The boost of growth and development in Malta would cause an outward shift of the AD curve (AD1 to AD2).

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/aaa0ab42-4a1c-40cc-8b4c-9ec7cc2e373e)

- However, tourism reliance can create vulnerability during global crises (as in 2020, with an 8% contraction in real GDP). Sustaining economic expansion solely through tourism is risky when external demand drops.

2. Impact on Development Indicators  
- Tourism revenue can help fund government initiatives in health-care and education, potentially reducing poverty rates (currently around 14%).  
- FDI inflows (rising from EUR 90 million in 2020 to EUR 250 million in 2022) may be partly driven by tourism-related activities, offering job opportunities and skills training.  
- Despite these benefits, certain communities may be excluded from tourism-led growth, illustrated by pockets of higher youth unemployment above 10%.  
- Development requires improving income distribution, yet the poverty rate remains persistent, and some analysts argue that insufficient access to higher education perpetuates generational poverty.

3. Environmental Sustainability and Future Growth  
- Rapid tourism development can strain natural assets and infrastructure, leading to environmental degradation and congestion, jeopardizing Malta’s long-term tourism appeal.  
- Government policies such as stricter environmental regulations and renewable energy projects may safeguard the environment and secure long-term growth.  
- Nevertheless, these policies might slow hotel expansions or discourage some investors concerned about added costs and delays, possibly dampening short-term growth benefits.  
- Long-term development hinges on balancing economic gains from tourism with the sustainability of natural and cultural resources.

4. Diversification and Resilience  
- Overreliance on tourism exposes Malta to external shocks; diversifying into other sectors (e.g., financial services, IT, pharmaceuticals) enhances economic resilience.  
- Data show services already comprise 75% of GDP, suggesting a broad base. However, some local populations feel overshadowed by foreign competition and rising property prices.  
- Further skill development and vocational training can elevate workforce capabilities, but realizing broader development requires tackling underemployment and reducing poverty cycles.  
- Encouraging local entrepreneurship and investing in human capital development can lessen dependence on tourism and foster more balanced growth.

**Conclusion and Evaluation:**

- Tourism provides a strong source of income, foreign exchange, and job creation, supporting Malta’s relatively robust growth and low unemployment.  
- However, relying heavily on tourism makes Malta vulnerable to global demand fluctuations, impacting both economic growth and social well-being.  
- Proper management of the environment and fostering inclusive policies can help translate tourism revenues into improved education, reduced poverty, and sustainable development over time.  
- Overall, while tourism is vital to Malta’s economic success, long-term development requires strategies that mitigate environmental damage, diversify the economy, and address inequality so that benefits extend across all social groups.

questions.specification:
## Economic Developments in Georgia

(1) Georgia, a country situated in the South Caucasus region, has undergone considerable economic transformation since the early 2000s. With a population of around 3.7 million, its strategic location bridging Europe and Asia has encouraged policymakers to pursue an open economy model. Essential to this approach is the Deep and Comprehensive Free Trade Area (DCFTA) agreement with the European Union (EU). Over the last decade, Georgia’s real GDP growth averaged 4.5% annually. In 2022, real GDP growth reached 5.3%, partly driven by a rebound in tourism and rising demand for Georgian wine exports, which are major sources of foreign exchange.

(2) Despite consistent growth, Georgia has faced issues of inflation. Following a surge in global energy and food prices, the National Bank of Georgia adopted a contractionary monetary policy stance in 2021, raising interest rates to curb price increases. These measures appeared to temper inflation, which fell from 13% in 2021 to 7.5% in 2022. However, local firms have complained that higher interest rates constrained their access to credit, slowing investment in crucial sectors like agriculture and small-scale manufacturing.

(3) Agriculture, historically a cornerstone of the Georgian economy, is undergoing modernization through increased government support. The “Growth in Agriculture Initiative” introduced in 2020 provided subsidies for local farmers to invest in irrigation systems and advanced machinery. While the program expanded rural output, critics argue that these subsidies may disproportionately benefit large agribusinesses rather than smallholders. A notable microeconomic concern is the possibility of market distortion if subsidies create dependencies, discouraging resource allocation based on market signals.

(4) Additionally, structural unemployment remains high at 11%. Many young people migrate from rural to urban areas, hoping to find jobs in Tbilisi’s growing service sector. In 2022, the official unemployment rate dropped slightly to 10.2%, but observers suggest that underemployment is prevalent, particularly in rural regions. The government has responded by expanding vocational education programs, with the aim of developing a workforce skilled in IT, construction, and tourism services.

(5) Foreign direct investment (FDI) inflows are essential for Georgia’s growth strategy. In 2019, net FDI stood at US 1.2 billion, but declined to US 0.9 billion in 2020 amid global economic uncertainties. Since 2021, improved investor sentiment and eased pandemic restrictions have begun to reverse this trend, with net FDI reaching US$1.1 billion by mid-2022. However, domestic entrepreneurs claim competition is skewed in favor of multinational corporations, which receive tax incentives the local firms cannot match. The government is reviewing its policies to ensure fair treatment of domestic producers while keeping Georgia attractive to foreign investors.

(6) International trade patterns have also evolved. Apart from the EU, Georgia has trade ties with China, Turkey, and post-Soviet states. Wine, mineral water, and agricultural products account for a large share of Georgia’s exports. Despite new bilateral agreements aimed at lowering tariffs, non-tariff barriers such as stringent product standards still challenge Georgian exporters, particularly smaller producers who lack the finance to meet certification costs. Georgia’s currency, the lari (GEL), floated more freely in recent years, although the central bank maintains foreign reserves to smooth extreme exchange rate fluctuations.

(7) The government’s current fiscal policy involves moderate levels of public spending, mainly directed toward infrastructure projects, including better road networks to facilitate trade. The budget deficit reached 4.8% of GDP in 2021, spurring debate over the sustainability of public debt, which stood at roughly 52% of GDP. Nonetheless, policymakers argue these infrastructure investments will foster long-term economic growth and encourage both domestic and foreign investments in the logistics sector.

(8) Looking ahead, policymakers are focused on balancing economic expansion with social equity and external stability. The Gini coefficient has hovered around 0.37 in recent years, reflecting moderate income inequality. While tourism earnings, estimated at US$1.8 billion in 2022, buoyed Georgia’s external accounts, regional geopolitical tensions pose risks to both investor confidence and exports. In the coming years, the success of Georgia’s developmental agenda will hinge on its ability to foster inclusive growth, maintain price stability, and navigate the complexities of international trade.

**Table 1: Georgia’s Selected Macroeconomic Indicators (2019–2022)**

| Indicator                       | 2019   | 2020   | 2021   | 2022   |
|---------------------------------|--------|--------|--------|--------|
| Nominal GDP (US$ billion)       | 17.5   | 15.9   | 16.8   | 18.2   |
| Real GDP Growth Rate (%)        | 5.0    | -6.2   | 4.8    | 5.3    |
| Inflation Rate (%)              | 4.9    | 5.2    | 13.0   | 7.5    |
| Budget Deficit (% of GDP)       | -2.4   | -8.7   | -5.1   | -4.8   |
| Unemployment Rate (%)           | 11.6   | 12.3   | 11.0   | 10.2   |
| Exchange Rate (GEL per US$)     | 2.84   | 3.06   | 3.15   | 2.90   |

**Table 2: Development & Trade Indicators for Georgia**

| Indicator                                      | 2019   | 2020   | 2021   | 2022   |
|------------------------------------------------|--------|--------|--------|--------|
| FDI Inflows (US$ billion)                      | 1.20   | 0.90   | 1.05   | 1.10   |
| Tourism Revenues (US$ billion)                 | 2.20   | 1.00   | 1.50   | 1.80   |

parts.content: Sketch an AD/AS diagram to illustrate the potential impact on inflation when real GDP growth outpaces growth in productive capacity, referring to the inflation data in Table 1.
parts.markscheme:
AD/AS diagram to illustrate the potential impact on inflation  
- If real GDP grows faster than productive capacity, the economy may approach or exceed its full employment level of output, leading to demand-pull inflation.  
- In an AD/AS diagram, AD shifts outward more quickly than AS, causing a higher price level.  
- Table 1 data shows inflation peaking at 13.0% in 2021 before moderating to 7.5% in 2022, suggesting that demand pressures contributed to inflation when the economy expanded strongly.

parts.content: Using a demand-and-supply of labor diagram, explain how vocational education programs (paragraph 4) might help reduce structural unemployment in Georgia.
parts.markscheme:
How vocational education programs might reduce structural unemployment  
- Vocational education improves workers’ skills, making their labor more adaptable to the changing structure of the economy (for example, Tbilisi’s growing service sector).  
- The supply of labor qualified for modern or emerging industries shifts right in a labor market diagram, decreasing the equilibrium level of unemployment.  
- Structural unemployment decreases because workers gain skills relevant to changing labor demand (e.g., IT, tourism).  
- As illustrated by the rightward shift of the supply curve of skilled labor, the economy fills industry-specific job vacancies more efficiently, reducing structural unemployment in the long run.

parts.content: Using a supply-and-demand of currency (foreign exchange) diagram, explain how improvements in tourism revenues (paragraph 8) could affect the value of the Georgian lari.
parts.markscheme:
How improvements in tourism revenues could affect the Georgian lari  
- Rising tourism revenues increase foreign demand for the lari because tourists need local currency for goods and services.  
- In a currency market diagram, the demand curve for the lari shifts right, causing the lari to appreciate if supply remains constant.  
- An appreciation might strengthen external accounts but can also make exports relatively more expensive.  
- Tourism revenues of US$1.8 billion in 2022 (Table 2) signify a key foreign exchange earner that can bolster the lari’s value.

parts.content: Using a product market diagram (microeconomics), explain how government subsidies in the agriculture sector (paragraph 3) might lead to market distortions.
parts.markscheme:
How government subsidies in agriculture might lead to market distortions  
- Subsidies reduce production costs for farmers, shifting the supply curve to the right and lowering price in the agricultural market.  
- Cheaper output may encourage overproduction if producers rely on artificial cost reductions rather than market fundamentals.  
- This can lead to inefficiency if resources are misallocated, potentially disadvantaging smaller farmers who cannot compete on scale even with subsidies.  
- Over time, dependence on subsidies may disincentivize efficiency gains and distort market signals.

parts.content: Using a business cycle diagram, explain how the sharp decline in real GDP growth in 2020 (Table 1) could have influenced cyclical unemployment.
parts.markscheme:
How the sharp decline in real GDP growth in 2020 could have influenced cyclical unemployment  
- The drop in real GDP growth from 5.0% in 2019 to -6.2% in 2020 (Table 1) suggests a recessionary phase in the business cycle.  
- During a recession, firms cut back production, decreasing the demand for labor and causing cyclical unemployment to rise.  
- In a business cycle diagram, this downturn would be represented by a movement below the potential output level.  
- By 2022, the recovery (with real GDP growth at 5.3%) likely helped reduce cyclical unemployment as economic conditions improved.

parts.content: Using information from the text/data (including Table 2) and your knowledge of economics, evaluate Georgia’s development strategy of combining open trade policies, moderate subsidies, and infrastructure spending.
parts.markscheme:
**Definition:**

- Development strategy: a set of policies to improve living standards, increase economic growth, and reduce poverty.  
- Open trade policies: policies that encourage free flow of goods and services internationally, lowering tariff and non-tariff barriers.  
- Infrastructure spending: government investment in transport, energy, and communication networks to support economic activity.  

**Range of Arguments:**

Arguments in Favor of Georgia’s Development Strategy  
- Open trade policies have bolstered export growth (e.g., wine and mineral water) and attracted FDI, contributing to an average real GDP growth of 4.5% in the last decade and an FDI rebound to US$1.1 billion by mid-2022 (Table 2).  
- The DCFTA with the EU expands market access for Georgia’s exports, potentially spurring higher export revenues and diversification of trade.  
- Moderate subsidies in agriculture can spur modernization, improve productivity (paragraph 3), and ensure food security. This is evidenced by rising agricultural output and increased subsidies from US 180 million to US$245 million (Table 2).  
- Infrastructure spending supports long-term growth through better transport networks, potentially attracting additional FDI and facilitating trade expansion. The budget deficit remains at 4.8% of GDP in 2022, suggesting manageable, if slightly elevated, government borrowing.

Arguments Against or Limitations  
- Open trade policies can expose local producers to intense foreign competition, and some domestic entrepreneurs feel competition is skewed in favor of multinational corporations (paragraph 5).  
- Agricultural subsidies may create market distortions, leading to inefficiency if overproduction is encouraged and smaller farmers are marginalized.  
- Infrastructure spending raises concerns over public debt sustainability (about 52% of GDP). Higher future debt servicing could crowd out other essential government expenditure.  
- Despite strong growth, income inequality persists (Gini coefficient around 0.37), indicating that benefits are not evenly distributed. Rural underemployment remains, highlighting structural issues (paragraph 4).  
- External risks, such as regional geopolitical tensions, might disturb investor confidence, making reliance on open trade and FDI strategies uncertain.

**Evaluation and Conclusion:**

- The strategy helps sustain economic growth, evident by robust real GDP increases and rising tourism revenues. These factors strengthen external accounts and draw in foreign capital.  
- Balancing social equity alongside open market reforms remains a challenge, as shown by persistent unemployment and inequality due to labor market rigidities.  
- The success of this development model depends on continued infrastructure improvements, policies ensuring fair competition and inclusive benefits from subsidies, and structural reforms (e.g., enhanced vocational training).  
- In the long term, diversifying trade partners, maintaining macroeconomic stability, and effectively managing public debt are crucial to ensure sustained, inclusive growth.

questions.specification:
Ghana’s economy has experienced relatively high levels of economic growth over the past decade, largely driven by the export of primary commodities, such as cocoa and gold, and an expanding services sector. Ghana is one of the world’s largest exporters of cocoa, and cocoa alone contributes a significant share of the country’s export earnings. Despite these gains, Ghana has faced macroeconomic challenges in recent years, including rising inflation and fiscal deficits.

The government has attempted to diversify the economy away from its reliance on primary commodities, investing in infrastructure and seeking to encourage more value-added activities to boost productivity and employment. However, issues such as income inequality and limited access to finance in rural areas persist and may limit the potential for inclusive growth.

Below are some data tables about Ghana’s economy.

Table 1: Selected Macroeconomic Indicators for Ghana (2022)

| Indicator                                              | Value                |
|--------------------------------------------------------|----------------------|
| Nominal GDP (US$ billions)                             | 74                   |
| Real GDP growth rate (%)                               | 3.7                  |
| Inflation rate (%) (year-on-year, end of period)       | 31                   |
| Government expenditure (GH¢ billions)                  | 103                  |
| Population (millions)                                  | 31.4                 |
| Gini coefficient                                       | 0.43                 |

Table 2: Cocoa Market Data (2022 estimates)

| Item                       | Value                |
|----------------------------|----------------------|
| Domestic demand for cocoa (tonnes)          | 70 000              |
| Domestic cocoa price (GH¢ per tonne)        | 10 000              |
| World cocoa price (GH¢ per tonne)           | 11 000              |
| Quantity exported (tonnes)                  | 800 000             |
| Estimated price elasticity of demand (PED)  | –0.5                |
| Estimated price elasticity of supply (PES)  | +0.3                |

Table 3: Income Tax Rates in Ghana

| Tax Rate Bracket            | Rate of Tax |
|-----------------------------|-------------|
| 0 – GH¢ 3 828/year         | 0 %         |
| GH¢ 3 828 – GH¢ 20 000/year | 15 %        |
| GH¢ 20 000 – GH¢ 40 000/year | 25 %        |
| Above GH¢ 40 000/year       | 30 %        |

Additional information:
• The marginal propensity to consume (MPC) in Ghana is estimated at 0.8.  
• Government tax revenue from cocoa exports is calculated as 10 % of the value of cocoa exported.  
• The government aims to reduce inflation by tightening monetary policy and improving the efficiency of its tax collection system.
