parts.content: Using the information in Table 2, calculate the total tax revenue (in Ghana cedis) the government earns from cocoa exports.
parts.markscheme:
• Total value of cocoa exported = 800 000 tonnes × GH¢ 11 000 = GH¢ 8 800 000 000  
• Tax revenue = 10% × GH¢ 8 800 000 000 = GH¢ 880 000 000

parts.content: Assume that the government injects an additional GH¢ 1 billion into infrastructure spending. Given that the marginal propensity to consume (MPC) in Ghana is 0.8, calculate the total change in real GDP resulting from this additional spending using the Keynesian multiplier formula.
Using the information in Table 1, calculate Ghana’s approximate GDP per capita in US dollars. Show your working.

From Table 2, using the estimated PES (+0.3), calculate the percentage change in quantity supplied of cocoa if its price rises from GH¢ 10 000 per tonne to GH¢ 11 000 per tonne in the domestic market.
parts.markscheme:
• Percentage change in price = (11 000 – 10 000) ÷ 10 000 × 100% = 10%  
• Percentage change in quantity supplied = 0.3 × 10% = 3%

parts.content: Define the term “income inequality.”
parts.markscheme: • Income inequality is the uneven distribution of income among individuals or households within an economy.

parts.content: Using an AD/AS diagram, explain how tightening monetary policy could help reduce inflation in Ghana.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/c9323df4-9db3-4a30-a780-61e95e804b46)
• Tighter monetary policy raises interest rates, which reduces borrowing and spending in the economy, shifting the aggregate demand curve left.  
• Lower aggregate demand suppresses upward pressure on the general price level, helping to control inflation.  
• In the AD/AS diagram, this appears as a leftward shift of AD from AD1 to AD2, reducing the price level from PL1 to PL2 and lowering real output from Y1 to Y2.  
• This decrease in demand-pull pressure can help stabilize prices and contain high inflation.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks>

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using information from Table 2, calculate the total revenue (in GH¢) from domestic cocoa sales. Show your working.
parts.markscheme:
- Domestic demand for cocoa: 70,000 tonnes
- Domestic cocoa price: GH¢ 10,000 per tonne
- Total revenue = Price × Quantity = 10,000 × 70,000
- Total revenue = GH¢ 700,000,000 (or GH¢ 700 million)

parts.content: With reference to the data in Table 1 and Table 3, explain two ways in which income tax policy could influence income distribution in Ghana.
parts.markscheme:
• A progressive tax structure, indicated by rising rates (15%, 25%, 30%) for higher brackets, could reduce income inequality by transferring a larger share of wealth from higher-income groups to the government, which could fund social programs.  
• Low or zero tax for lower-income earners (0% for up to GH¢ 3 828) helps maintain disposable income for the poorest, potentially reducing the Gini coefficient (0.43) through more equitable income distribution.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and your knowledge of economics, recommend a policy that could be implemented by the government of Ghana to promote inclusive growth (i.e., reduce income inequality while sustaining economic growth). Justify your recommendation.
parts.markscheme:
• Inclusive growth means economic expansion that provides opportunities and benefits across all socio-economic groups.  
• Income inequality refers to the uneven distribution of income, measured here by a Gini coefficient of 0.43, indicating moderate inequality.  
• Economic growth is the increase in real GDP, here achieved mainly through cocoa exports, gold, and a growing services sector.  
• A policy promoting vocational training and education subsidies in rural areas can reduce income inequality by enhancing skills and productivity, especially since limited access to finance and infrastructure constrains many rural communities.  
• This policy can potentially boost long-term aggregate supply (shifting LRAS right), sustaining growth without large inflationary pressures if managed carefully with ongoing investments in infrastructure.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/97a75aa6-7713-45b6-ae2c-e4cf5fcd5206)
• Financing for this policy can be drawn from improved tax collection and the government’s attempt to diversify the economy, as indicated by its focus on value-added activities and more efficient tax receipts.  
• Data on fiscal deficits (with government expenditure at GH¢ 103 billion) suggests a need for careful budgeting; however, spending on human capital is likely to yield returns by raising productivity and reducing reliance on volatile primary commodity exports.  
• Higher-skilled labor improves competitiveness in value-added sectors, enhancing exported services and products beyond raw cocoa or gold, thus supporting longer-term growth.  
• More equal opportunities reduce the gap between urban and rural incomes, improving inclusive growth and addressing persistent inequality, as reflected by the Gini coefficient.  
• This strategically balances the government’s goals: raising living standards, reducing inflationary pressures through a stronger productive base, and fostering a more equitable distribution of income.

questions.specification:
## Economic Challenges and Policies in the Maldives

The Maldives, an archipelagic nation of approximately 540,000 inhabitants, has long relied on tourism as the primary driver of economic growth, with tourism-related activities accounting for nearly 28% of GDP in 2022. However, growing vulnerabilities, including climate change impacts and limited economic diversification, have compelled successive governments to pursue wide-reaching reforms.

Over the last five years, real GDP growth has varied significantly. In 2019, the Maldives recorded a growth rate of 5.8%, buoyed by robust tourist arrivals from Europe, Asia, and the Middle East. By 2020, global economic disruptions caused by pandemics and recessions in major tourist markets led to a contraction of 17%. A modest rebound in 2021 and 2022, averaging 6.2% annually, has been supported by targeted marketing campaigns to attract foreign tourists. Yet, inflation historically in the range of 2–4% briefly spiked to 6% in 2022, largely due to higher global energy and food prices.

Even with the rebound in GDP, nearly 15% of the population lives below the national poverty line. Among those, the rural communities scattered across outer islands face the highest vulnerabilities. A severe shortage of skilled labor in the private sector is often filled by expatriates, partly due to insufficient local vocational training. Although unemployment stands at around 7%, youth unemployment is estimated at 18%. To address rising poverty, the government in 2021 increased its reliance on official development assistance (ODA) and began exploring bilateral loan agreements. Critics argue that escalating external debt now around 78% of GDP could threaten long-term fiscal stability if not managed prudently.

Tourism receipts are the key source of foreign exchange, and the Maldivian rufiyaa is managed with periodic central bank interventions. Fluctuations in tourist arrival numbers particularly from Europe can lead to sudden shifts in the current account balance. Additionally, the government is promoting trade liberalization in other sectors such as fishing and logistics to reduce reliance on tourism. However, domestic firms express concerns about inadequate infrastructure and high import taxes on capital goods, which they claim hinder productivity gains.

Given the low-lying geography of the Maldives, environmental challenges are especially pressing. Rising sea levels threaten vital infrastructure, and overfishing may undermine the second-largest economic sector: fisheries. Although environmental taxes have been introduced to discourage pollution in fragile coastal areas, local businesses complain about high compliance costs. Some economists argue that better-defined property rights could help reduce negative externalities on coral reefs, but political disagreements have stalled legislative reforms.

To stimulate inclusive growth, recent budgets have increased spending on education and health, aiming to raise the human capital stock. The Ministry of Economic Development has also launched targeted subsidy programs for small island entrepreneurs, focusing on agribusiness and eco-tourism. While these subsidies are intended to break the cycle of poverty by enabling local communities to invest in skill development, critics worry about misallocation of funds in politically favored regions.

Official data indicate that progress on key development goals is mixed. Life expectancy has risen to 79 years, and the infant mortality rate has fallen, indicating improvements in health-care. Nonetheless, inequality persists, as measured by a national Gini coefficient of 0.38. Policy debates revolve around whether more market-oriented solutions such as lowering import tariffs on capital equipment or greater government intervention is the best path to achieve both economic efficiency and equity.

Moving forward, the Maldivian government acknowledges that economic resilience hinges on diversification and sustainable investment. International organizations emphasize capacity building, cautioning that high debt levels limit the fiscal space for large-scale infrastructure projects. Policymakers must balance the immediate priorities of poverty reduction, climate adaptation, and robust macroeconomic management to ensure the Maldives’ long-term development.

**Table 1: Selected Macroeconomic Indicators, Maldives (2019–2023)**

| Indicator                       | 2019  | 2020   | 2021  | 2022  | 2023* |
|--------------------------------|-------|--------|-------|-------|-------|
| Real GDP Growth Rate (%)       | 5.8   | -17.0  | 5.9   | 6.5   | 5.2   |
| Inflation Rate (%)             | 2.2   | 0.3    | 3.1   | 6.0   | 4.5   |
| Tourism Arrivals (millions)    | 1.70  | 0.61   | 1.03  | 1.30  | 1.45  |
| Current Account Balance (%GDP) | -15.0 | -10.3  | -15.7 | -11.0 | -10.5 |
| Unemployment Rate (%)          | 6.0   | 10.2   | 8.5   | 7.5   | 7.0   |
| External Debt (% of GDP)       | 72    | 76     | 80    | 78    | 77    |

*Forecast

**Table 2: Development and Social Indicators, Maldives**

| Indicator                                         | 2019   | 2021   | 2023*  |
|---------------------------------------------------|--------|--------|--------|
| Population (thousands)                            | 515    | 530    | 540    |
| Poverty Rate (% of population below nat. poverty) | 14.0   | 15.2   | 15.0   |
| Gini Coefficient                                  | 0.39   | 0.38   | 0.38   |
| Official Development Assistance (US$ million)     | 58     | 110    | 120    |
| Average Years of Schooling                        | 7.5    | 7.8    | 7.9    |
| Youth Unemployment Rate (%)                       | 16.0   | 18.0   | 18.0   |

*Forecast

-  High youth unemployment compounds this problem, as fewer young people gain job experience and income for reinvestment in education or entrepreneurial activities. 
- This creates a cycle in which poverty persists across generations, since lower income leads to insufficient education and health outcomes, which in turn limits productivity and future earning potential.

- Developers and tourism operators do not bear the full social cost of the damage, so the quantity of such development exceeds the socially optimal level.

Over time, as the quality of human capital improves, the long-run aggregate supply (LRAS) may also shift outward due to a more skilled and healthier workforce. 
- This can increase real GDP, reduce unemployment, and promote long-term economic growth, provided there is capacity to absorb the additional spending without creating excessive inflationary pressures.

parts.content: Using information from the text/data (particularly Table 2) and your knowledge of economics, evaluate the effectiveness of the Maldives’ reliance on official development assistance and targeted subsidies to address poverty and foster sustainable growth.
parts.markscheme:
**Definition:**  

  - Official development assistance: Concessional funds from advanced economies or multilateral institutions to promote economic development in recipient countries.  
  - Subsidies: Government payments to reduce producer costs or encourage particular economic activities.  

**Range of Arguments:**

- Arguments in support of ODA and subsidies  
  - Increase in ODA from US 58 million in 2019 to US$120 million in 2023 (Table 2) provides funding for poverty-reduction programs, infrastructure, and human capital development. This can help reduce immediate income disparities, particularly as 15% of the population remains below the national poverty line.  
  - Targeted subsidies in agribusiness or eco-tourism can create employment opportunities in outer islands with higher vulnerabilities. These subsidies may catalyze investment and stimulate local entrepreneurship, potentially breaking poverty cycles if directed effectively.  
  - Youth unemployment, at 18%, could be mitigated by subsidy programs that encourage skill development and training in growing sectors such as sustainable tourism or fisheries. This could improve the long-term employability of young people.  
  - ODA can lower the government’s immediate fiscal burden, allowing for increased public spending on health-care, education, and infrastructure without overwhelming domestic resources. This supports sustainable growth by enhancing human capital stocks and providing a foundation for diversification. 
  - Thus this can potentially shift the aggregate deman curve (AD) outwards (AD1 to AD2).

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/8967343f-f575-4a62-9935-a179abd31c95)

- Limitations of ODA and subsidies  
  - High external debt at 78% of GDP raises concern about future debt repayment burdens. If ODA comes in the form of loans rather than grants, there could be long-term fiscal risks.  
  - Reliance on subsidies can create market distortions if they are not well-targeted. Politically favored regions might benefit disproportionately, leading to inefficiency in resource allocation and potential corruption.  
  - ODA and subsidies may reduce the incentive for the private sector to develop independently if investors expect continuous government support. Over-reliance on external funding can limit the drive for self-sustaining growth or innovation.  
  - Poverty reduction might be uneven across regions if rural and remote islands are not the focal point of subsidy allocation or if skills training remains insufficiently available to younger populations.  

**Evaluation and Conclusion:**

  - ODA and subsidies can provide much-needed short-term relief and facilitate investments in human capital, especially with recent data indicating that poverty has remained around 15%. These measures are likely to help vulnerable communities if carefully monitored to avoid misallocation of funds.  
  - Over the long run, prudent management of external debt and strategic diversification are essential. Gradual reduction in reliance on subsidized programs would motivate domestic industries to become more competitive, mitigating any dependency problems.  
  - Strengthening governance structures for subsidy distribution and ensuring ODA is used efficiently for climate adaptation, education, and health-care can improve equity and stimulate growth.  
  - Balancing the benefits of ODA and targeted subsidies with the risk of rising debt and potential distortions suggests that reliance on external aid and subsidies is only part of the solution. 
  - A broader strategy focusing on economic diversification, human capital improvement, and environmental sustainability is crucial for lasting poverty reduction and sustainable development in the Maldives.

questions.specification:
## Lebanon’s Economic Crisis and Reform

Lebanon, a small country on the eastern coast of the Mediterranean, has been grappling with a severe economic crisis since 2019. The country’s public debt has reached alarming levels, exceeding 170% of GDP in 2021, and the Lebanese pound (LBP) has suffered a drastic depreciation against major currencies. Between 2018 and 2021, the currency lost over 80% of its official value, triggering triple-digit inflation. In the same period, official inflation rates soared from 9% to over 120%, while real GDP contracted sharply due to a collapse in consumer and investor confidence. As a result, the World Bank has characterized Lebanon’s recent financial crisis as one of the worst globally in over a century.

Although Lebanon has traditionally depended on tourism, banking services, and remittances from its large diaspora to drive economic growth, the country’s precarious situation has disrupted these revenue streams. Businesses are struggling to access foreign currency to import raw materials, leading to shortages in essential goods. The government, facing a persistent fiscal deficit, reduced fuel subsidies in 2021 to contain budgetary pressures, although it continues to provide partial subsidies on bread and basic medicines. Meanwhile, discussions with the International Monetary Fund (IMF) have focused on stabilizing the currency, restructuring the banking sector, and improving public finances.

Social conditions have deteriorated markedly: estimates suggest that over 50% of the population was living below the national poverty line in 2021, up from 28% in 2018. Youth unemployment exceeded 35%, and entire segments of the population have fallen into a poverty trap, where low incomes restrict access to education and healthcare, reducing the workforce’s productivity in the long run. NGOs and international donors have stepped in to provide short-term relief, yet many families rely heavily on remittances from relatives abroad to meet basic needs. These remittance inflows are increasingly critical for stabilizing household consumption and shoring up the supply of foreign exchange.

Lebanese policymakers also face structural issues in the electricity sector. State-owned power generation fails to meet demand, causing daily blackouts. Households resort to private diesel generators, which remain costly. Although the government has pledged to reform the power sector and reduce reliance on imported fuel, implementation has been slow due to political fragmentation and limited fiscal space. International investors often demand clearer regulations and macroeconomic stability before committing to large-scale projects in Lebanon’s energy infrastructure.

On the global front, Lebanon’s trade relations remain complicated. Historically, it has depended on imported food, machinery, and refined oil, while exporting agricultural products, jewellery, and some manufactured goods. However, with currency depreciation, import costs have surged. The trade deficit narrowed in nominal terms, but not necessarily due to increased exports—rather, imports have plunged because of foreign currency shortages. With about 15% of the labor force employed in agriculture, local producers see an opportunity to expand export markets if the currency remains depreciated. Yet high costs of capital and inconsistent electricity supplies hamper productivity gains.

In an effort to address widening income inequality, the government has proposed mechanisms to target subsidies toward the poorest households. There is also an ongoing discussion about raising taxes on luxury goods and engaging in long-term structural reforms. However, political instability continues to undermine consistent policy application. Without reliable governance, foreign investment remains subdued, and local entrepreneurs face an uncertain business environment.

Despite these challenges, there are glimmers of hope. The United Nations has partnered with local organizations to promote vocational training, aiming to break the poverty cycle by enhancing the skill set of Lebanon’s youth. Moreover, diaspora investors occasionally launch entrepreneurial ventures in the tech and creative sectors, leveraging Lebanon’s human capital and cultural heritage. Whether these initiatives can offset the broader macroeconomic imbalances, however, depends on the government’s ability to implement robust reforms, attract sustainable financing, and negotiate favorable terms with international creditors.

parts.content: Using a demand and supply of currency diagram, explain how increasing remittance inflows might affect the exchange rate of the Lebanese pound (LBP).
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/fd084406-3104-4946-a95a-fc3e3c048ea4)

- Remittances represent an inward flow of foreign currency, increasing the supply of foreign exchange in Lebanon. Increase is Supply of foreign currency is the same as increase in Demand for Lebanese Pound. 
- Rightward shift of demand for LBP, from D1 to D2, potentially appreciates the LBP.
- If the demand for LBP rises (or if the supply of foreign currency increases), the LBP’s exchange rate may strengthen, increasing from e1 to e2, reducing further depreciation pressures.
- This can help stabilize Lebanon’s currency, given strong reliance on remittance inflows.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using an AD/AS diagram, explain how partial removal of fuel subsidies (paragraph 3) could impact short-run economic growth in Lebanon.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/4580b489-a1fd-4bf7-b992-2de6473ece03)

-  Removing or reducing fuel subsidies raises energy costs to firms, increasing short-run costs of production.
- This shifts the short-run aggregate supply (SRAS) curve leftward (SRAS₁ to SRAS₂), causing a possible rise in the price level (PL₁ to PL₂) and a contraction of real output in the short run (Y₁ to Y₂).
- Aggregate demand may also adjust slightly if households face higher transportation or heating costs, reducing consumption.
- Short-run economic growth is likely to slow, as firms and consumers face higher costs and inflationary pressures (movement from E₁ to E₂).

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 
<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

- A Lorenz curve that bows further away from the line of equality shows a larger share of income going to higher-income groups.

parts.content: Using information from the text/data and your knowledge of economics, discuss the impact of Lebanon’s partial subsidy removals on its path to economic stability and long-term development.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include 

**Definition:**

- Subsidy: A payment by the government to reduce the costs of production or consumption, often aimed at lowering prices for consumers or encouraging production.  
- Economic stability: A state of steady growth, low inflation, and manageable unemployment without volatility.  
- Economic development: A qualitative measure of a nation’s standard of living, encompassing income distribution, education, health, and overall well-being.

**Range of Arguments:**

- Short-run price and inflation effects  
  - Reducing subsidies on fuel can shift SRAS to the left as production costs rise. This might lead to cost-push inflation, further hurting households already under financial stress.  
  - With inflation already at 120% in 2021, partial subsidy removals could cause additional upward pressure on prices, risking social discontent and reducing real incomes.  

- Fiscal position and government debt  
  - Cutting subsidies is a way to reduce public expenditure and address a persistent fiscal deficit (debt at 173% of GDP in 2021).  
  - Lower government expenditure might help improve the budget balance, potentially satisfying IMF requirements for an economic recovery program and encouraging international financial support.  

- Incentives and efficiency in the energy market  
  - Removing subsidies on fuel can encourage more efficient energy use and investment in alternative energy sources. Improved resource allocation may spur innovation in areas less dependent on fuel imports.  
  - However, high setup costs and political fragmentation could delay investments in alternative energy, leaving consumers bearing higher costs in the short run.  

- Effects on income distribution and poverty  
  - With over 50% of the population living below the poverty line, higher fuel costs may disproportionately affect lower-income groups.  
  - The government’s targeting of subsidies toward essential items like bread and medicines can partially offset the negative effects on the most vulnerable, though incomplete coverage could still exacerbate inequality.  

- Implications for macroeconomic stability  
  - Cutting subsidies can signal a commitment to reform, potentially boosting foreign investor confidence if accompanied by broader banking and fiscal reforms.  
  - The move may help stabilize the exchange rate over time by reducing demand for foreign currency to finance subsidies, though immediate external imbalances persist due to low investor confidence.  

- Long-term development considerations  
  - Better allocation of public funds could free resources for infrastructure, health, and education, leading to stronger human capital development and improved productivity.  
  - Slow implementation of reforms risks undermining long-term growth if political instability continues and if social unrest rises due to high unemployment and reduced real incomes.
  - Long term development is illustrated as a rightward shift of LRAS curve, from LRAS1 to LRAS2, as country's economic capacity and potential output will increase, opening way for new industries. 
  
 ![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/6d30ba2a-8e15-4c47-8d5c-2fe0367ffcab)

**Conclusion:**

- Partial subsidy removals can help address fiscal imbalances and support IMF-backed reform programs, which might improve Lebanon’s credibility and attract investment.  
- While cost-push inflation and potential social unrest in the short term may slow development, improved efficiency and targeted subsidies could promote more sustainable growth if complemented by structural reforms, greater government accountability, and political stability.  
- Over the long term, channeling public resources into productive investments rather than blanket subsidies can support human capital development, fostering economic stability and inclusive growth.

questions.specification:
Greece is a country in southeastern Europe that has faced significant economic challenges over the past decade. Despite recent signs of recovery, with the economy expanding in 2022 after a prolonged recession, Greece continues to experience relatively high unemployment and concerns about income inequality. Tourism remains a major contributor to GDP, accounting for around 25% of economic activity, while shipping generates about 21% of the country’s export revenue. Another prominent export is olive oil, widely recognized for its quality in the global market.

The Greek government imposes a progressive income tax system, with tax rates rising at higher income brackets. While attempts have been made to strengthen tax collection processes, tax evasion and inefficiencies still pose challenges. Moreover, consumption is subject to a 24% value-added tax (VAT) on most goods and services, although some essential items enjoy reduced rates.

In 2022, Greece’s real GDP growth rate reached 5.9%, supported by a strong rebound in tourism and improving global economic conditions. Nonetheless, the unemployment rate remains elevated at 12.9%. Income distribution, measured by a Gini coefficient of approximately 0.33, suggests moderate inequality, prompting policymakers to consider fiscal and structural measures to reduce disparities. The data below provide insights into the country’s economic performance and market structures.

Table 1: Greek Macroeconomic Indicators (2018–2022)

| Year | Real GDP (billion €) | Nominal GDP (billion €) | Unemployment Rate (%) | Real GDP Growth Rate (%) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 2018 | 173 | 181 | 19.3 | 1.9 |
| 2019 | 176 | 185 | 17.3 | 1.7 |
| 2020 | 165 | 168 | 16.4 | -6.3 |
| 2021 | 172 | 180 | 14.7 | 4.2 |
| 2022 | 182 | 193 | 12.9 | 5.9 |

parts.content: Using data from Table 2, calculate the price elasticity of supply (PES) for Greek olive oil when the price increases from €4.50 to €5.50 per litre. Show your working.
parts.markscheme:
Percentage change in quantity supplied: [(105 - 100) ÷ 100] × 100 = 5%
Percentage change in price: [(5.50 - 4.50) ÷ 4.50] × 100 = 22.22%
PES = 5 ÷ 22.22 = 0.225 (or 0.23)

parts.content: With reference to Figure 1, explain how the Keynesian multiplier might affect Greece’s real GDP following an increase in government expenditure on infrastructure.
parts.markscheme:
• An initial injection of government spending on infrastructure generates additional rounds of spending by firms and households, multiplying the initial effect on aggregate demand.  
• Figure 1 shows a multiplier between 1.2 and 1.4, indicating that each euro spent leads to a greater overall increase in national income.  
• As people earn more from the spending, they consume more, creating further demand for goods and services.  
• This cycle results in a final increase in real GDP greater than the original government injection.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and your knowledge of economics, recommend a policy that the Greek government could implement to reduce the relatively high unemployment rate. Justify your recommendation.
parts.markscheme:
• Unemployment can be defined as the portion of the labor force that is actively seeking work but unable to find a job.  
• A suitable policy to reduce unemployment is an expansionary fiscal policy that focuses on infrastructure projects and targeted subsidies for industries such as tourism and shipping.  
• Higher government spending on infrastructure can shift aggregate demand to the right, as indicated by the data showing strong multiplier effects (Figure 1), enabling a larger final increase in real GDP.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/095ef7bd-b9b9-4c78-8476-444c948f2d7d)
• Funding improvements in tourism facilities and port infrastructure builds on the 25% GDP share from tourism and 21% export revenue from shipping, creating new jobs in construction, services, and complementary sectors.  
• Improved infrastructure is likely to attract more visitors, raising demand for labor in tourism-related businesses, helping to reduce the 12.9% unemployment rate reported in 2022.  
• The policy targets Greece’s high unemployment by directly creating construction and service jobs, leading to more consumption and investment, supported by the moderate Gini coefficient (0.33), which indicates room for spending to lower inequalities.  
• There is a risk of higher budget deficits and potential inefficiencies, but stronger tax collection and a progressive taxation system help finance these projects, countering the concern about growing public debt.  
• Some structural reforms in labor markets could accompany fiscal expansion, reducing labor inefficiencies and tax evasion issues noted in the text, maximizing the employment impact.  
• The anticipated result is a virtuous cycle of job creation, higher household incomes, and additional tax revenue that can fund further development, addressing concerns of persistent joblessness.  
• Overall, an expansionary fiscal policy directed toward improving tourism and shipping infrastructure, coupled with efficiency improvements in tax collection, reduces unemployment while leveraging Greece’s core economic sectors.

questions.specification:
## Panama’s Economic Strategies
 
Panama, located in Central America, has long been recognized for its strategic geographic position and the economic benefits generated by the Panama Canal. In 2022, the country’s real gross domestic product (GDP) grew by 3.8%, a slower pace than the 5.1% seen in 2021. A recent rise in inflation to 3.2% (up from 1.6% in the previous year) has prompted concerns about the sustainability of economic growth and the adoption of additional demand management policies.

The Panama Canal still accounts for a substantial share of government revenue and foreign exchange earnings, with government data estimating that canal-related activities contribute about 40% of Panama’s export service revenue. Shipping tolls collected in balboas (PAB) and in US dollars are a major part of this revenue. However, logistical bottlenecks and growing competition from alternative shipping routes have led policymakers to consider ways to diversify the economy. In particular, the tourism sector which employed approximately 205 000 workers in 2022 has been highlighted for potential expansion.

To balance growth and inflationary pressures, the central bank has gradually raised interest rates in an effort to manage aggregate demand. Despite this monetary tightening, Panama’s service sector continues to grow, helped by foreign companies setting up regional headquarters in the country. Additionally, the government has introduced subsidies for agricultural inputs such as fertilizers to protect domestic producers from recently rising import costs. Some environmental groups suggest that these agricultural subsidies may exacerbate negative externalities by encouraging excessive use of fertilizers in ecologically sensitive areas near the Panama Canal Basin.

The government hopes that greater export diversification will come from new trade agreements, particularly those formulated with emerging markets in Asia. Recently negotiated treaties aim to reduce tariffs on competitive exports like bananas and specialty coffee, while providing cheaper imports of technology and manufactured goods. However, small-scale producers worry that free trade might expose local agriculture to fierce competition, highlighting the importance of supply-side policies that strengthen competitiveness.

A separate concern is the rise in structural unemployment, especially among workers displaced from declining industries, such as certain manufacturing sectors that have faced international competition. Retraining programs and better education initiatives are underway, though the effectiveness of these measures has been questioned. The government is also promoting special economic zones offering tax incentives to foreign investors, hoping to attract more foreign direct investment (FDI) to finance infrastructure and human capital development projects.

Panama’s currency, the balboa (PAB), is pegged to the US dollar, which limits the scope for independent exchange rate policy. This arrangement generally stabilizes trade flows but can also limit the government’s ability to address external shocks. While the canal’s revenues partly mitigate balance-of-payments pressures, policymakers remain attentive to potential shortfalls in other parts of the current account, especially if tourism revenues or merchandise exports fall.

Economic observers have noted mixed signals in the country’s external accounts. Although canal toll revenue has increased by nearly 5% in 2022, the balance of trade in goods has not improved substantially. Panama still relies heavily on imports of equipment and consumer goods. Nevertheless, there are expectations that expansion of the canal’s capacity and improved logistics corridors could boost short-term service exports and attract portfolio investment.

Environmental concerns feature prominently in national debates. Some critics argue that insufficient regulation of tourism and real estate development near the country’s coastlines can result in adverse effects on local communities’ welfare and ecosystems. On the other hand, green infrastructure investments partially funded by foreign direct investment are expected to reduce carbon emissions and create new growth opportunities, especially in renewable energy projects.

**Table 1: Selected Macroeconomic Indicators for Panama**  

| Year | Real GDP (PAB billions) | Unemployment Rate (%) | Inflation Rate (%) |  
| --- | --- | --- | --- |  
| 2019 | 66.8 | 4.2 | 1.2 |  
| 2021 | 70.2 | 4.9 | 1.6 |  
| 2022 | 72.9 | 5.0 | 3.2 |  

**Table 2: Panama’s Balance of Payments Highlights (PAB millions)** 

|  | 2019 | 2022 |  
| --- | --- | --- |  
| Balance of trade in goods | -3 100 | -3 200 |  
| Balance of trade in services | 2 300 | 2 550 |  
| Foreign direct investment (FDI) | 3 850 | 4 200 |  
| Portfolio investment | 500 | 780 |  
| Current account balance | -400 | -270 |

parts.content: Using a demand and supply diagram for the agricultural market, explain how subsidies for fertilizers (Text A, paragraph 3) might affect the equilibrium price and quantity of agricultural products in Panama.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/91ea8114-9f5f-4488-809a-9d3847c7aa91)

Subsidies for fertilizers shift the supply curve of agricultural products to the right(from S1 to S2) as producers’ costs of production decrease. This leads to:  
- Lower equilibrium price for agricultural products(P2<P1)
- Higher equilibrium quantity(Q2>Q1), because producers can supply more at every price level.  
- The market moves to a new equilibrium(from point A to point B) with greater output and lower price.

parts.content: Using an externalities diagram, explain how fertilizer subsidies might lead to negative externalities in the Panama Canal Basin (Text A, paragraph 3).
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/87ada8e7-6e82-48ec-b64c-8acfafaf93a0)

- In an externalities diagram, the marginal private cost (MPC) curve shifts downward due to the subsidy, increasing output beyond the socially optimal level(Qm>Qopt)
- The increased quantity produced leads to overuse of fertilizers, causing pollution and ecological damage, shown by the divergence between marginal social cost (MSC) and marginal private cost.  
- The result is a welfare loss to society, as the environmental costs are not fully accounted for in production decisions.

parts.content: Using information from the text/data (including Table 2) and your knowledge of economics, evaluate the effectiveness of Panama’s strategies for managing its balance of payments while maintaining economic growth.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include 

**Definition:**

- Balance of payments: A record of all transactions between one country and the rest of the world, consisting of the current account, capital account, and financial account.  
- Economic growth: An increase in a country’s real GDP over time.

**Range of Arguments:**

- Strategy 1: Using canal revenues and service exports to support the current account.  
  - The Panama Canal brings substantial service export revenue (about 40% of export service revenue) and has grown by nearly 5% in 2022. This revenue eases pressure on the current account, contributing to a smaller current account deficit over time (Table 2 shows a decrease from -400 to -270 million).  
  - However, heavy reliance on canal tolls and shipping could be risky if shipping routes elsewhere become more competitive or if global trade slows.

- Strategy 2: Trade diversification and tariff reduction.  
  - Export diversification aims to reduce vulnerability to external shocks and improve competitiveness, especially in bananas and specialty coffee. According to Table 2, FDI inflows have risen (from 3 850 to 4 200 million), potentially financing upgrades in infrastructure to facilitate these exports.  
  - Nonetheless, small-scale producers may face competition from cheaper foreign imports under free trade, which could harm parts of the agricultural sector if they lack support to improve efficiency.

- Strategy 3: Monetary policy tightening to manage inflation and maintain stability.  
  - Higher interest rates (Text A, paragraph 3) can curb inflationary pressures (inflation rose to 3.2% in 2022). This may prevent the currency peg arrangement from coming under stress. Stable inflation rates and a pegged currency can maintain investor confidence, potentially supporting FDI inflows.  
  - Increased interest rates reduce aggregate demand, from AD1 to AD2, risking a slowdown in economic growth and making it harder for local businesses to finance expansion.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/991913df-d4c2-4f9b-842c-edee88f15459)

- Strategy 4: Targeted subsidies and supply-side policies.  
  - Subsidies for fertilizers and other agriculture inputs lower costs of production, potentially boosting supply and improving food security. This could help reduce imports in some sectors and improve the trade balance.  
  - However, fertilizer subsidies may cause negative environmental externalities near the Panama Canal Basin, increasing long-term costs. Overuse of fertilizers can jeopardize tourism and fisheries, potentially harming the service export balance.

- Strategy 5: Special economic zones to reduce unemployment and attract FDI.  
  - Tax incentives encourage foreign firms to set up production, resulting in higher FDI (up from 3 850 to 4 200 million in Table 2). This inflow can help fund infrastructure and human capital development, contributing to economic growth.  
  - If retraining programs are lacking in quality, structural unemployment might persist for some displaced workers. The policy’s success depends on matching labor supply with the skill needs of incoming industries.

**Evaluation:**

- Strengths:  
  - Canal revenue and rising service exports have partially offset the negative balance in goods trade.  
  - The currency peg with the US dollar ensures stability, which can attract investors.  
  - Growth in FDI and portfolio investment points to increasing confidence in Panama’s economy and helps fund infrastructure upgrades.  
- Limitations:  
  - Over-reliance on the canal and import-dependent sectors suggests vulnerability to external shocks. The trade in goods remains in deficit.  
  - Structural unemployment may persist if retraining and education programs do not align with new industries, undermining long-term growth.  
  - Environmental damage from large-scale fertilizer use could undermine tourism and sustainability, negatively affecting future growth.

**Conclusion:**

- Panama’s blend of canal revenues, trade diversification, and foreign investment has helped reduce its current account deficit and sustain moderate economic growth. 
- Yet reliance on the canal sector and high imports leaves the economy exposed to global economic conditions. 
- Sustainably managing agricultural subsidies and improving the effectiveness of structural policies is vital for long-term balance of payments improvements and continued growth.

questions.specification:
## Economic Policy and Structural Challenges in South Africa

South Africa has long been regarded as one of Africa’s leading economies, with significant mineral endowments and a relatively diversified industrial base. However, persistent structural challenges such as high unemployment, inequality, and currency volatility—continue to shape its economic development prospects. In 2021, official figures put the unemployment rate at 34.5%, while inflation stood at approximately 4.5%. By 2022, elevated global energy prices and supply-chain disruptions pushed inflation above 6%, prompting the South African Reserve Bank to gradually raise its policy rate.

In an effort to accelerate growth, the government introduced a renewed infrastructure plan in 2022, focusing on transportation, power generation, and digital connectivity. Advocates argue that these projects can lower production costs and bolster the long-term productive capacity of the economy. Critics, however, highlight South Africa’s already high public debt, which reached about 70% of GDP in 2022, worrying that escalating debt service costs may limit the government’s fiscal space.

Seeking to address extreme income disparities, South Africa introduced a series of social grants targeting low-income households. While these measures have provided a degree of income security, structural unemployment remains an obstacle, particularly for young people. Many have questioned whether the social grants undermine labor market incentives or whether they are indispensable given high poverty levels. To promote job creation, the government has partnered with the private sector to devise targeted skill-development programs—especially aimed at the mining and manufacturing sectors.

In terms of external trade, South Africa has maintained significant exports of precious minerals (particularly platinum and gold), as well as manufactured goods like automobiles. However, the country also faces constraints in global markets due to occasional quality and regulatory concerns. To shield domestic producers from heightened competition in textiles, the government imposed a 10% tariff on imported textile goods in mid-2022. Proponents believe the measure guards crucial local jobs, but critics note that such protection may increase consumer prices and trigger retaliation from trading partners.

Environmental and health impacts from mining activities have sparked debate on the need for tighter regulation of negative externalities. In 2019, the government introduced a carbon tax to curb emissions from energy-intensive industries. Despite concerns about increased costs, many analysts believe the tax is a necessary step toward addressing climate change and encouraging firms to adopt cleaner technologies. Additionally, cost-reduction schemes exist to assist small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) in transitioning to greener practices.

The South African Reserve Bank follows a managed float approach to the rand, occasionally intervening to smooth out erratic fluctuations. The rand has shown volatility in recent years, in part due to shifting investor confidence and external shocks, such as surging global commodity prices. Foreign direct investment (FDI) has oscillated, with inflows of about US 3 billion in 2020 but only US 1.2 billion in 2021, partly reflecting uncertainty over public policy directions and pandemic-related disruptions.

Moving forward, policymakers must reconcile the need for long-term infrastructure investment with the pressing requirement to maintain public debt sustainability. Balancing social goals, such as job creation and reducing inequality, with macroeconomic stability remains a central challenge. Many observers suggest South Africa must deepen structural reforms ranging from labor market policies to trade liberalization if it hopes to achieve higher, more inclusive growth.

Below are two tables containing selected data referenced in the case study:

**Table 1: Selected Macroeconomic Indicators for South Africa (2019–2022)**

| Indicator                    | 2019  | 2020  | 2021  | 2022 (est.) |
|-----------------------------|-------|-------|-------|-------------|
| Real GDP Growth Rate (%)    | 0.2   | -6.4  | 4.9   | 2.0         |
| Inflation Rate (%)          | 4.1   | 3.3   | 4.5   | 6.2         |
| Unemployment Rate (%)       | 29.0  | 32.6  | 34.5  | 34.0        |
| Government Debt (% of GDP)  | 60.0  | 67.0  | 68.5  | 70.0        |
| Exchange Rate (ZAR per US$) | 14.4  | 16.5  | 15.8  | 17.2        |

**Table 2: Trade and Income Distribution Indicators**

| Indicator                                                       | 2019 | 2020 | 2021 | 2022 (est.) |
|-----------------------------------------------------------------|------|------|------|-------------|
| Net Exports (US$ billion)                                       | 13.5 | 8.1  | 10.6 | 9.4         |
| Tariff on Imported Textiles (%)                                 | 0    | 0    | 0    | 10          |
| Gini Coefficient (0 = perfect equality; 1 = perfect inequality) | 0.63 | 0.63 | 0.62 | 0.62        |
| FDI Inflows (US$ billion)                                       | 5.3  | 3.0  | 1.2  | 2.0         |

parts.content: Using a negative externalities diagram, explain how the carbon tax (paragraph 5) may help reduce social costs associated with emissions from energy-intensive industries.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/b0d4240c-e575-45b7-8869-ce143c099c18)

- Without government intervention, the market equilibrium where marginal private cost (MPC) equals marginal private benefit (MPB) ignores external costs of pollution.  
- The carbon tax increases production costs for emitting firms, shifting MPC upward toward marginal social cost (MSC).  
- The higher cost may reduce output of polluting goods, bringing the market closer to the socially optimal level of production and internalizing the externality.  
- By internalizing external costs, the tax incentivizes firms to adopt cleaner technologies, potentially reducing overall emissions and social costs.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/5fa03fee-e728-48c4-99da-230cf72db179)

- Skill-development programs increase the quality of labor, shifting the labor supply curve rightward for the specific skills in demand or raising labor productivity.  
- Better-trained workers meet employers’ requirements more effectively, reducing occupational mismatch that leads to structural unemployment.  
- As employers find adequate talent locally, demand for labor in mining and manufacturing increases, lowering equilibrium unemployment.  
- By addressing structural issues, such programs help reduce underemployment and boost the employability of the workforce.

- A tariff on imports raises the price of foreign textiles, causing the domestic price to rise from the world price to the world price plus tariff.  
- Domestic producers benefit as they can sell more at the higher price, potentially increasing their output and revenue.

- Government may earn tariff revenue, but there is an overall deadweight loss to society due to inefficiency and potential retaliation in global markets.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/b0bd4f95-ae28-401f-ab11-56189acb2816)

- The Gini coefficient of around 0.62–0.63 indicates a high level of income inequality, where the Lorenz curve is relatively far from the line of perfect equality.  
- The slight decrease from 0.63 to 0.62 suggests marginal improvement in income distribution over time, though inequality remains significant.  
- A persistently high Gini reflects structural disparities in skills, employment opportunities, and wage differentials.  
- Efforts such as social grants and skill-development programs aim to reduce this gap, but the data shows that inequality remains a substantial challenge.

parts.content: Using information from the text/data and your knowledge of economics, evaluate the potential impact of increased public infrastructure investment on South Africa’s economic growth and development, given the country’s rising public debt.
parts.markscheme:
**Definition:**

- Economic growth: An increase in real output (real GDP) over time.  
- Economic development: Improvement in standards of living, reduction in poverty, and increased access to goods and services, often measured by qualitative factors.  
- Public debt: The total of government’s borrowing liabilities, expressed as a percentage of GDP in this context.

**Range of Arguments:**

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/a0b2a172-c19a-465a-bcc3-9bbc96ac919b)

Arguments for increased public infrastructure investment  
- Short-run aggregate demand (AD) increase:  
  - Direct spending on infrastructure projects increases government expenditure (G), shifting AD to the right.  
  - Potential rise in real GDP and employment in the short term, helping address high unemployment (34% in 2022).  
- Potential long-run aggregate supply (AS) gains:  
  - Improved roads, power generation, and digital connectivity reduce production costs and enhance productivity.  
  - Expansion in AS may help moderate inflation (which increased to 6.2% in 2022) over the longer term.  
- Enhanced investor confidence:  
  - Infrastructure upgrades can attract FDI by demonstrating a commitment to modernizing the economy, supporting efficient production and trade.  
  - More diverse industries can develop around improved infrastructure, potentially reducing heavy reliance on mining exports.  
- Development gains:  
  - Infrastructure investments can boost access to public services, reduce regional disparities, and improve quality of life.  
  - Can be linked with social goals such as better training (paragraph 3) and job creation, addressing inequality.

Arguments against or for limitations  
- Rising public debt pressures (70% of GDP in 2022):  
  - Higher debt service payments may crowd out other government expenditures, limiting future fiscal space.  
  - May necessitate future tax increases or spending cuts if debt burden becomes unsustainable, reducing disposable income and slowing growth.  
- Inflationary risk in the short run:  
  - Increased AD can exacerbate demand-pull inflation, especially if the economy is operating near capacity.  
  - Higher inflation can undermine consumer purchasing power and potentially deter foreign investors if it becomes persistent.  
- Implementation challenges:  
  - Inefficient project management or corruption in infrastructure spending can reduce effectiveness, leading to cost overruns that worsen the debt situation.  
  - Existing structural issues (e.g., skills mismatch, regulatory concerns) may limit the effectiveness of infrastructure-led growth.  
- Opportunity cost:  
  - Resources directed toward large-scale infrastructure might reduce spending on other critical areas (e.g., education, healthcare), potentially slowing broader development.

**Conclusion:**

- Increased infrastructure investment can stimulate short-run economic growth through higher AD and long-run growth through improvements in productive capacity.  
- There are significant development benefits, including reduced unemployment and improved public services, but these depend on efficient project execution and complementary policies (such as skill-development programs).  
- The rising public debt of 70% suggests caution; infrastructure spending might need to be balanced with strategies to maintain fiscal sustainability to avoid crowding out and future tax burdens.  
- Overall, infrastructure investment has the potential to boost long-term prosperity, but it requires prudent management of debt, transparent governance, and alignment with structural reforms to maximize inclusive growth and development.

questions.specification:
## Economic Developments in Spain

Spain, located on the Iberian Peninsula in southwestern Europe, is renowned for its diverse economic activities, including tourism, agriculture, automotive manufacturing, and renewable energy. Despite the challenges presented by recent global economic downturns, Spain’s real GDP bounced back in 2022 with a growth rate of 5.1%. However, unemployment remains among the highest in the European Union, recorded at 13.4% in 2022. Inflation has also posed a challenge, rising to 6.9% in the same year, driven partly by higher energy costs after policy shifts to reduce reliance on imported fossil fuels.

To foster long-term growth, the Spanish government has increased public spending on digital infrastructure and green energy projects. These initiatives aim to stimulate research and development in automotive manufacturing (particularly electric vehicles), while also attracting foreign direct investment (FDI) in specialized technology clusters. However, some small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) have found it difficult to access affordable credit, limiting their capacity to adapt to new environmental regulations imposed by the government.

Tourism, which traditionally accounts for around 12–14% of Spain’s GDP, rebounded sharply in 2022 after pandemic-related restrictions eased. Although tourist arrivals reached nearly 71 million close to pre-crisis levels rising inflation and political tensions in certain regions have introduced uncertainty. There are also concerns about negative externalities associated with the rapid growth of tourism, such as increased pollution and stresses on local housing markets.

Spain is subject to fiscal guidelines under the European Union’s Stability and Growth Pact, which recommends that budget deficits remain under 3% of GDP and total public debt remains sustainable. Nonetheless, Spain’s public debt ratio exceeded 115% of GDP in 2021, prompting warnings from the European Commission. Partly in response, the government implemented a new minimum wage policy in 2022, hoping to boost consumer spending and reduce inequality while still adhering to the need for balanced budgets.

On the international trade front, Spain benefits from its membership in the world’s largest single market: the European Union. Exports of automobiles, machinery, and agricultural products rose by 6.2% in 2022 relative to the previous year. However, imports increased more sharply due to higher energy prices, causing a modest widening in the trade deficit. Meanwhile, policymakers have discussed imposing additional environmental standards on imported products to align with the country’s goal of cutting carbon emissions by 40% before 2030.

The government has also proposed a carbon tax on major polluters, aiming to shift production toward cleaner technologies and reduce the external costs associated with high emissions. While environmental groups have expressed support, some Spanish manufacturers worry about losing price competitiveness in global markets. In response, there are calls for complementary policies, such as subsidies targeting low-carbon production methods, to balance environmental goals with economic competitiveness.

Spain’s regional governments (Comunidades Autónomas) have considerable autonomy in implementing social programs, including unemployment benefits, healthcare, and educational initiatives. Certain regions, like the Basque Country and Catalonia, have introduced additional measures to attract high-skilled foreign labor and strengthen innovation. However, disparities in fiscal resources across regions remain a concern, contributing to debates on how to achieve more balanced regional development.

**Table 1: Selected Macroeconomic Indicators for Spain**

| Indicator                      | 2019   | 2020    | 2021    | 2022    |
|--------------------------------|--------|---------|---------|---------|
| Real GDP Growth Rate (%)       | 2.0    | -10.8   | 5.1     | 5.1     |
| Unemployment Rate (%)          | 14.1   | 15.5    | 14.8    | 13.4    |
| Inflation Rate (%)             | 0.8    | -0.3    | 3.1     | 6.9     |
| Public Debt (% of GDP)         | 95.5   | 120.0   | 115.3   | 113.0   |
| Budget Balance (% of GDP)      | -2.9   | -11.1   | -6.9    | -5.0    |

**Table 2: Socioeconomic and Trade-Related Indicators**

| Indicator                                         | 2019     | 2021     | 2022     |
|---------------------------------------------------|----------|----------|----------|
| Tourism Revenues (EUR billion)                    | 92       | 34       | 60       |
| FDI Inflows (EUR billion)                         | 10.2     | 6.1      | 10.8     |
| Exports of Goods (EUR billion)                    | 290      | 277      | 294      |
| Imports of Goods (EUR billion)                    | 325      | 330      | 355      |
| Minimum Wage (Monthly, EUR)                       | 1050     | 1108     | 1160     |

parts.content: Using a negative externalities diagram, explain how the expansion of tourism (paragraph 3) might lead to the over-consumption of resources and increased social costs.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/16e6b4c4-70d5-4d67-93b0-80882a320dcd)

- The market equilibrium for tourism is set where private costs and private benefits intersect, ignoring the higher social costs (e.g. pollution, housing market strains) mentioned in the text.  
- In a negative externalities diagram, the marginal social cost (MSC) curve lies above the marginal private cost (MPC) curve.  
- Because consumers only consider the private cost, tourism is consumed at a quantity greater than the socially optimal level.  
- This leads to overallocation, as shown by the gap between Qopt and Qm in the diagram, and causes spillover effects like overcrowding and environmental degradation.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks>
<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using a demand-and-supply diagram, explain how raising the minimum wage (Table 2) may affect the labor market in Spain, based on the text.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/a4ec1b6f-3a2a-4d2e-acab-8d132149b066)

- In a labor market diagram with wage on the vertical axis and employment on the horizontal axis, setting a minimum wage above the equilibrium wage (Wₑ) — as shown by Wmin — can reduce the quantity of labor demanded if demand for labor is relatively elastic.
- Employment may fall for some workers, leading to potential unemployment among low-skilled workers.  
- However, given Spain’s goal to boost consumer spending and reduce inequality, higher wages, indicated by the increase in Wmin, could increase the purchasing power of those still employed.
- The ultimate impact depends on labor market conditions: if aggregate demand is strong (as suggested by Spain’s GDP growth), any job losses might be mitigated by overall economic expansion.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks>
<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using an AD/AS diagram, explain how an increase in public spending on green energy and digital infrastructure (paragraph 2) might influence Spain’s long-term economic growth.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/7504728e-4e99-4c3a-a7f2-ee618bee5ac3)

- An increase in government spending shifts AD to the right initially, boosting real GDP in the short run.  
- Over time, improved infrastructure and innovation can shift the long-run aggregate supply to the right (from LRAS1 to LRAS2) through increased productivity and efficiency.  
- Spain’s focus on electric vehicles and digital technology can attract further FDI, enhancing productive capacity and fostering sustainable, long-term growth.  
- This reduces reliance on imported fossil fuels, potentially stabilizing energy costs and inflation.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using a tariff diagram (or trade diagram), explain how introducing additional environmental standards on imported products (paragraph 5) could affect the level of imports and domestic production in Spain.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/4b36b446-07fb-4ffb-aa23-5a21dcd8920e)

- Stricter environmental standards raise the costs or requirements for foreign producers exporting to Spain, effectively shifting the supply curve of imports leftward in a trade diagram.  
- The price of imports may rise, reducing the quantity of imports (from Q₄ − Q₁ to Q₃ − Q₂) while encouraging domestic producers to expand production (as seen by the increase from Q₁ to Q₂) since they face relatively less competition if they meet these standards.
- Domestic industries benefit from higher market share, but consumers may face higher prices, and trade partners might retaliate with their own standards.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks>
<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using information from the text/data (especially Table 2) and your knowledge of economics, discuss the potential benefits and drawbacks for Spain of relying on both tourism expansion and an emerging green energy sector to drive future economic growth.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include 

**Definition:**

- Economic growth: An increase in a country’s real level of national output over time.  
- Sustainable development: Meeting present needs without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs.

**Range of Arguments:**

Benefits of Relying on Tourism Expansion  
- Tourism generates significant revenue inflows (EUR 60 billion in 2022, up from EUR 34 billion in 2021). This provides foreign exchange and stimulates local businesses (hotels, restaurants).  
- Employment opportunities can rise in hospitality and related services, reducing unemployment from its current level of 13.4%.
- A rebound to nearly 71 million tourist arrivals indicates strong potential for further growth.  
- Positive spillover effects for regional economies: demand for cultural, recreational, and infrastructure services helps develop remote regions.
- This will cause a rightward shift of LRAS, from LRAS1 to LRAS2, as new jobs are going to be created, consequently increasing the economic capacity of Spain. 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/7504728e-4e99-4c3a-a7f2-ee618bee5ac3)

Drawbacks of Tourism Reliance  
- Volatility of tourism due to global economic fluctuations, pandemics, or political tensions, as noted by uncertainties in some Spanish regions.  
- Over-reliance can cause seasonal and structural unemployment outside peak tourist periods.  
- Negative externalities (increased pollution, pressure on local resources, housing market stress), leading to higher social costs.  
- Longer-term sustainability could be threatened if mass tourism conflicts with environmental regulations, raising costs or damaging Spain’s tourism brand.

Benefits of Relying on Green Energy Sector  
- Aligns with policy aims to reduce carbon emissions by 40% before 2030, supporting global initiatives and possibly generating positive externalities (cleaner air, lower health costs).  
- Potential to attract FDI in green tech clusters, enhancing Spain’s position as a leader in renewables (as renewable share of electricity generation rose from 38% in 2019 to 45% in 2022).  
- Creation of high-skilled jobs and new R&D opportunities in fields like electric vehicles and digital infrastructure.  
- Improved energy security by reducing dependence on imported fossil fuels, helping stabilize Spain’s trade deficit.

Drawbacks of Green Energy Reliance  
- High initial costs to invest in new infrastructure, which may burden public and private finances, especially given Spain’s public debt ratio of 113% of GDP in 2022.  
- Potential competitiveness concerns for Spanish manufacturers if carbon taxes or strict environmental standards raise production costs.  
- Risk of unequal regional development, as some regions may lack resources to invest in green tech, deepening regional disparities.  
- If the global economy experiences a slowdown, the demand for green technology exports may be weaker than expected, limiting the sector’s growth potential.

Integration and Synthesis  
- Tourism and green energy both have potential to boost Spain’s real GDP, reduce unemployment, and increase government revenues.  
- The government must balance the opportunities from tourism expansion with environmental sustainability goals, especially given tourism’s Negative externalities.  
- Green energy and digital infrastructure projects have a long-term benefit in shifting LRAS to the right, but high public debt and uneven regional resources could pose constraints.  
- Combining tourism’s short- to medium-term growth with longer-term structural benefits of green energy could diversify Spain’s economy, reduce volatility, and support stable development.

**Conclusion:**

- A balanced approach—continuing to support tourism while investing in the green economy—could stabilize growth and create sustainable development paths.  
- Policy measures, such as subsidies for low-carbon production and strict but workable environmental regulations, might ensure competitiveness and long-term social welfare.  
- The combination of these two sectors could yield broad-based benefits, though careful management of risks (negative externalities, public debt) is essential to secure sustainable prosperity.

Estonia is a small, open economy located in Northern Europe. Since joining the European Union in 2004 and adopting the euro in 2011, Estonia has experienced consistent economic growth. Strong investment in technology has positioned Estonia as a pioneer in digital services, with many government services offered online. Timber remains an important export, and the country has recently implemented progressive taxation measures to address income inequality.

| 2021 | 29.6 | 10.6 | 0.31 | 41.0 |

parts.content: Using information from Table 2, calculate the price elasticity of demand (PED) for e-service applications in Estonia as the price increases from €20 to €25 per month.
parts.markscheme:
• Percentage change in price = ((25 − 20) / 20) × 100% = 25%  
• Percentage change in quantity demanded = ((85 − 100) / 100) × 100% = −15%  
• PED = (−15%) / (25%) = −0.6 (in absolute value, 0.6)

parts.markscheme:

• Increase in real GDP = €29.6 billion − €26.8 billion = €2.8 billion

parts.content: Referring to Figure 1, calculate the price elasticity of supply (PES) for Estonia’s timber exports as the price changes from €100 per cubic meter to €120 per cubic meter.
parts.markscheme:
• Percentage change in price = ((120 − 100) / 100) × 100% = 20%  
• Percentage change in quantity supplied = ((35 000 − 40 000) / 40 000) × 100% = −12.5%  
• PES = (−12.5%) / 20% = −0.625 (in absolute value, 0.625)

parts.content: Using information from the text, calculate the total Keynesian multiplier effect on real GDP if the Estonian government increases its infrastructure spending by €200 million, assuming the marginal propensity to consume (MPC) is 0.8.
parts.markscheme:
• Multiplier = 1 ÷ (1 − 0.8) = 5  
• Total effect on real GDP = €200 million × 5 = €1 000 million (or €1 billion)

parts.content: Using information from Table 3, explain how progressive taxation might help reduce income inequality in Estonia.
parts.markscheme:
• Individuals earning higher incomes (above €25 000) face higher marginal tax rates, taking a larger proportion of their additional income.  
• This structure narrows after-tax income differentials because higher earners contribute a progressively larger share.  
• Lower-income groups benefit from lower tax rates, potentially increasing their disposable income proportionately more.  
• The overall effect is a reduction in income inequality, as measured by measures like the Gini coefficient.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and knowledge of economics, recommend a policy which the Estonian government could implement to support further economic growth while addressing income inequality.
parts.markscheme:
• Define “economic growth” as a sustained increase in a country’s real output or real GDP over time.  
• Define “income inequality” as an unequal distribution of income within a population, often measured by the Gini coefficient.  
• Consider that Estonia is a small, open economy with growing digital services and an emphasis on exports like timber, indicating a reliance on global markets.  
• Recognize that income inequality can persist despite growth; progressive taxation already exists, yet supplementary measures may be necessary to address income disparities.  
• Propose an expansion of targeted education and training programs to enhance technological and digital skills, building on Estonia’s strengths in digital services.  
• Argue that improved labor force skills lead to higher productivity and competitiveness of exports, such as e-service applications, driving economic growth.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/03a13168-fd09-47f3-8e73-31e727eb8089)
• Emphasize that equipping lower-income groups with better skills increases their potential earnings, reducing income inequality.  
• Note that the government could fund these programs through existing progressive tax revenues, ensuring that higher earners contribute a larger portion to finance inclusive growth.  
• Suggest that improved skills and higher employment would support government revenues in the long run, creating a virtuous circle of growth and reduced inequality.  
• Conclude that targeted investment in human capital is consistent with the existing digital infrastructure, addresses income imbalances, and strengthens Estonia’s position in international markets.

questions.specification:
## Armenia: Policies for Inclusive Growth 

Armenia is a landlocked country in the South Caucasus region, experiencing considerable economic transitions since the early 2000s. Despite geographical and political challenges, Armenia has steadily increased its real GDP, supported mainly by a thriving IT sector, robust agricultural outputs, and steady inflows of remittances from a global diaspora. The government has set medium-term objectives to stabilize inflation around 4%–5% and reduce unemployment from its current rate of 10.2% (as of late 2022).

To foster inclusive growth, officials have employed a combination of fiscal stimulus and monetary policy fine-tuning. Fiscal stimulus has included infrastructure investments and carefully targeted subsidies for small-scale rural farmers. At the same time, the Central Bank of Armenia has used interest rate adjustments to manage inflationary pressures. As part of ongoing reforms, the government increasingly relies on indirect taxes (like excise duties on tobacco and alcohol) to raise revenue, reflecting a shift from direct taxation of income.

Notably, Armenia has introduced a modest price floor for certain agricultural goods, such as grapes used in winemaking, aiming to ensure stable income for farmers. This policy emerged after grape producers faced volatility when key export destinations revised import quotas. Policymakers defend the price floor as a safeguard against declining rural incomes, but critics argue it may cause surpluses if domestic demand does not keep pace.

In the macroeconomic arena, Armenia’s inflation rate stood at 7.4% in 2022, partly driven by rising global commodity prices. The Central Bank responded with incremental interest rate hikes. Meanwhile, due to ongoing structural updates across industries, real GDP growth has fluctuated between 3% and 5% over the past three years. Still, policymakers remain optimistic about medium-term economic expansion, underpinned by public spending on energy infrastructure and small-business incentives.

On the microeconomic front, there is growing concern about externalities related to precious-metal mining. Some environmental groups advocate stricter regulations on mining companies, arguing these firms should be taxed more for the pollution they generate. An alternative policy idea is to implement a tradable permits system, yet it remains under discussion due to technical and administrative challenges.

Internationally, Armenia is a member of the Eurasian Economic Union (EAEU), which has facilitated easier trade with neighboring Russia and other member states. Some exporters have benefited from lower tariff barriers within the EAEU market. Nonetheless, there are renewed calls to diversify trade beyond the bloc to reduce vulnerability to demand shocks. Armenia’s government reports a 14% increase in total exports in 2021, with ICT services and mineral-related products leading the surge.

Foreign direct investment in Armenia remains modest, though a slight uptick occurred in 2022, particularly in the tourism and hospitality sectors. Moreover, remittance inflows approached US$2 billion last year—30% higher than in 2020—which continues to underpin private consumption. However, reliance on remittances raises questions about long-term sustainability, prompting discussions on strategies to attract more productive investment in manufacturing and technology.

Overall, policymakers aim to balance short-run efforts—such as stabilizing price levels and safeguarding rural incomes—with longer-run strategies geared toward export diversification, environmental protection, and human capital development. Whether these measures can accelerate Armenia’s transition into a more resilient and inclusive economy remains to be seen. Meanwhile, observers note that the interplay between government policy, external demand, and private sector innovation will be key to shaping the country’s economic trajectory in the coming decade.

**Table 1: Key Macroeconomic Indicators of Armenia (2019–2022)**

| Indicator                  | 2019   | 2020   | 2021   | 2022   |
|----------------------------|--------|--------|--------|--------|
| Nominal GDP (US$ billion)  | 13.7   | 12.6   | 13.1   | 14.5   |
| Real GDP Growth Rate (%)   | 5.2    | -7.4   | 5.0    | 4.3    |
| Inflation Rate (%)         | 2.5    | 1.2    | 5.8    | 7.4    |
| Unemployment Rate (%)      | 18.0   | 20.2   | 15.3   | 10.2   |
| Exchange Rate (AMD per US$)| 480    | 520    | 495    | 395    |

**Table 2: Selected Data on Armenia’s Agricultural and External Sectors**

| Indicator                      | 2019  | 2020  | 2021  | 2022  |
|--------------------------------|-------|-------|-------|-------|
| Agricultural Subsidies (US$ m) | 100   | 120   | 110   | 130   |
| Price Floor on Grapes (US$/kg) | 0.45  | 0.50  | 0.50  | 0.55  |
| FDI (US$ million)              | 254   | 210   | 225   | 270   |
| Remittance Inflows (US$ bn)    | 1.35  | 1.20  | 1.50  | 2.00  |
| Export Growth Rate (%)         | 12    | -5    | 8     | 14    |
