parts.content: Define economic growth.
parts.markscheme:
An increase in a country's real output (real GDP) <marks>1 mark</marks>
over a period time.<marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: List two reasons why inflation increased in the Czech Republic in 2022.
parts.markscheme:
- The case study highlights that the country relies heavily on imported raw materials, contributing to a widening trade deficit. Disruptions in global supply chains increased production costs, leading to higher prices for goods and services. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Fossil fuels still account for 70% of electricity production in the Czech Republic. The introduction of carbon taxes ($30 per metric ton of CO₂) and rising global energy prices pushed up costs for businesses and consumers, fueling inflation. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- The unemployment rate in 2022 was at a record low of 2.5%, leading to increased competition for workers. This tight labor market drove up wages, which, in turn, contributed to cost-push inflation. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- The Czech koruna depreciated by 10% against major trading partners in 2022 due to high inflation and geopolitical instability. A weaker currency made imports more expensive, increasing the overall price level in the economy. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using information from Table 1, calculate the percentage change in GDP from 2019 to 2022.
parts.markscheme:
The formula to calculate the percentage change in GDP is:
\[
\text{Percentage Change} = \left( \frac{\text{New Value} - \text{Old Value}}{\text{Old Value}} \right) \times 100
\] <marks>1 mark</marks>

From Table 1:

- GDP in 2019 = 200 billion USD
- GDP in 2022 = 240 billion USD

\[{Percentage Change}= \left( \frac{240 - 200}{200} \right) \times 100
\]

\[
= \left( \frac{40}{200} \right) \times 100
\]

\[
= 20\%
\] <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Draw an aggregate demand and aggregate supply (AD-AS) diagram to illustrate the impact of an increase in interest rates on the Czech economy.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/2c89ebfa-98ab-4aa7-b4b5-2de5aa09ee94)

- When the interest rate increases from 1.5% in 2021 to 4.25% in 2022, borrowing becomes more expensive for consumers and businesses.<marks>1 mark</marks>
- This leads to a decrease in consumption and investment, shifting the Aggregate Demand (AD) curve leftward from AD1 to AD2.<marks>1 mark</marks>
- As a result, real GDP decreases (from Y1 to Y2) and inflationary pressures ease (from P1 to P2). <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using an exchange rate diagram, explain how the depreciation of the Karuna in 2022 may have affected the Czech Republic’s exports and imports.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/cebcea44-b50e-4f17-93ec-716f29e6de40)

- In 2022, the Karuna depreciated by 10% against major trading partners.
- In an exchange rate diagram (foreign exchange market), the supply of CZK increases (shifting S1 to S2) due to capital outflows and economic instability.
- This depreciation makes Czech exports cheaper for foreign buyers, increasing demand for exports.
- Conversely, imports become more expensive, reducing the demand for foreign goods.

<marks>2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks>
<marks>4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using a trade deficit diagram, explain how the Czech Republic’s dependence on imported raw materials has influenced its balance of payments.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/5f9b914f-7517-4c90-a9f4-788c07a0718b)
- The Czech Republic relies heavily on imported raw materials, increasing its import expenditure.
- As imports exceed exports, the country experiences a trade deficit (negative trade balance).
- This worsens the current account balance, reflected in the increasing trade deficit from USD -10 billion in 2020 to USD -15 billion in 2022 (Table 1).
- In the trade deficit diagram, the import curve (M) is higher than the export curve (X), indicating a negative balance.

<marks>2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks>
<marks>4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using a market failure diagram, explain how the implementation of carbon taxes could reduce negative externalities in the Czech Republic.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/483e6c57-2751-4254-9d0b-31ae17fea8d8)
- Observing the diagram above:

	- The introduction of a carbon tax helps shift the MPC curve to the MSC curve.
	- However, as there are alternatives to carbon emitting fossil fuels, which do not get affected from carbon tax, a lot of firms may switch to those alternatives.
	- Hence, firms still pollute the environment, but by a lower factor, which is represented by the shift from MSC1 to MSC2
	- Therefore the externality may not be fully corrected, but rather reduced.
- The Czech Republic relies on fossil fuels for 70% of electricity production, contributing to negative externalities such as air pollution and climate change.
- Carbon taxes (USD 30 per metric ton of CO₂) aim to internalize the external cost by increasing production costs for polluting firms.

<marks>2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks>
<marks>4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using a labor market diagram, explain how record-low unemployment can contribute to labor shortages in certain sectors of the Czech Republic’s economy.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/605ad5a9-881d-40d4-9ac9-44ec81c2c28d)

- Increased Competition for Workers: With fewer unemployed workers, businesses must compete to attract talent. 
- Higher wages: Companies offer better salaries to lure workers, increasing labor costs.
- Skills Mismatches: Not all unemployed workers have the skills needed for high-demand sectors.
- The technology and renewable energy sectors are expanding.
- However, vocational training and R&D investment remain low, leading to a gap between available skills and industry needs.
- Reduced Economic Growth Potential: When businesses cannot find enough workers, they may delay expansion plans or relocate to countries with larger labor pools and increase automation, replacing jobs with technology.
- Labor shortages can push up wages, which raises production costs. Businesses may pass these costs onto consumers, contributing to inflation, as seen in the Czech Republic's 8% inflation rate in 2022.

<marks>2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks>
<marks>4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using information from the text/data and your knowledge of economics, evaluate the effectiveness of the Czech Republic’s government policies in promoting economic development.
parts.markscheme:
### Evaluation of fiscal policies

- Carbon taxes ($30 per metric ton of CO₂)
  - Positive: Addresses negative externalities from fossil fuel dependence
  - Negative: May increase production costs and contribute to inflation
  - Long-term benefit: Encourages transition to renewable energy sources

- Investment in technology and renewable energy sectors
  - Positive: Diversifies economy and creates higher-skilled jobs
  - Negative: Insufficient vocational training creates skills mismatch
  - Development impact: Potential for sustainable growth but requires complementary education policies

### Evaluation of monetary policies

- Interest rate increases (from 1.5% in 2021 to 4.25% in 2022)
  - Positive: Helps control 8% inflation rate
  - Negative: May reduce investment and consumption, slowing economic growth
  - Development impact: Short-term pain for long-term stability

### Evaluation of labor market policies

- Record-low unemployment (2.5%)
  - Positive: High employment contributes to higher living standards
  - Negative: Labor shortages in key sectors limit growth potential
  - Development impact: Wage increases benefit workers but may fuel inflation

### Evaluation of trade policies

- Heavy reliance on imported raw materials
  - Negative: Contributes to trade deficit ($15 billion in 2022)
  - Development impact: Vulnerability to external shocks and exchange rate fluctuations

### Overall evaluation

- GDP growth (20% from 2019-2022) suggests some policy success
- However, high inflation, currency depreciation, and trade deficit indicate challenges
- Sustainable development requires addressing structural issues:
  - Reducing fossil fuel dependence
  - Improving skills training
  - Diversifying the economy
  - Managing inflation without stifling growth

<note>Award up to 15 marks using the following rubric:
Level 1 (1-5 marks): Basic description of policies with limited evaluation
Level 2 (6-10 marks): Sound analysis of multiple policies with some evaluation
Level 3 (11-15 marks): Comprehensive evaluation with well-supported judgments about effectiveness</note>

questions.specification:
## Economic Developments and Challenges in Bahrain

Bahrain is a small archipelagic nation in the Persian Gulf, known historically for its pearl industry and, more recently, for its role as a regional financial hub. Blessed with oil reserves but also facing depletion concerns, Bahrain has embarked on various economic diversification strategies. Over the last decade, government officials have introduced a national development plan to reduce dependence on hydrocarbons, increase non-oil exports, and expand the country’s tourism and financial sectors. Nonetheless, this transition has been gradual, and oil-related revenues still account for a significant share of government income.

In 2019, Bahrain’s Ministry of Finance implemented an ambitious fiscal program aiming to contain the budget deficit. These measures included a partial reduction of the longstanding electricity subsidies, an introduction of a value-added tax (VAT), and tightened regulations on certain imports. While these policies have helped reduce the government deficit from 10% to 7.5% of GDP, inflation showed volatility reaching 3.2% in 2021 before subsiding to 2.5% in 2022. The government also maintains a fixed exchange rate between the Bahraini dinar and the US dollar to stabilize external transactions, which can limit Bahrain’s monetary policy autonomy.

Despite improvements in macroeconomic indicators, approximately 11% of Bahrain’s population still lives on incomes classified below the national poverty line an issue that has garnered significant attention from social policymakers. In certain low-income neighborhoods, many families struggle with inadequate access to quality education and health care, contributing to a persistent “poverty cycle.” To address this, Bahrain’s government recently implemented targeted transfers for low-income households and launched vocational training programs to increase labor market participation. However, analysts caution that the effectiveness of these programs depends largely on stable financing and robust oversight mechanisms.

Foreign direct investment (FDI) inflows have been mixed. Financial services, manufacturing, and logistics have attracted multinational firms, partly due to Bahrain’s easy business registration processes and low corporate taxes. On the other hand, geopolitical uncertainties and periodic fluctuations in oil prices have deterred some investors. Meanwhile, the tourism sector, ranging from luxury hotels to cultural heritage tours, is seen as a growth area, contributing to job creation in services. Yet concerns persist about workforce skill mismatches and the need to import foreign labor for highly specialized roles.

Bahrain’s trade profile is characterized by significant exports of aluminum products and refined petroleum. As a member of the Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC), it benefits from regional trade agreements and reduced tariffs. However, non-oil exports remain relatively small as a portion of total sales abroad. To enhance its global participation, the government has encouraged small and medium enterprises (SMEs) to adopt innovative technologies and expand e-commerce platforms. Critics argue that administrative barriers such as cumbersome licensing procedures still hinder domestic firms’ access to international markets.

In terms of development indicators, life expectancy has gradually increased, but income inequality, as measured by the Gini coefficient, has inched upward over the past five years. Government officials attribute this partly to the rising cost of living in urban areas and a significant pay gap between skilled and unskilled labor. A new National Social Support Program proposes enhanced social spending on education and housing, which policy advocates believe is crucial for forging a more inclusive economy. Still, critics worry about the sustainability of such programs given the government’s reliance on volatile oil revenues.

Ultimately, Bahrain’s trajectory toward a more diverse and inclusive economy depends on balancing immediate social needs with long-term economic reforms. By strengthening the link between investments in human capital, innovation, and poverty reduction, policymakers hope to break the poverty cycle that continues to affect vulnerable segments of the population. Maintaining fiscal discipline while financing large-scale infrastructure projects, sustaining a stable exchange rate, and fostering global integration will be pivotal to Bahrain’s economic future.

**Table 1: Bahrain’s Selected Macroeconomic Indicators (2019–2022)**

| Indicator                        | 2019  | 2020  | 2021  | 2022  |
|----------------------------------|-------|-------|-------|-------|
| Real GDP Growth Rate (%)         | 2.2   | -4.9  | 2.1   | 3.3   |
| Inflation Rate (%)               | 1.0   | 2.8   | 3.2   | 2.5   |
| Budget Deficit (% of GDP)        | -10.0 | -13.0 | -9.0  | -7.5  |
| Oil Sector Contribution (% GDP)  | 17.0  | 15.8  | 16.3  | 15.0  |
| Public Debt (% of GDP)           | 102   | 128   | 130   | 123   |

**Table 2: Development Indicators for Bahrain**

| Indicator                                  | 2019  | 2020  | 2021  | 2022  |
|--------------------------------------------|-------|-------|-------|-------|
| Poverty Rate (% of population)            | 11.0  | 11.2  | 11.0  | 10.8  |
| Gini Coefficient                           | 0.36  | 0.37  | 0.38  | 0.38  |
| Life Expectancy (years)                   | 77.2  | 77.3  | 77.4  | 77.6  |
| FDI Inflows (US$ billion)                 | 1.56  | 0.80  | 1.20  | 1.40  |
| Non-oil Exports (% of total exports)       | 25    | 26    | 26.5  | 27.0  |
| Participation in Vocational Training (’000)| 14    | 13    | 16    | 18    |

parts.markscheme:
Using information from Table 1, calculate the absolute change in Bahrain’s real GDP growth rate from 2020 to 2022.  
- In 2020, real GDP growth rate was –4.9%.  
 - In 2022, real GDP growth rate was 3.3%.  
- Absolute change = 3.3 – (–4.9) = 3.3 + 4.9 = 8.2 percentage points.

parts.content: Using a poverty cycle diagram, explain how insufficient access to education and health care (paragraph 3) might perpetuate low incomes in certain Bahraini communities.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/74a7bd30-1ad0-4821-a3e1-f15fefce913c)

- Limits human capital formation, preventing many individuals from acquiring skills needed for higher-paid jobs.  
- Contributes to low productivity, which keeps wages low due to limited skill development.  
- Low incomes reduce the ability to invest in further education or better health-care, reinforcing the cycle of poverty.  
- Over generations, this cycle repeats, as children inherit limited opportunities, perpetuating poverty within those communities.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using a demand-and-supply-of-labor diagram, explain how the influx of foreign labor (paragraph 4) can affect wage rates in Bahrain’s high-skilled and low-skilled labor markets.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/e44510aa-cb52-415d-940b-9350e6a3de43)

- Shifts the supply of labor rightward (S1 to S2), driving wages down if demand remains unchanged.  
- In high-skilled sectors, if domestic skill sets are scarce, foreign labor can fill specialized roles, contributing to lower wage pressures for those positions.  
- In low-skilled sectors, an even larger increase in supply intensifies downward pressure on wages because of high competition for jobs.  
- Wage differentials between high-skilled and low-skilled labor may widen, possibly exacerbating income inequality.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

- Indicates a more unequal distribution of income, as the Lorenz curve bows further from the line of perfect equality.  
- Urban living costs and pay gaps between skilled and unskilled workers widen income differentials. 
- Even though life expectancy improved, limited redistribution might mean median incomes have not risen enough to prevent a rise in the Gini coefficient.

parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/44ef9b5b-253c-4016-8d68-efe6b24459b6)

- Reduce investor confidence, shifting demand for the Bahraini dinar left (D1 to D2) if investors prefer safer assets.  
- Can cause the dinar’s equilibrium exchange rate to depreciate if left to market forces.  
- The government may intervene by buying its own currency (or using reserves) to keep the dinar pegged, indicated by the text’s note on a fixed exchange rate regime.  
- Such intervention counters downward pressure, but if uncertainties persist, reserves might be needed continually to support the peg.

parts.content: Using information from the text/data (especially Table 2) and your knowledge of economics, evaluate the impact of Bahrain’s strategies to reduce poverty and develop non-oil sectors on the country’s economic growth and development prospects.
parts.markscheme:
**Definition:**  

- Economic growth: A sustained increase in real GDP or real output over time.  
- Economic development: Improvements in living standards, reduction of poverty, and enhanced access to basic needs such as education and healthcare.  

**Range of Arguments:** 

- Government Spending on Human Capital:  
	- Public investment in education, vocational training, and health care can promote a more skilled labor force.
	- An increase in Human Capital would lead to an increase in aggregate demand shifting the AD curve outwards (AD1 to AD2)  
- Table 2 shows increased participation in vocational training from 13,000 in 2020 to 18,000 in 2022, which can enhance productivity and raise GDP growth.  

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/f158dbdf-e7ba-4c2f-928d-241b015388ac)

- Improved human capital may reduce the poverty rate (down from 11.2% in 2020 to 10.8% in 2022), facilitating higher overall development.  
- However, government spending depends on oil revenues (since oil still contributes 15% to GDP in 2022), introducing volatility and jeopardizing program sustainability.

- Value-Added Tax and Fiscal Reforms  
	- Introduction of VAT aims to stabilize government finances and reduce the budget deficit (which fell from 10% in 2019 to 7.5% in 2022).  
	- Fiscal stability can create room for programs targeting poverty reduction and diversification.  
	- Yet higher consumption taxes can raise the cost of living, possibly worsening inequality unless supportive measures like targeted subsidies are in place.

- Development of Non-oil Sectors  
	- Manufacturing, financial services, and tourism have attracted FDI inflows, albeit with fluctuations (Table 2 shows FDI rising from US0.80 billion in 2020 to US$1.40 billion in 2022).  
	- Non-oil exports have also inched up from 25% to 27% of total exports, reflecting ongoing diversification that may boost long-run growth.  
	- However, reliance on specialized foreign labor highlights skill mismatches in Bahrain, potentially reducing local employment gains in high-value industries due to gaps in domestic education and training.

- Addressing Poverty and Inequality  
	- Targeted transfers and the National Social Support Program aim to break the poverty cycle and enhance equity.  
	- Reduced poverty rates (from 11.2% to 10.8%) and rising life expectancy (77.2 to 77.6 years) suggest progress, but the Gini coefficient remains at 0.38, indicating persistent inequality.  
	- If inequality worsens, social tensions may arise, undermining long-term development despite respectable growth figures.

- Macroeconomic Constraints  
	- The fixed exchange rate regime stabilizes prices and aids foreign investment decisions, but Bahrain cannot use independent monetary policy to address domestic imbalances because of exchange rate commitments.  
	- Public debt, although decreasing (from 130% of GDP in 2021 to 123% in 2022), remains high, constraining the government’s ability to sustain large-scale spending on social programs.

**Evaluation / Conclusion:**  

- Bahrain’s poverty-reduction measures (targeted transfers, National Social Support Program) and diversification efforts have contributed to positive, albeit gradual, outcomes: a lower budget deficit, a modest decrement in poverty rates, and slight growth in non-oil exports.  
- These moves can bolster both economic growth (through improved productivity and investment inflows) and development (through improved education, health care access, and reduced poverty) by strengthening institutional capacity and social infrastructure.  
- However, the heavy reliance on oil revenues risks undermining development strategies if oil prices remain volatile due to unpredictable fiscal inflows. This introduces sustainability concerns for social programs and infrastructure projects.  
- Maintaining macroeconomic stability, raising the skillset of the domestic workforce, and ensuring continued progress in poverty reduction and inequality alleviation are crucial.  
- With balanced reforms, enhanced oversight, and ongoing diversification, Bahrain is more likely to achieve sustained growth and broader development benefits by reducing economic vulnerability and boosting resilience, though persistent concerns about inequality and fiscal dependence on oil remain key challenges.

questions.specification:
Ghana, located in West Africa, has a population of approximately 31 million people. The economy relies heavily on its primary export commodities: cocoa and gold. In 2022, Ghana’s real Gross Domestic Product (GDP) reached GH₵490 billion, compared to GH₵460 billion in 2021, and GH₵430 billion in 2020. Inflation has remained elevated, driven partly by global commodity price increases. Government spending on infrastructure has increased substantially in the last two years. Economists estimate that a recent fiscal stimulus of GH₵600 million, aimed at boosting aggregate demand, could lead to a larger overall impact on national income.  

Ghana’s cocoa industry is one of the largest in the world, with demand influenced by global chocolate consumption. The government also imposes taxes on personal and corporate income, although the tax system struggles with compliance and enforcement. The government is also exploring new policies to reduce income inequality, which remains a challenge despite moderate economic growth.  

Table 1: Ghana’s Macro Data (2020–2022)

| Year | Real GDP (GH₵ billions) | Inflation Rate (%) | Government Spending (GH₵ billions) |  
|-----:|------------------------:|--------------------:|------------------------------------:|  
| 2020 | 430                    | 9.8                | 60                                  |  
| 2021 | 460                    | 10.3               | 65                                  |  
| 2022 | 490                    | 13.1               | 72                                  |  

Table 2: Cocoa Market Changes in Ghana (2022)

| Price per kg (GH₵) | Quantity Demanded (tonnes) | Quantity Supplied (tonnes) |  
|--------------------|---------------------------:|---------------------------:|  
| 13                 | 700,000                   | 550,000                    |  
| 15                 | 680,000                   | 600,000                    |  

Table 3: Ghana’s Tax Brackets (Personal Income)

| Annual Income Range (GH₵) | Tax Rate (%) |  
|---------------------------:|-------------:|  
| 0 – 18,000                | 0            |  
| 18,001 – 36,000           | 10           |  
| 36,001 – 60,000           | 17.5         |  
| Above 60,000              | 25           |  

According to the latest official data, Ghana’s Gini coefficient stands at 0.43, indicating considerable income disparity. While the government aims to reduce inequality, it also seeks to maintain sufficient tax revenue to finance public goods and services.

parts.content: Using the information from Table 1, calculate the rate of real GDP growth in 2022.
parts.markscheme:
• Subtract the 2021 real GDP (GH₵460 billion) from the 2022 real GDP (GH₵490 billion) to find the increase: 490 – 460 = 30 (billion).  
• Divide by the 2021 value and multiply by 100: (30 ÷ 460) × 100 ≈ 6.52%.  
• Final answer: approximately 6.52%.

parts.content: Economists suggest that the GH₵600 million stimulus will increase national income by GH₵2,000 million. Using this information, calculate the Keynesian multiplier for Ghana.
parts.markscheme:
• Use the formula Multiplier = Change in national income ÷ Initial injection.  
• Substitute the values: 2,000 ÷ 600 ≈ 3.33.  
• Final answer: 3.33 (approximately).

parts.content: Using the information from Table 2, calculate the price elasticity of demand for cocoa as the price rises from GH₵13 per kg to GH₵15 per kg.
parts.markscheme:
• Calculate percentage change in quantity (using the midpoint method):  
  %ΔQ = [(680,000 – 700,000) ÷ ((700,000 + 680,000) ÷ 2)] × 100 ≈ –2.90%.  
• Calculate percentage change in price (midpoint method):  
  %ΔP = [(15 – 13) ÷ ((13 + 15) ÷ 2)] × 100 ≈ 14.29%.  
• PED = (–2.90%) ÷ (14.29%) ≈ –0.20 (absolute value 0.20).

parts.content: Using the information from Table 2, calculate the change in total revenue for cocoa producers resulting from the change in price from GH₵13 per kg to GH₵15 per kg.
parts.markscheme:
• Initial total revenue: 13 × 700,000 = GH₵9,100,000.  
• New total revenue: 15 × 680,000 = GH₵10,200,000.  
• Change in revenue: GH₵10,200,000 – GH₵9,100,000 = GH₵1,100,000.

parts.content: Define the term “progressive tax.”
parts.markscheme: • A progressive tax is one where the tax rate increases as income rises, causing higher-income earners to pay a larger proportion of their income in tax.

parts.content: Using an AD/AS diagram, explain how an increase in government spending might affect Ghana’s price level and real output
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/2c0af740-21c0-4237-a2c8-76ccc4b3bfcf)

• An increase in government spending shifts the aggregate demand curve to the right.  
• This leads to an increase in real output from Y1 to Y2 and an increase in the price level from P1 to P2.  
• The short-run impact is a rise in both output and the general price level due to higher demand.  
• The magnitude of the increase depends on the responsiveness of aggregate supply.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks>

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using information from Table 1, calculate the percentage increase in government spending from 2020 to 2022. Show your working.
parts.markscheme:
- Change in government spending: 72 - 60 = 12 billion GH₵
- Percentage increase: (12 ÷ 60) × 100 = 20%
- Percentage increase in government spending: 20%

parts.content: Using the information from Table 3, explain how Ghana’s tax structure might influence income inequality.
parts.markscheme:
• As income rises above GH₵18,000, the tax rate increases in steps to 10%, 17.5%, and 25%.  
• Higher earners pay a greater proportion of their income in taxes, which narrows the income gap between higher- and lower-income groups.  
• The zero rate for the lowest earning bracket helps protect the poorest households’ disposable income.  
• This progressive approach can reduce the Gini coefficient over time, though income inequality at 0.43 remains a concern.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and your knowledge of economics, recommend a policy that the government of Ghana could implement to promote economic development.
parts.markscheme:
• Economic development can be defined as sustained improvements in living standards, education, healthcare, and access to basic services.  
• A policy to enhance educational infrastructure could be funded by higher government spending, complemented by efforts to improve tax compliance using the tax brackets in Table 3.  
• This policy could help reduce inequality (Gini coefficient of 0.43) and maintain long-term growth by increasing human capital.  
• Improved education raises productivity, potentially increasing Ghana’s real GDP beyond GH₵490 billion through a positive supply-side effect.  
• Higher skills in the workforce might reduce poverty and lift living standards, aligning with development goals.  
• Referring to the case study’s fiscal stimulus of GH₵600 million that boosted aggregate demand, allocating funds specifically to education could have a larger multiplier effect. 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/cba3fff9-d354-45e0-9189-5c9dc51c49a5)
• Careful implementation is required to avoid excessive inflation, which is already at 13.1%, by balancing the increase in aggregate demand with sufficient increases in aggregate supply.  
• The policy could involve subsidies for teacher training, school construction, and learning materials, especially in rural areas.  
• This may create a more equitable distribution of income over time, supporting the government’s aim of reducing inequality while maintaining tax revenue.  
• Sustained improvements in education could lead to broader development achievements, supported by the moderate yet growing GDP figures in the data.

questions.specification:
## Economic Developments and Challenges in Kuwait

Kuwait, located in the Arabian Gulf, has historically relied on oil as its primary source of government revenue and export earnings. In 2022, oil accounted for nearly 85% of Kuwait’s export revenues. While this has supported high per capita income, policymakers now emphasize economic diversification to reduce dependency on oil and increase private sector participation. Recently, Kuwait has invested in infrastructure, financial services, and technology hubs to encourage foreign direct investment (FDI) and create high-skilled jobs. In 2021, the government spent an estimated KD 1.5 billion on new tech hubs and start-up incubators.

Alongside diversification, Kuwait experiences moderate macroeconomic fluctuations influenced by global oil prices. In 2020, the economy contracted sharply when global demand for oil fell, contributing to elevated unemployment (rising to 6% in that year) and reduced consumer spending. However, Kuwait’s prudent fiscal management supported by substantial sovereign wealth funds helped cushion the economy from more severe shocks. In 2022, inflation remained relatively low at 3.2%, partly due to government subsidies on water and electricity, which keep costs manageable for households and businesses.

Despite its high average income levels, Kuwait faces development challenges. Although official poverty rates remain lower than in many other regions, some studies estimate that about 2% of the population (particularly among migrant workers) live below the national poverty line. This group often lacks access to social protection and faces limited upward mobility. The government has launched new training programs to increase their basic skills, hoping to interrupt what officials refer to as a potential “poverty trap” among vulnerable groups. Other measures include direct transfer payments to low-income households and the expansion of subsidized health-care services.

Kuwait also aims to enhance its participation in international trade beyond oil exports. Since 2019, it has reduced tariffs on specific imports from trading partners within the Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC), and it is exploring free trade agreements that could boost non-oil exports, such as petrochemicals and financial services. Foreign investors, attracted by stable macroeconomic fundamentals and strategic location, have collectively funneled growing inflows into Kuwait. In 2022, FDI inflows reached KD 650 million, particularly in logistics and tech start-ups.

On the social front, inequality appears moderate, but many analysts believe the official Gini statistic may not fully capture disparities, especially between citizens and non-citizens. The government is also exploring ways to shift from broad subsidies to more targeted support. With oil revenues fluctuating, policymakers see diversification not only as a means of economic sustainability but also to sustain social and development goals such as reducing the risk of long-term unemployment.

The future trajectory of Kuwait’s economy largely depends on oil market dynamics, the success of diversification projects, and continued investments in human capital. As the country expands digital infrastructure, fosters new industries, and strengthens local entrepreneurship, it hopes to reduce its vulnerability to external shocks and create more inclusive opportunities for residents.

**Table 1: Kuwait’s Selected Macroeconomic Indicators**  
| Indicator                         | 2019 | 2020 | 2021 | 2022 |
| --------------------------------- | ---- | ---- | ---- | ---- |
| Nominal GDP (KD billion)          | 36.2 | 32.1 | 34.0 | 36.8 |
| Real GDP Growth Rate (%)          | 2.0  | -9.2 | 1.0  | 2.6  |
| Unemployment Rate (%)             | 3.5  | 6.0  | 5.2  | 4.7  |
| Inflation Rate (%)                | 1.5  | 2.8  | 2.4  | 3.2  |
| Government Subsidies (KD billion) | 3.0  | 3.5  | 3.3  | 3.4  |




**Table 2: Development and Social Indicators for Kuwait**  

| Indicator                                                      | 2019 | 2020 | 2021 | 2022 |
| -------------------------------------------------------------- | ---- | ---- | ---- | ---- |
| Population (million)                                           | 4.6  | 4.7  | 4.8  | 4.9  |
| Estimated Poverty Rate (% of total population)                 | 1.8  | 2.0  | 2.1  | 2.0  |
| Gini Coefficient (estimated)                                   | 0.31 | 0.32 | 0.31 | 0.31 |
| FDI Inflows (KD million)                                       | 540  | 300  | 450  | 650  |
| Government Expenditure on Training Programs (KD million)       | 100  | 120  | 140  | 160  |
| Share of Non-Oil Exports in Total Exports (%)                  | 10.5 | 10.0 | 11.2 | 12.0 |
| Public Spending on Healthcare and Social Services (KD billion) | 1.6  | 1.7  | 1.8  | 1.9  |

parts.content: Define the term “sovereign wealth fund” mentioned in the text (paragraph 2).
parts.markscheme: Sovereign wealth fund is a state-owned investment fund or entity that manages national savings for investment purposes, often funded by revenues from resources such as oil.

parts.content: Define the term “transfer payments” mentioned in the text (paragraph 3).
parts.markscheme: Transfer payments are redistributions of income by the government to individuals or households without any exchange of goods or services.

parts.content: Using information from Table 1, calculate the change (in KD billion) in Kuwait’s nominal GDP from 2019 to 2022.
parts.markscheme:
Calculation of change in nominal GDP from 2019 to 2022:  
-  2022 nominal GDP = KD 36.8 billion  
- 2019 nominal GDP = KD 36.2 billion  
- Change = 36.8 − 36.2 = KD 0.6 billion

parts.content: Sketch an aggregate demand and aggregate supply (AD/AS) diagram to show how increases in oil revenue, as reflected in nominal GDP data in Table 1, might affect the inflation rate.
parts.markscheme: - An AD/AS diagram would show an outward shift of the AD curve (due to rising oil revenues increasing consumption, investment, or government spending), potentially causing a higher price level (inflation) if aggregate supply remains unchanged.

parts.content: Using a demand and supply diagram for the Kuwaiti dinar, explain how rising foreign direct investment (FDI) inflows (paragraph 5) might affect the exchange rate of Kuwait’s currency.
parts.markscheme:
- Rising foreign direct investment inflows into Kuwait increase the demand for the Kuwaiti dinar as foreign investors need dinars to invest in local projects and assets. 
- This upward demand pressure causes the exchange rate of the dinar to appreciate if supply remains the same. 
- The exchange rate diagram would show a rightward shift of the demand curve for the dinar, resulting in an appreciation from its equilibrium level.

parts.content: Using a tariff diagram, explain how reducing tariffs on select imports (paragraph 5) could affect domestic firms that produce similar goods.
parts.markscheme:
- Reducing tariffs on select imports lowers the price of these imported goods, shifting the world supply curve downwards in a tariff diagram.
- Domestic firms facing competition from cheaper imports may have their market share reduced, as consumers can purchase the now more affordable imported goods.
-  This may lead to reduced revenue and possible cost-cutting or efficiency measures among domestic producers.

parts.content: Using a business cycle diagram, explain how fluctuating oil prices could lead to changes in unemployment in Kuwait (paragraph 2).
parts.markscheme:
- Fluctuating oil prices affect Kuwait’s real output and employment levels through changes in export earnings and fiscal revenue. When oil prices fall, aggregate demand may contract, leading to slower output growth and rising unemployment. 
- During periods of higher oil prices, overall economic activity increases, boosting employment. 
- The business cycle diagram would show periods of expansion when oil prices are high and recessions when oil prices are low.

parts.content: Using a poverty cycle diagram, explain how limited skill development among low-income or vulnerable groups can perpetuate poverty (paragraph 3).
parts.markscheme:
- Limited skill development hinders employability and earning potential for low-income groups, reducing their ability to escape poverty. 
- A poverty cycle diagram highlights how inadequate training prevents individuals from accessing higher-paying jobs, resulting in persistently low incomes that limit investment in education or skill upgrades, thus perpetuating poverty across generations.

parts.content: Using information from the text/data and your knowledge of economics, evaluate the effectiveness of Kuwait’s diversification strategy in promoting economic growth and development.
parts.markscheme:
**Definition:**

  - Economic diversification refers to a policy of broadening the range of output produced in an economy, away from heavy reliance on a single sector like oil.  
  - Economic development includes improvements in standards of living, reduction in poverty, increasing access to education, and broader human capital formation.

**Range of Arguments:**

  - Kuwait’s heavy reliance on oil is evident (85% of export revenues). Diversification efforts (paragraph 1) are attempts to reduce vulnerability to external shocks, such as volatile global oil prices.  
  - Investment in infrastructure, financial services, and technology hubs (KD 1.5 billion in 2021) supports the development of new sectors, potentially creating more high-skilled employment. This may spur long-run economic growth and reduce overdependence on oil.  
  - Macroeconomic stability, assisted by prudent fiscal management and sovereign wealth funds, has mitigated severe downturns, but deeper diversification could achieve more sustainable growth by lowering the risk from oil-price volatility.  
  - Data from Table 2 indicates FDI has risen significantly (from KD 300 million in 2020 to KD 650 million in 2022). Growing FDI can bring technology transfer, managerial expertise, and job creation, positively impacting growth.  
  - The government’s training programs and targeted social strategies can address structural unemployment and reduce poverty by enhancing skill sets of low-income groups, potentially breaking the “poverty trap.” This suggests better inclusivity and human development outcomes.  
  - However, diversification depends on maintaining competitive policies to attract non-oil investors, continued improvements in the business environment, and the pace of private sector development.  
  - Global oil price fluctuations may still cause economic instability, so long as oil remains a large share of export revenue. Reforms and targeted investments must be consistent over time to ensure resilience.  
  - Factors such as unequal distribution of wealth between citizens and non-citizens could limit effectiveness if diversification gains are not broadly shared. The Gini coefficient (around 0.31−0.32) may not fully capture disparities.  
  - Diversification may lead to higher productivity, greater export potential (petrochemicals and financial services), and improved economic growth, provided government continues policy reforms, strengthens innovation, and promotes private sector participation.

**Conclusion:**

  - Kuwait’s diversification efforts show promise by attracting FDI, investing in new industries, and promoting skill development. These measures can reduce reliance on oil and enhance growth and development outcomes.  
  - Risks include persistent oil price volatility, challenges in effectively implementing reforms, and potential inequalities between different population groups. Despite these obstacles, Kuwait’s policies aimed at creating a more varied economic base and boosting human capital represent a potentially effective pathway toward long-term economic stability and development.

questions.specification:
## Australia’s Evolving Economy and Policy Directions

Over the last decade, Australia has posted moderate but steady growth, with real GDP expanding at an average annual rate of 2.6% between 2019 and 2022. This was largely driven by robust demand for natural resources, particularly iron ore and coal, which continue to dominate export earnings. However, a gradual global shift toward renewable energy has prompted Australia to diversify its export base. Increasingly, the services sector—especially tourism and education—has become a key contributor to national income, rebounding strongly in late 2022 after pandemic-related disruptions. Meanwhile, inflation rose from 1.3% in 2019 to 3.5% in 2022, spurred partly by supply chain constraints and a strong recovery in consumer spending.

The unemployment rate dropped from 5.3% in 2019 to 4.7% in 2022, reflecting both post-pandemic re-hiring and structural shifts in the labor market. Yet certain regions and industries still face challenges due to skill mismatches and the phasing out of some high-polluting processes. To address rising living costs, the federal government introduced an updated minimum wage policy in 2021. This policy, intended to ensure all workers can afford basic necessities, is debated among economists: while advocates emphasize it can reduce income inequality, critics argue it may impose higher costs on small businesses.

Australia has traditionally relied on an expansionary monetary policy to stimulate aggregate demand during downturns. From 2020 to 2022, the Reserve Bank of Australia (RBA) maintained historically low policy interest rates in response to pandemic-related disruptions. Simultaneously, government stimulus packages offered support to households and firms. This blend of monetary and fiscal interventions aimed to sustain consumer spending and reduce the risk of entrenched unemployment.

In terms of international trade, Australia relies heavily on exports of primary commodities, although recent trade agreements have encouraged the development of advanced manufacturing and technology services. Australia’s geographical proximity to Asia provides markets for tourism, international education, and agriculture.

To tackle environmental concerns and diversify the economy further, the government introduced a carbon credit scheme in 2022. Under this program, firms meeting or exceeding emissions targets can receive tradable permits or carbon credits, which can be sold to more polluting firms. Critics note that if carbon credits become too inexpensive, they may not incentivize lasting changes in production techniques. However, supporters argue that this approach internalizes negative externalities, helping Australia balance economic growth with environmental stewardship.

Looking ahead, the economy faces both challenges and opportunities. Two of the most pressing issues are sustaining a competitive exchange rate and ensuring workforce adaptability. A rising Australian dollar, driven by higher commodity prices, can hurt sectors like tourism and education by making them more expensive for foreign consumers. In addition, skill shortages persist in industries such as renewable energy and high-tech manufacturing. Policymakers are exploring ways to boost productivity—investments in education, infrastructure, and research—to ensure the economy can remain resilient and internationally competitive.

**Table 1: Australia’s Key Macroeconomic Indicators (2019–2022)**

| Indicator                   | 2019  | 2020  | 2021  | 2022  |
|-----------------------------|-------|-------|-------|-------|
| Real GDP Growth Rate (%)    | 2.2   | -0.3  | 2.8   | 3.0   |
| Inflation Rate (%)          | 1.3   | 0.9   | 2.2   | 3.5   |
| Unemployment Rate (%)       | 5.3   | 6.8   | 5.6   | 4.7   |
| Policy Interest Rate (%)    | 1.00  | 0.50  | 0.25  | 0.10  |
| AUD/USD Exchange Rate (avg) | 0.70  | 0.69  | 0.75  | 0.73  |

**Table 2: Sectoral Composition of Australia’s Exports, 2022**

| Sector                      | Percentage of Total Exports |
|-----------------------------|-----------------------------|
| Iron Ore and Coal           | 35%                         |
| Education Services          | 15%                         |
| Tourism and Hospitality     | 10%                         |
| Agriculture and Food        | 10%                         |
| Advanced Manufacturing      | 10%                         |
| Financial/Business Services | 8%                          |
| Other Goods and Services    | 12%                         |

parts.content: Using an exchange rate diagram, explain how an increase in global commodity prices could lead to an appreciation of the Australian dollar (paragraph 6).

- Rising global commodity prices increase foreign demand for Australia’s major exports (iron ore, coal).  
- This raises the demand for Australian dollars, shifting the demand curve for AUD to the right, causing the exchange rate to appreciate from E1 to E2.  
- An appreciated currency makes Australia’s exports relatively more expensive, which may later reduce demand for exports like tourism and education services.  
- The extent of the appreciation depends on the size of Australia’s commodity export sector.

parts.content: Using a production possibilities curve (PPC) diagram, explain how Australia’s ongoing shift toward more diversified production (paragraph 4) may influence its long-run productive capacity.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/494f2bec-00db-4171-b30b-15cb0385b3f3)

- Shifting production from mainly primary commodities to higher-value services and technology can enhance skills, productivity, and capital formation through innovation and diversification.
- Over time, the production possibilities curve shifts outward as resources are reallocated to more efficient and innovative sectors.  
- This results in increased potential output and higher overall economic capacity.  
- The economy becomes less vulnerable to volatility in global commodity markets, supporting a more stable growth path.

parts.content: Using a business cycle diagram, explain how Australia’s moderate but steady real GDP growth from 2019 to 2022 (Table 1) could affect cyclical unemployment.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/994e3f2d-97f4-4b69-8678-77277c6d5079)

 - Moderate real GDP growth suggests the economy moves toward or remains in an expansionary phase of the business cycle.  
- Cyclical unemployment typically falls because firms employ more labor in response to growing demand for goods and services.  
- Data in Table 1 shows unemployment fell from 6.8% in 2020 to 4.7% in 2022, consistent with a recovery and expansion.  
- Some structural unemployment may persist due to skill mismatches, but cyclical unemployment is likely reduced as economic activity rebounds.

parts.content: Evaluate the impact of Australia’s carbon credit scheme on economic growth and international competitiveness.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include: 

**Definition:**

- **Carbon credit scheme**: A market-based mechanism where firms that pollute less are rewarded with permits/credits they can sell to firms that pollute more.  
- **Economic growth**: Sustained increases in real output or GDP over time.  
- **International competitiveness**: The ability of domestic firms to compete internationally in terms of price, quality, and innovation.

**Range of Arguments:**

1. Reduction in Negative Externalities  
- The tradable permit mechanism can help internalize the external costs of pollution.
- This creates negative externality of production, and a welfare loss, causing marker failure  
- Firms have an incentive to invest in cleaner technologies to sell excess credits or minimize the need to buy more.  
- This can provide a healthier environment, aligning with long-term sustainable growth objectives.
- As government imposes policies to decrease supply, such as imposition of tax, the Supply curve - MPC, will decrease and coincide with MSC, eventually leading to market equilibrium at Qopt. 

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/aa7d80e4-7dd7-4138-87ea-dc73d1242c03) 

2. Short-Term Cost Increases and Competitiveness  
- Firms purchasing credits face higher costs, potentially raising the prices of some goods  due to increased compliance expenses.  
- Higher costs may reduce Australia’s price competitiveness, particularly for emission-heavy industries reliant on coal and iron ore.  
- In the short run, this could slow growth in those sectors and reduce export revenues because of rising production costs.

3. Stimulus for Innovation and Diversification  
- Encouraging lower emissions can spur development of renewable energy and advanced manufacturing.  
- Table 2 highlights a growing share of diversified exports (such as advanced manufacturing at 10% and services like education at 15%).  
- In the medium to long run, this diversification can lead to new revenue streams and more sustainable growth.

4. Attracting Environmentally Conscious Investors and Consumers  
- A carbon credit scheme may enhance Australia’s reputation for environmental responsibility.  
- This can attract foreign direct investment (FDI) and appeal to consumers in countries with strong environmental preferences.  
- Over time, this can offset any near-term investment losses in more polluting industries through gains in cleaner technologies.

5. Government Revenue and Possible Redistribution  
- If the government auctions some permits, revenue can be used for investments in research, infrastructure, or skill development, reinforcing long-term growth.  
- Funds could also help workers transition from polluting industries to more sustainable sectors, improving structural employment outcomes.

**Evaluation:**

- Australia’s export base in 2022 is still dominated by iron ore and coal (35%); higher carbon costs may reduce competitiveness for those exports in the short run.  
- However, services (25% total for education and tourism) may face less direct impact from carbon credits, and advanced manufacturing (10%) might evolve with new, cleaner technologies.  
- Over time, these shifts could raise productivity and sustain economic growth through innovation. 
- The inflation rate has trended upward to 3.5%, suggesting that additional cost pressures from carbon credits must be carefully managed to prevent further inflationary pressure driven by supply chain impacts.
- Expanded skill development in renewables and high-tech manufacturing, noted in paragraph 6, can support a transition to less carbon-intensive industries, fostering long-term international competitiveness.

**Conclusion:**

- A carbon credit scheme can slow short-term growth for heavily polluting industries by increasing their costs, due to stricter emissions limits, but can generate long-term benefits by fostering investment in cleaner technologies.  
- It may strengthen Australia’s position among environmentally conscious trading partners, enhancing future competitiveness.  
- Effectiveness depends on maintaining sufficiently high credit prices to encourage meaningful emissions reductions and on complementary policies (e.g., skill upgrading, infrastructure investments).

questions.specification:
Finland is a Nordic country with a population of approximately 5.5 million. It has a highly developed economy supported by a robust welfare system and a strong emphasis on research and development (R&D). Finland’s export-oriented industries focus on forest products, technology, and machinery. The public sector is comparatively large, accounting for a significant percentage of total employment and national output. Finland’s tax system is progressive, characterized by both high personal income tax rates and a 20% corporate tax rate.

Table 1 below shows selected macroeconomic indicators for Finland from 2019 to 2022. The Finnish government’s budget balance improved somewhat in 2022, but the country faced relatively higher inflation compared to the pre-2020 period. In 2021, the government expanded its support for research and development, aiming to boost innovation and long-term economic growth.

Table 2 provides data on Finland’s paper exports to the European Union (EU), a significant market for the country’s forest products. Analysts are interested in the price elasticity of demand for Finnish paper to assess how changes in global demand might affect export revenue.

Table 3 outlines a measure of income distribution in Finland, illustrating the nation’s relatively low income inequality. Finland has a Gini coefficient of 0.27, making it one of the more equal countries in the EU. Still, there are ongoing debates about how income inequality and social welfare expenditures affect long-term growth.

Below are three tables with the relevant data:

Table 1: Macroeconomic Indicators for Finland, 2019–2022

| Year | Real GDP Growth (%) | Unemployment Rate (%) | Inflation Rate (%) | Government Surplus/Deficit (% of GDP) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 2019 | 1.1 | 6.7 | 1.0 | -0.9 |
| 2020 | -2.9 | 7.8 | 0.3 | -4.0 |
| 2021 | 3.0 | 7.1 | 2.0 | -2.7 |
| 2022 | 2.4 | 6.7 | 5.0 | -2.0 |

Table 2: Price and Quantity Demanded for Finnish Paper Exports in the EU

| Price (€/tonne) | Quantity Demanded (thousand tonnes) |
| --- | --- |
| 990 | 620 |
| 1,090 | 570 |

Table 3: Income Distribution in Finland, Selected Indicators (2021)

| Indicator                                     | Value   |
|----------------------------------------------|---------|
| Gini coefficient                             | 0.27    |
| Share of income held by top 10%              | 23.5%   |
| Share of income held by bottom 10%           | 2.9%    |
| Estimated government spending multiplier     | 1.5     |

In 2021, Finland introduced an additional progressive tax bracket aimed at higher-income earners. The policy objective was to generate added tax revenue to fund social programs and R&D, while maintaining relatively low levels of income inequality.

parts.content: Using the information in Table 1, calculate the average annual real GDP growth for Finland over the period 2019–2022.

In 2021, a Finnish worker earning €45 000 is subject to a tax rate of 20% on the first €40 000 of income, and 25% on any income above €40 000. Calculate the total personal income tax paid by this worker.
Using the information in Table 2, calculate the price elasticity of demand for Finnish paper exports when the price increases from €990 to €1 090 per tonne.

Suppose the Finnish government increases its expenditure by €500 million in 2022. Using the estimated spending multiplier in Table 3, calculate the total increase in real GDP that might result from this fiscal stimulus.
parts.markscheme:
• Multiplier = 1.5.  
• Total increase = 1.5 × €500 million = €750 million.

parts.content: Define the term “progressive tax.”
parts.markscheme: • A progressive tax is a tax where the fraction of income paid as tax rises as income increases.

parts.content: Using an aggregate demand (AD)/aggregate supply (AS) diagram, explain how an increase in government spending on research and development might affect Finland’s long-run aggregate supply (LRAS).
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/a28e5f9c-1f7b-4c8c-a1d6-260f62976366)
• Additional R&D spending can improve technology and productivity, shifting LRAS to the right over time.  
• This shift reflects an increase in the economy’s productive capacity and potential output.  
• On the AD/AS diagram, the new LRAS curve is drawn to the right of the original LRAS curve, illustrating a higher full employment level of output.  
• In the longer term, the economy can achieve a higher real GDP level without upward pressure on the price level caused by capacity constraints.

parts.content: Using information from Table 3, calculate the ratio of income share held by the top 10% compared to the bottom 10%. Show your working.
parts.markscheme:
- Income share of top 10%: 23.5%
- Income share of bottom 10%: 2.9%
- Ratio = 23.5 ÷ 2.9 = 8.1

parts.content: Using Table 3 and the information in the text, explain how Finland’s income inequality level could impact its long-term economic growth.
parts.markscheme:
• A Gini coefficient of 0.27 indicates relatively low inequality, which can lead to greater social cohesion and higher labor productivity.  
• Social welfare programs and progressive taxes may ensure more equitable access to education and health, raising human capital.  
• These factors can strengthen long-term growth by enhancing the skills base, consumption levels, and overall stability.  
• Excessive equality measures might reduce incentives for entrepreneurship, but Finland’s approach tries to balance equity with economic incentives, supporting sustained growth.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and your knowledge of economics, recommend a policy which could be implemented by the Finnish government to strengthen economic growth while maintaining low levels of income inequality.
parts.markscheme:
• Define “economic growth” as a sustained increase in real output over time and define “income inequality” as the unequal distribution of income within a population.  
• Emphasize that Finland’s current low Gini of 0.27 shows relatively high equality, supported by progressive taxation and welfare systems.  
• Propose targeted public investment in human capital, such as increased spending on advanced technical education and vocational training, drawing on Finland’s existing emphasis on research and development.  
• Argue that enhancing specialized education aligns with Finland’s technology-oriented and export-focused industries, boosting labor productivity and innovation in forest products, technology, and machinery.  
• Highlight that such spending can shift the long-run aggregate supply to the right, indicating higher productive capacity; Table 3 suggests a positive multiplier effect, increasing real GDP by more than the initial injection.  
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ddf05e40-a20d-46c5-9725-736f7711f014)
• Note that a progressive tax system can fund this investment while keeping income distribution relatively balanced, as higher-income earners contribute a larger share to government revenue.  
• Point out that this maintains equity because it reduces the burden on lower-income groups, preventing a rise in inequality and preserving consumer demand.  
• Acknowledge the risk that higher taxes might disincentivize some investors or highly skilled workers, but Finland’s moderate corporate tax rate of 20% could mitigate this concern.  
• Indicate that linking part of the additional tax revenue to social programs and R&D helps sustain low inequality and encourages research-based growth, consistent with the policy objective mentioned in 2021.  
• Conclude that this approach likely fosters long-term growth and innovation without undermining Finland’s strong track record of income equality.

questions.specification:
Botswana is a landlocked country in Southern Africa. Its economy has historically relied on diamond mining for export revenue, contributing significantly to GDP and government revenue. However, there is growing concern over high unemployment, income inequality, and the need to diversify into other sectors such as tourism and financial services. A recent government infrastructure project, valued at US$200 million, aims to create jobs and stimulate growth. Botswana’s marginal propensity to consume (MPC) has been estimated at 0.8. Table 1 and Table 2 provide selected data on Botswana’s economy.

Table 1: Selected Macroeconomic Indicators for Botswana

| Year | Real GDP (US$ billions) | Unemployment rate (%) | Gini coefficient |  
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 2019 | 17.2 | 19.5 | 0.53 |
| 2020 | 15.6 | 22.2 | 0.57 |
| 2021 | 18.0 | 20.0 | 0.55 |

Table 2: Price and Quantity Demanded of Diamond Exports

| Price per carat (Pula) | Quantity demanded (million carats) |
| --- | --- |
| 1000 | 10 |
| 1100 | 9  |

parts.content: Using information from Table 1, calculate the rate of real GDP growth from 2019 to 2020.

parts.content: Assuming the marginal propensity to consume (MPC) in Botswana is 0.8, calculate the total change in real GDP resulting from the government’s US$200 million infrastructure project. Show working.
parts.markscheme:
• Multiplier = 1 ÷ (1 − 0.8) = 5  
• Total change in real GDP = 5 × 200 million = US$1 000 million

Using the information in Table 2, calculate the change in total revenue from diamond exports as the price increases from 1000 Pula per carat to 1100 Pula per carat.
parts.markscheme:
• Original total revenue = 1000 × 10 = 10 000 million Pula  
• New total revenue = 1100 × 9 = 9 900 million Pula  
• Change in total revenue = 9 900 − 10 000 = −100 million Pula

parts.markscheme: • The marginal propensity to consume (MPC) is the fraction of additional income that is spent on consumption.

• In an AD/AS diagram, this is shown by a leftward shift of the AD curve.  
• Real output (GDP) and the price level both tend to fall in the short run compared to the original equilibrium.  
• The economy may experience lower growth and higher unemployment if the lower export revenue persists.

parts.content: Using information from Table 1, explain how high income inequality may act as a barrier to economic development in Botswana.
parts.markscheme:
• Botswana’s Gini coefficient remains high (0.53 in 2019, rising in 2020, then 0.55 in 2021), indicating significant income disparities.  
• High inequality decreases access to education, healthcare, and financial services for lower-income groups.  
• This can prevent many individuals from improving skills or investing in businesses, limiting overall development potential.  
• Large segments of the population may remain in poverty, reducing domestic demand for goods and services and slowing long-term growth.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and knowledge of economics, recommend a policy which the government of Botswana could implement to reduce dependence on diamond export revenue.
parts.markscheme:
• Diversification reduces reliance on a single product and can help stabilize income and employment; a policy focusing on developing tourism and financial services may achieve this.  
• Defining “diversification” indicates shifting resources across multiple industries rather than depending on diamond mining alone.  
• Highlighting “economic development” relates to improving the welfare of citizens through increased incomes, quality of life, and lowering unemployment.  
• Implementing supply-side policies, such as investment in education and training for tourism and financial services, increases the country’s human capital and attracts investment.  
• Improvements to infrastructure support new industries and encourage private sector growth by reducing costs of production, especially for tourism (building roads and airports) and financial services (technological infrastructure).  
• Diagrammatic representation could show a long-run aggregate supply shift rightward as productivity rises, helping to sustain growth beyond diamond mining. 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/813dd8a4-ccb6-40c5-8e5a-a26271ae447a)
• Adopting tax incentives for companies entering high-potential sectors encourages entrepreneurship and foreign direct investment outside mining, generating more diverse export revenues.  
• Incorporating information from the text on high unemployment and income inequality identifies that focusing on labor-intensive sectors (e.g., eco-tourism) can create broad-based employment.  
• Ensuring that new industries provide opportunities for both skilled and unskilled workers facilitates more inclusive income distribution and counters high Gini coefficients.  
• Weighing opportunity costs is important, as government resources directed toward new industries imply less funding for other projects, but long-term gains in growth and employment can offset initial expenses.

questions.specification:
## Economic Challenges and Opportunities in Vanuatu

(1) Vanuatu, an archipelago nation in the South Pacific with a population of roughly 320,000, has experienced a mix of economic successes and setbacks over the last few years. Agriculture and tourism are its primary economic drivers, with the export of kava, cocoa, and fish generating important foreign exchange earnings. Tourism, focused on eco-friendly resorts and cultural attractions, has grown steadily in recent years, despite occasional disruptions caused by cyclones and global economic shocks.

(2) Real GDP growth was estimated at 2.5% in 2019 but contracted briefly in 2020 due to border closures, subsequently rebounding to 3.1% in 2021 and 3.8% in 2022. Inflation has remained moderate, rising from 1.0% in 2019 to 2.8% by 2022, although some policy makers remain concerned about increasing fuel and imported food prices. Cyclone Rani, which struck in early 2021, underscored Vanuatu’s vulnerability to climate-related events and highlighted the need for more resilient infrastructure.

(3) In response to rising living costs, the government introduced a new minimum wage in 2021, increasing it by 20%. This policy aimed to protect lower-income workers employed mainly in tourism, retail, and agriculture. Critics argue that the higher minimum wage may increase unemployment if small businesses cannot absorb higher labor costs, whereas supporters believe it may boost domestic consumption and labor productivity.

(4) Foreign direct investment (FDI) in tourism-related ventures has grown, and overseas companies have funded new beachfront resorts and hotels. The government is keen to ensure that the influx of foreign capital provides benefits for local communities, partly by imposing regulations on land development to limit negative externalities such as erosion of coastal habitats.

(5) Public debt has gradually climbed over the past decade, partly due to borrowing to finance large-scale infrastructure projects (such as port expansions and improved inter-island ferry networks). These projects are intended to facilitate trade, attract more visitors, and improve distribution channels for agricultural exports. However, concerns persist about Vanuatu’s ability to service its debt if global conditions deteriorate, especially given the government’s existing commitments to social welfare and disaster response funds.

(6) While the unemployment rate was officially at 6.2% in 2019, it reached 7.5% during the pandemic disruptions and remained around 6.9% in 2022. Seasonal unemployment is common, given the cyclical nature of tourism, which peaks from June to September. Government efforts to diversify the economy include encouraging small-scale manufacturing and expanding digital infrastructure, although progress has been uneven across the islands.

(7) Income inequality, reflected in a significant urban-rural divide, remains a challenge. Some rural communities have limited access to healthcare, education, and financial services. Efforts to reduce inequality have focused on improving transport links between outer islands and the main centers, alongside targeted subsidies for basic necessities such as electricity and water. Still, environmental groups worry that tourism expansion could harm marine ecosystems and threaten the sustainability of both fishing and local livelihoods.

(8) International trade plays a major role in Vanuatu’s economy. Exports of kava and fish are crucial, yet they are subject to volatile world prices. The country also faces the high costs of importing capital goods, processed foods, and fuel. With global travel demand recovering, tourism in Vanuatu is poised for further expansion, but managing this growth so that it fosters equitable development and safeguards the fragile environment will remain a central policy challenge.

Table 1: Key Macroeconomic Indicators for Vanuatu (2019–2022)

| Indicator                     | 2019     | 2020     | 2021     | 2022     |
|------------------------------|----------|----------|----------|----------|
| Real GDP Growth Rate (%)     | 2.5      | -1.8     | 3.1      | 3.8      |
| Inflation Rate (%)           | 1.0      | 1.6      | 2.3      | 2.8      |
| Unemployment Rate (%)        | 6.2      | 7.2      | 7.5      | 6.9      |
| Public Debt (% of GDP)       | 45.0     | 48.5     | 50.2     | 51.3     |
| Minimum Wage (VUV/hour)      | 250      | 250      | 300      | 300      |

Table 2: Tourism and Development Indicators for Vanuatu (2019–2022)

| Indicator                           | 2019      | 2020      | 2021      | 2022      |
|-------------------------------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| International Tourists (thousands)  | 325       | 100       | 190       | 280       |
| Average Tourist Spending (US$/day)  | 110       | 85        | 95        | 105       |
| Gini Coefficient                    | 0.45      | 0.44      | 0.43      | 0.42      |
| Rural Population below Poverty Line | 24%       | 24%       | 23%       | 22%       |
| FDI Inflows (US$ million)           | 40        | 25        | 30        | 37        |
